title: A Computability Theoretic Equivalent to Vaught’s Conjecture
overall_summary: The paper proves, assuming projective determinacy, that for an Lω1,ω sentence T with uncountably many countable models, being a counterexample to Vaught’s conjecture is equivalent to a computability-theoretic cone property: relative to some oracle, every hyperarithmetic model of T has a computable copy. It also gives a precise cone-relative description of all degree spectra of models of such a T: they are exactly threshold sets of the form {X : ω1^X ≥ α}. The core tools are Martin’s Turing determinacy, ranked equivalence relations with countable rank fibers, Scott rank and admissible ordinal arguments, and a fine α-back-and-forth structure whose presentations can be manipulated effectively. For the original problem, the most relevant contributions are the on-a-cone method for converting projective/cofinal information into uniform computability, the use of infinitary Σ/Π formulas and back-and-forth types as effective invariants, and the coding argument showing that too many back-and-forth types contradict a hyperarithmetic-is-recursive cone property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume ZFC plus projective determinacy. Let T be an Lω1,ω sentence with uncountably many countable models. The following are equivalent. First, T is a counterexample to Vaught’s conjecture in the paper’s sense: T is scattered, meaning that for each countable ordinal α there are at most countably many Σ^in_α-elementary equivalence classes among the countable models of T, and T has uncountably many countable models. Second, T satisfies hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on a cone: there is an oracle Y such that for every X ≥T Y, every model of T whose atomic diagram is hyperarithmetic in X has an X-computable copy. Third, there is an oracle Y relative to which the family of degree spectra of models of T is exactly the family of threshold spectra {Z ∈ 2^ω : ω1^{Z⊕Y} ≥ α}, as α ranges over countable ordinals; here Sp(A) is the set of reals computing a copy of A and ω1^X is the least ordinal with no X-computable presentation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication from scattered counterexample to the spectral description is proved by Theorem 5.2, whose main input is Theorem 5.1: on a suitable cone, a model A of T is computable from Z exactly when ω1^A is at most ω1^Z, with the correct relativization. The spectral description implies hyperarithmetic-is-recursive because if a model has a copy hyperarithmetic in X, then no new admissible ordinal is created beyond ω1^X, so the threshold condition forces X itself to compute a copy. The implication from hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on a cone to scatteredness is the coding argument in Section 5.1: if there were continuum many α-back-and-forth types at the least such α, a perfect set of Σ1-types in an expanded back-and-forth language would allow one to code a set hyperarithmetic in a sufficiently high oracle Y but not Σ^0_α(Y), producing a model hyperarithmetic in Y with no Y-computable copy. Projective determinacy enters through Martin’s cone theorems, used to obtain the required uniform cone bases.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3, Nadel’s effective Scott isomorphism theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ∈ 2^ω be an oracle. If two X-computable countable structures are elementarily equivalent for the computable infinitary language L^c,X_{ω1,ω}, meaning they satisfy the same computable infinitary sentences whose infinite conjunctions and disjunctions are X-computably enumerable, then the two structures are isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem from Nadel. It is used as the effective replacement for Scott’s isomorphism theorem: when two structures are computable in a common oracle W and agree on all L^c,W_{ω1,ω} sentences, the theorem upgrades computable-infinitary elementary equivalence to actual isomorphism. In later arguments, one arranges ω1^W = α and proves two W-computable structures agree on all computable infinitary formulas below α; since all W-computable infinitary formulas occur below ω1^W, Nadel’s theorem yields isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Background Scott-rank bounds and relativized high Scott rank observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A and tuple ā, ρ_A(ā) is the least ordinal α such that any tuple satisfying all Π^in_α formulas true of ā satisfies all Lω1,ω formulas true of ā. The Scott rank is SR(A) = sup{ρ_A(ā)+1 : ā ∈ A^{<ω}}. If A has an X-computable copy, then SR(A) ≤ ω1^X + 1. Define ω1^A = min{ω1^X : X ∈ Sp(A)} and, relative to an oracle Y, define ω1^{A,Y} = min{ω1^X : X ∈ Sp(A) and X ≥T Y}. If SR(A) ≥ ω1^{A,Y}, then in fact ω1^{A,Y} = ω1^A and A has high Scott rank in the unrelativized sense.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Scott-rank bound follows from the effective Scott analysis together with Nadel’s theorem: an X-computable structure cannot require distinguishing formulas above ω1^X+1. For the relativized observation, the paper records the chain ω1^{A,Y} ≤ SR(A) ≤ ω1^A + 1 ≤ ω1^{A,Y} + 1. Since the ordinals ω1^A and ω1^{A,Y} are admissible limit ordinals, this forces equality ω1^{A,Y} = ω1^A whenever SR(A) reaches the relativized threshold.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3, Martin’s pointed-tree lemma
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume ZFC plus projective determinacy. Let φ ⊆ 2^ω be a projective class of reals that is cofinal in the Turing degrees, meaning that for every X ∈ 2^ω there is Y ≥T X with Y ∈ φ. Then there is a pointed perfect tree P ⊆ 2^{<ω} such that every path through P belongs to φ. A perfect tree P is pointed if every path through P computes P itself; for such P, the path P(X) obtained by following X through the splitting nodes satisfies P(X) ≡T X⊕P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes Martin’s theorem. The role of pointedness is crucial: if every path through P satisfies φ and each such path computes P, then closing the set of paths under Turing equivalence yields the entire cone above P. The lemma is a determinacy-driven way of strengthening cofinality in the Turing degrees to a uniform perfect set, and hence to a cone after imposing Turing invariance.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.4, Turing determinacy
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume ZFC plus projective determinacy. If φ ⊆ 2^ω is projective and invariant under Turing equivalence, then either φ contains a Turing cone or φ is disjoint from a Turing cone. A Turing cone is a set of the form {X ∈ 2^ω : X ≥T Y} for some base real Y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If φ is cofinal in the Turing degrees, apply Lemma 2.3 to obtain a pointed perfect tree P whose paths are in φ. Since φ is Turing-invariant and every real above P is Turing equivalent to a path through P joined with P, φ contains the cone above P. If φ is not cofinal, then its complement is cofinal above some degree, and the same argument gives a cone disjoint from φ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.5, Martin’s cone-constancy lemma for projective ordinal functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume ZFC plus projective determinacy. Let f : 2^ω → ω1 be invariant under Turing equivalence. Suppose f has a projective presentation, meaning there is a projective map g : 2^ω → 2^ω such that g(X) codes a well-order isomorphic to f(X), and suppose f(X) < ω1^X for every X. Then f is constant on a Turing cone.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each X, because f(X) < ω1^X, some Turing functional e computes from X a well-order of type f(X). By countability of e, for some fixed e this happens on a cofinal set of X. Martin’s pointed-tree lemma gives a pointed perfect tree P such that for every path X through P, {e}^X is a well-order isomorphic to f(X). Reparameterizing paths as P(Y), the map Y ↦ {e}^{P(Y)} is continuous from 2^ω into the set of well-orders and agrees with f on the cone above P. By Σ1^1-boundedness, the order types in the range of this continuous map are bounded below ω1. Hence f is bounded on a cone. Since f is Turing-invariant and projectively presented, one of the countably many values below that bound occurs cofinally; Turing determinacy then gives a subcone on which f is exactly that ordinal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.1 and Theorem 2.2, scattered projective ranked equivalence relations satisfy rank-bounded computability on a cone
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A ranked equivalence relation consists of a set K ⊆ 2^ω, an equivalence relation ≡ on K, and a rank function r : K/≡ → ω1. It is projective if K and ≡ are projective and the rank has a projective presentation by real codes for well-orders. It is scattered if for each countable ordinal α, the set of ≡-classes of rank α is countable. Under ZFC plus projective determinacy, if (K, ≡, r) is a scattered projective ranked equivalence relation, then relative to some oracle, for every real Z and every X ∈ K, if r(X) < ω1^Z, then Z computes a member of the ≡-equivalence class [X].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If there are only countably many ≡-classes, choose an oracle computing one representative from each class, making the conclusion immediate. Otherwise, the rank image is unbounded in ω1. Suppose toward contradiction that on every cone there is a bad Z for which some class [X] has r(X) < ω1^Z but no Z-computable representative. By Turing determinacy, there is a cone of such bad Z. For each bad Z in that cone, let α_Z be the least ordinal α < ω1^Z such that some class of rank α has no Z-computable representative. The map Z ↦ α_Z is Turing-invariant and projectively presented, so Lemma 2.5 makes it constant, say α, on a further cone. But there are only countably many classes of rank α, and hence an oracle sufficiently high in the cone computes representatives for all of them, contradicting the definition of α_Z. This proves that, after moving to a suitable cone, every class of rank below ω1^Z has a Z-computable representative.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3, club of agreement for high-rank models of a minimal counterexample
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be a minimal counterexample to Vaught’s conjecture: T has ℵ1 many countable models, T is scattered, and for every Lω1,ω sentence φ, either T∧φ or T∧¬φ has only countably many countable models. Then there is a closed unbounded set C ⊆ ω1 such that for every α ∈ C, any two models of T whose Scott ranks are at least α are Σ^in_{<α}-elementarily equivalent, meaning they satisfy the same infinitary formulas of complexity below α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define C to be the set of α with the stated high-rank agreement property. To prove unboundedness, begin with any α0 and construct an increasing sequence α_i. Given α_i, each ≡_{α_i}-equivalence class of models is defined by a Π^in_{α_i+1} sentence ψ_σ. By minimality of T, for each σ either the class T∧ψ_σ or its complement within T has countably many models. Thus all but possibly one ≡_{α_i}-class contain only countably many models. Since T is scattered, there are only countably many ≡_{α_i}-classes; choose α_{i+1} bounding the Scott ranks of all models lying in the countable classes. Let α = sup_i α_i. If two models have Scott rank at least α, then at each stage i they must lie in the unique non-countable ≡_{α_i}-class, so they are equivalent for all complexities below α. Closedness follows from the same limiting argument.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4 and Corollary 3.5, admissible ordinals are realized by high-Scott-rank models
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be an L^c_{ω1,ω} counterexample to Vaught’s conjecture. For every admissible ordinal α, there is a model A of T such that SR(A) ≥ ω1^A = α. Relativized form: for every oracle Z, there is a model A of T with SR(A) ≥ ω1^{A,Z} = ω1^Z.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose X with ω1^X = α. For each β < α, there is an L^c,X_{ω1,ω} sentence S_β saying that the Scott rank of a model is at least β; such sentences are built using the back-and-forth relations. The theory T together with all S_β for β < α is a Π1^1(X) theory of computable infinitary logic and has a model because T has models of arbitrarily high Scott rank. The property of being a model of this Π1^1(X) theory is Σ1^1(X), so Gandy’s basis theorem gives a model A with ω1^A ≤ ω1^X = α. Since A satisfies all S_β, α ≤ SR(A), while the general bound gives SR(A) ≤ ω1^A+1. Because α and ω1^A are limit ordinals, this forces ω1^A = α and SR(A) is α or α+1. The corollary is obtained by relativizing the entire construction to Z and taking α = ω1^Z.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6, equal-ω1 amalgamation by a generic pullback
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If Y,Z ∈ 2^ω satisfy ω1^Y = ω1^Z, then there exists G ∈ 2^ω such that ω1^Z = ω1^{Z⊕G} = ω1^G = ω1^{G⊕Y} = ω1^Y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let α = ω1^Y = ω1^Z. Take Harrison linear orderings H_Z and H_Y, computable respectively in Z and Y, both of order type α·(1+Q) on domain ω. Let f : H_Z → H_Y be an isomorphism. Choose a permutation g of ω that is hyperarithmetically generic relative to Y, Z, and f. Genericity over Z gives ω1^{g⊕Z} = ω1^Z; genericity of f∘g over Y gives ω1^{f∘g⊕Y} = ω1^Y. Let G be the pullback of H_Z through g. Then G has initial segments of all order types below α, so α ≤ ω1^G, while G ≤T g⊕Z implies ω1^G ≤ ω1^{G⊕Z} ≤ ω1^{g⊕Z} = α. Thus ω1^G = ω1^{G⊕Z} = α. Since the same G is also the pullback of H_Y through f∘g, the identical argument yields ω1^{G⊕Y} = α.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2, spectra of models of a minimal counterexample on a cone
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be a minimal counterexample to Vaught’s conjecture. Then there is an oracle Z0 such that, for every Z ≥T Z0 and every model A of T, ω1^{A,Z0} ≤ ω1^Z if and only if Z ∈ Sp(A). Here ω1^{A,Z0} is the minimum of ω1^X over X ≥T Z0 that compute a copy of A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication from Z computing A to ω1^{A,Z0} ≤ ω1^Z is immediate. For the converse, let K be the class of models of T and apply Theorem 2.2 to the scattered projective ranked equivalence relation (K, ≅, SR), yielding a cone C4 on which every Z computes all models A with SR(A) < ω1^Z. Relativize to the base and make T computably infinitary. Let C3 be the set of Z that compute at least one model A with SR(A) ≥ ω1^{A,Z} = ω1^Z; by Corollary 3.5 this is cofinal, hence contains a cone with base Z3. Define C0 to be the set of Z that compute all models A with SR(A) ≥ ω1^{A,Z3} = ω1^Z. To show C0 is cofinal, start with Z2 ≥ Z3, choose a Z2-admissible ordinal α in the club from Lemma 3.3, and choose Z1 ≥ Z2 with ω1^{Z1} = α. For any A with SR(A) ≥ ω1^{A,Z3} = α, choose Y ≥ Z3 computing A with ω1^Y = α and use Lemma 3.6, relativized to Z3, to get G with all relevant joins still having ω1 = α. Since G and Z1 lie above the C3 cone, G computes a high-rank B and Z1 computes a high-rank C. The models A, B, C all have Scott rank at least α, so Lemma 3.3 makes them Σ^in_{<α}-equivalent. Because ω1^{Z1⊕G} = α and Z1⊕G computes B and C, Nadel’s theorem gives B ≅ C; similarly, G⊕Y computes A and B and gives A ≅ B. Therefore Z1 computes A, proving cofinality of C0. Turing determinacy gives a cone contained in C0 with base Z0. Finally, if Z ≥ Z0 and ω1^{A,Z0} ≤ ω1^Z, then either SR(A) < ω1^Z and C4 gives a Z-copy, or SR(A) ≥ ω1^Z, in which case the ordinal inequalities force SR(A) ≥ ω1^{A,Z3} = ω1^Z and C0 gives a Z-copy.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.2, Karp/Ash-Knight characterization of fine back-and-forth relations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a countable relational language, and for a tuple ā of length k in a structure A let D_A(ā) be the atomic diagram of ā using the first k symbols of L. Define (A, ā) ≤0 (B, b̄) when D_A(ā) = D_B(b̄). For α > 0, define (A, ā) ≤α (B, b̄) when for every finite tuple d̄ from B and every γ < α, there is a finite tuple c̄ from A such that (A, ā c̄) ≥γ (B, b̄ d̄). For α ≥ 1, the following are equivalent: (1) (A, ā) ≤α (B, b̄); (2) every Π^in_α formula true of ā in A is true of b̄ in B; (3) given a structure (C, c̄) known to be isomorphic to either (A, ā) or (B, b̄), the problem of deciding whether it is isomorphic to (A, ā) is boldface Σ^0_α-hard, i.e. for every Σ^0_α set C0 ⊆ 2^ω there is a continuous operator F such that F(X) is a copy of (A, ā) for X ∈ C0 and a copy of (B, b̄) otherwise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence of the back-and-forth definition with Π^in_α-type inclusion is Karp’s theorem and follows by induction on α: universal infinitary information at level α is preserved exactly when every finite extension on the right can be matched by a sufficiently equivalent extension on the left at all lower levels. The hardness formulation is quoted from Ash and Knight, with the paper noting that the exact stated form follows by relativization. It interprets back-and-forth reducibility as the descriptive/computability-theoretic difficulty of distinguishing the two possible isomorphism types.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 4.3 and extension-set identity for α-back-and-forth structures
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a class K of structures, let bf_{α,k}(K) be the quotient of pairs (A, ā), with A ∈ K and ā ∈ A^k, by the equivalence relation ≡α induced by ≤α and ≥α; it is partially ordered by ≤α. Let bf_α(K) = ⋃_k bf_{α,k}(K). If σ is the α-back-and-forth type of (A, ā), write |σ| = |ā|. For β < γ and a tuple ā in A, ext_β(A, ā) is the ≤β-downward closure in bf_β(K) of all β-types of tuples (A, ā d̄). Then (A, ā) ≤γ (B, b̄) if and only if for every β < γ, ext_β(A, ā) ⊇ ext_β(B, b̄). Consequently, for σ,τ ∈ bf_γ(K), σ ≤γ τ iff ext_β(σ) ⊇ ext_β(τ) for every β < γ. The α-back-and-forth structure of K consists of all partial orders bf_{β,k}(K) for β ≤ α and k ∈ ω, together with the projection maps between levels and tuple rearrangements, the extension relations τ ∈ ext_β(σ), and the finite atomic diagram map on 0-types.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The identity is a direct unpacking of the recursive definition of ≤γ. To say (A, ā) ≤γ (B, b̄) is to say that every finite extension of b̄ in B can be matched by an extension of ā in A at every lower back-and-forth level β. Recording all lower β-types obtainable as extensions gives exactly ext_β; the matching condition is therefore equivalent to inclusion of extension sets in the reversed direction. This identity is what makes the back-and-forth structure first-order presentable and later computable from its abstract presentation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4.2, expanded language L^B_α and recursive definitions of back-and-forth type predicates
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B be a presentation of the α-back-and-forth structure of a class K, typically the class of models of T. Define an expanded language L^B_α = L ∪ {φ_σ : σ ∈ bf_β(K), β ≤ α}, where φ_σ is a relation symbol of arity |σ|. For A ∈ K, define A^{(α)} by A^{(α)} ⊨ φ_σ(x̄) iff σ ≤β (A, x̄), where σ ∈ bf_β(K). If B is X-computable and σ ∈ bf_β, then φ_σ is equivalent on K to a Π^c,X_β computable infinitary formula. For β = 0 it says that x̄ has the finite atomic diagram D(σ); for β > 0 it is recursively equivalent to the conjunction over γ < β and τ ∈ bf_γ with τ ∉ ext_γ(σ) of ∀ȳ ¬φ_τ(x̄,ȳ). Moreover, the predicates φ_σ for σ ∈ bf_{≤α} form a complete set of Π^in_α formulas for K in the sense that every Σ^in_{α+1} L-formula is equivalent on K to a Σ^in_1 formula in the expanded language L^B_α. For β < α, the exact β-type predicate ψ_τ(x̄), meaning τ ≡β (A,x̄), can be defined by ψ_τ = ⋁{φ_σ : σ ∈ bf_α and (σ)_β = τ}, and also by ψ_σ(x̄) iff φ_σ(x̄) and, for every γ < β and τ ∈ ext_γ(σ), there exists ȳ with φ_τ(x̄,ȳ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The recursive Π^c,X_β definition follows from the extension-set identity: σ ≤β (A,x̄) holds exactly when no forbidden lower-level extension type τ outside ext_γ(σ) appears over x̄, for any γ < β. At level 0, only the finite atomic diagram is involved. Exact type ψ_τ is obtained either by disjoining all α-types projecting to τ or by saying both that τ is below the tuple and that all extension possibilities required by τ are realized. The completeness claim is proved in the paper by observing that the expanded predicates name all Π-level back-and-forth information, so one higher existential infinitary complexity in L can be translated to existential formulas over the named type relations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 4.5, Lemma 4.6, and Corollary 4.7, the theory T^B_α correctly enforces back-and-forth types
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Given a presentation B of the α-back-and-forth structure, the paper defines a Π^in_2 theory T^B_α in the expanded language L^B_α. Its axioms assert: every tuple has some α-type and exact lower-level types are unique; each φ_σ obeys the recursive definition in terms of forbidden lower extensions; and each exact type ψ_σ implies φ_σ and realizes all required lower extension types. Lemma 4.6 states that if C ⊨ T^B_α, σ ∈ bf_β, and c̄ is a tuple of the right length in C, then C ⊨ φ_σ(c̄) implies σ ≤β (C,c̄). If β < α, then C ⊨ ψ_σ(c̄) implies σ ≡β (C,c̄). Corollary 4.7 states that if T is Π^in_α-axiomatizable and C ⊨ T^B_α, then the L-reduct of C is a model of T.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 4.6 is proved by simultaneous induction on β. For φ_σ, choose a genuine tuple (A,ā) of β-type σ. Given an extension d̄ of c̄ in C and γ < β, the axioms give an exact γ-type τ for (c̄,d̄). Because C also satisfies φ_σ(c̄), the recursive axiom forbids τ from lying outside ext_γ(σ), so τ ∈ ext_γ(σ). By definition of extension sets there is an extension āē in A whose γ-type is at least τ, and the induction hypothesis gives the required back-and-forth comparison. For ψ_σ, the axioms give φ_σ plus realization of every τ ∈ ext_γ(σ), and the induction hypothesis supplies the reverse comparisons. For Corollary 4.7, choose an α-type σ asserted by the existence axiom. A genuine model A of T realizing σ satisfies A ≤α C by Lemma 4.6, so C satisfies all Π^in_α sentences true in A; since T is Π^in_α, C satisfies T.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.8, computable model from a Π^c_2 theory with c.e. finite diagrams
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be a Π^c_2 theory in a computable language, and let K be its class of countable models. Let K_fin be the set of finite atomic diagrams, over finite restrictions of the language, that occur as finite substructures of members of K. If K_fin is computably enumerable, then T has a computable model.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write T as ⋀_i ∀ȳ_i θ_i(ȳ_i), where each θ_i is Σ^c_1. Build an increasing chain of finite structures A_s whose finite diagrams D_s lie in K_fin. At stage s, take the least pair (i,ā) not yet addressed, where ā is a tuple from A_s of the arity of ȳ_i. Search effectively through the c.e. set K_fin for a finite extension A_{s+1} of A_s such that A_{s+1} ⊨ θ_i(ā). Such an extension exists because A_s occurs inside some genuine model B of T, and B satisfies θ_i(ā), which is witnessed in a finite substructure. The union A = ⋃_s A_s is computable and satisfies every requirement ∀ȳ_i θ_i(ȳ_i), hence models T.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.9, building a computable model of a prescribed α-back-and-forth type
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B be a presentation of the α-back-and-forth structure of a theory T, and let σ ∈ bf_{α,k}. Suppose T is Π^in_α-axiomatizable. Then there is a structure (A,ā) such that A ⊨ T, ā has α-back-and-forth type σ, and the atomic diagram of (A,ā) is computable from B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume B is computable, after relativization. Add constants c̄ of length k and consider the expanded theory T_{α,σ} = T^B_α ∪ {φ_σ(c̄)} ∪ {∃x̄ φ_τ(c̄,x̄) : γ < α and τ ∈ ext_γ(σ)}. Any model of this theory has L-reduct satisfying T by Corollary 4.7, and Lemma 4.6 plus the exact-type characterization imply that c̄ has α-type σ. To get a computable model, use Lemma 4.8. The finite diagrams of models of T_{α,σ} are computably enumerable from B: for each α-type τ extending σ, compute the L^B_α-diagram D_α(τ) of a tuple of type τ, with the L-part given by the 0-type and with φ_δ true exactly when δ ≤β (τ)_β. The finite diagrams are precisely those appearing in such D_α(τ) for τ ⊇ σ. Hence the Π^c_2 theory T_{α,σ} has a computable model, uniformly in B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.10, recognizing correct back-and-forth structures is Π1^1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The set of triples (T, α, B), where T is an Lω1,ω theory or sentence, α is a countable ordinal code, and B is a countable structure, such that B is the α-back-and-forth structure of T, is a Π1^1 set.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First fix an ordinal α and a countable list K of L-structures. From K one can construct the true α-back-and-forth structure C in a Δ1^1 way. To test whether B ≅ C, proceed level by level. At level 0, compare finite atomic diagrams occurring in K. Given an isomorphism below β, match each σ ∈ bf^B_β with a candidate σ̂ ∈ bf^C_β whose lower extension sets ext_γ agree under the previously built isomorphism for all γ < β; failure means B is incorrect, and success gives the level-β map to be checked as an isomorphism. This is Δ1^1 for a fixed list K. For a theory T, use Lemma 4.9 to build from B a list K of models of T^B_α realizing every α-type named in B. Check, in a Δ1^1 manner, that these structures satisfy T and that B is the α-back-and-forth structure of K. Finally require that every model of T is ≡α-equivalent to some model in K; this universal quantification over countable models is Π1^1. Therefore the whole correctness property is Π1^1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1, cone-relative spectra for every counterexample to Vaught’s conjecture
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be an Lω1,ω counterexample to Vaught’s conjecture, meaning T is scattered and has uncountably many countable models. Then there is an oracle Z0 such that for every Z ≥T Z0 and every model A of T, Z computes a copy of A if and only if ω1^{A,Z0} ≤ ω1^Z.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First, apply Theorem 2.2 to the projective scattered ranked equivalence relation whose elements are presentations B of β-back-and-forth structures of T, with rank r(B)=β and equivalence isomorphism. Lemma 4.10 supplies projectivity. On a cone, every X computes a presentation of the β-back-and-forth structure of T for every β < ω1^X; relativize to this cone. Now fix Z and A with α = ω1^Z and ω1^{A} ≤ α after the relativization. Choose Y computing A with ω1^Y = α, and use Lemma 3.6 to find G such that ω1^{Z⊕G} = ω1^G = ω1^{G⊕Y} = α. Work inside an ω-model M_{G,Y} of enough ZFC containing G,Y and all ordinals below α but omitting α, whose existence is attributed to H. Friedman via Gandy’s theorem. For each β < α, G computes the true β-back-and-forth structure, and this correctness is Π1^1 absolute into M_{G,Y}. Since M_{G,Y} omits α, overspill gives an ill-founded ordinal α* of M and a G-computable structure B_{α*} which M believes is the α*-back-and-forth structure of T; its well-founded initial segments below α are the true ones by uniqueness. Inside M, the model A has some α*-type σ*. Lemma 4.9, applied inside M, gives a G-computable model A* of T with α*-type σ*. Thus A and A* are ≡_{α*} inside M, hence in the real universe they agree on all Σ^c,G⊕Y_β formulas for β < α. Since both are computable in G⊕Y and ω1^{G⊕Y}=α, Nadel’s theorem gives A ≅ A*. Therefore G computes a copy of A. Repeating the same argument with an ω-model M_{Z,G} containing Z and G shows Z computes A*. Hence Z computes A. The reverse implication is immediate from the definition of ω1^{A,Z0}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2, exact family of spectra for counterexamples
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be an Lω1,ω counterexample to Vaught’s conjecture. Then there is an oracle Z0 such that, relative to Z0, a class C ⊆ 2^ω is the degree spectrum of some model of T if and only if C = {Z ∈ 2^ω : ω1^{Z⊕Z0} ≥ α} for some admissible ordinal α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Work relative to the oracle Z0 from Theorem 5.1 and enlarge it if necessary so that T is a computable infinitary sentence. Theorem 5.1 gives, for every model A of T, Sp(A) = {Z : ω1^Z ≥ ω1^A} in the relativized universe, so every spectrum has the stated threshold form with α = ω1^A. Conversely, for every admissible α, Lemma 3.4 produces a model A of T with ω1^A = α and high Scott rank, so its spectrum is exactly {Z : ω1^Z ≥ α}. The theorem states admissible α because values of ω1^Z are admissible; the formulation with all countable α in Theorem 1.1 yields the same threshold family after identifying repeated thresholds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 5.1, coding into the α-th jump proves hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on a cone implies scatteredness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose T satisfies hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on a cone. After relativizing to the base of the cone, if T had continuum many α-back-and-forth types for some countable α, then one could build an oracle Y and a model A of T that is hyperarithmetic in Y but has no Y-computable copy, contradicting the cone hypothesis. Therefore, for a theory T with uncountably many countable models, hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on a cone implies T is scattered, and hence T is a counterexample to Vaught’s conjecture in the paper’s sense.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume α is least such that there are continuum many α-back-and-forth types. By adding constants, assume bf_{α,0} has size continuum. Relativize so that α is computable and the (<α)-back-and-forth structure of T is computable. Let L_{<α} be the union of the expanded languages naming all lower back-and-forth type predicates. For each model A of T, let A^{(<α)} be the corresponding expansion; it has a copy Δ^0_α in the diagram of A. Let t_A be the finitary Σ1 theory of A^{(<α)}, viewed as a real via an effective list of Σ1 formulas. This t_A determines the Σ^in_α L-theory of A, and t_A = t_B iff A ≡α B. Hence R = {t_A : A ⊨ T} is an analytic subset of 2^ω of size continuum. By Suslin’s theorem, R has a perfect closed subset [S]; relativize so the perfect tree S is computable and view S as an order-preserving map from 2^{<ω} to 2^{<ω}. Choose an oracle Y above all relativizations and choose X hyperarithmetic in Y such that the lexicographic left cut Z_X = {σ ∈ 2^{<ω} : X ≥_lex σ} is not Σ^0_α(Y). The path S(X) is a Σ1-L_{<α} type consistent with T and has Turing degree X. Form the theory T_hat consisting of T_α, all Σ1 sentences ψ in S(X), and the negations of all Σ1-L_{<α} sentences not in S(X). This is Π^c,X_2, and its finite expanded substructures are determined by S(X). The finite-diagram construction from Section 4 gives an X-computable model A^{(<α)} of T_hat; its L-reduct A is a model of T and is hyperarithmetic in Y. If B is any copy of A, then B^{(<α)} can enumerate S(X), hence can enumerate the lexicographic cut of S(X), and since S is computable it can enumerate Z_X. Thus Z_X is Σ^0_α in the diagram of B. If B were Y-computable, then Z_X would be Σ^0_α(Y), contradicting the choice of X. So A has no Y-computable copy despite being hyperarithmetic in Y.

proof_techniques.name: Cone extraction from projective cofinality
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly turns a projective property that holds cofinally in the Turing degrees into a property holding on a cone. The mechanism is Martin’s pointed-tree lemma: a cofinal projective class contains all paths through a pointed perfect tree, and Turing invariance then upgrades this to a full cone. For ordinal-valued projective functions f with f(X) < ω1^X, Lemma 2.5 further shows that f is constant on a cone, using a fixed Turing functional on a cofinal set and Σ1^1-boundedness of continuous families of well-orders.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the main template for proving any computability-on-a-cone assertion about automorphisms: formulate the obstruction or required computation as a projective Turing-invariant property, show cofinality, and use determinacy to obtain a single cone base.

proof_techniques.name: Ranked equivalence relation with countable fibers
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 2.2 abstracts the common structure behind several hyperarithmetic-is-recursive phenomena. One has a projective class K, a projective equivalence relation ≡, and an ordinal rank r on equivalence classes with only countably many classes at each rank. If every bad oracle has a least bad rank below its ω1, Martin’s cone-constancy lemma makes that least bad rank fixed on a cone; countability of the fiber then lets a sufficiently high oracle compute representatives for all classes at that rank, contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A possible route for automorphism groups is to define an equivalence relation on automorphisms or partial automorphism data, equip it with a rank measuring back-and-forth complexity, and use countable rank fibers to force computability of all automorphisms on a cone.

proof_techniques.name: Relativize away all nonuniform data
proof_techniques.description: Throughout the paper, proofs begin by moving to a cone and then relativizing so that the sentence T, presentations of relevant back-and-forth structures, perfect trees, or bases of earlier cones become computable. Once this is done, arguments can be phrased as ordinary computable constructions relative to the current oracle. The final statements are then read back as cone-relative assertions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original question, finite parameters, the computable presentation of the structure, and any coding of the language or automorphism group can be absorbed into a cone base before attempting a uniform construction or definability analysis.

proof_techniques.name: Fine α-back-and-forth structure as an effective invariant
proof_techniques.description: The paper packages all α-level back-and-forth types bf_{β,k}, their partial orders, projections, tuple rearrangements, extension relations, and finite atomic diagrams into a first-order object: the α-back-and-forth structure. This structure records exactly which lower-level extension types are possible over a tuple, and the identity σ ≤γ τ iff ext_β(σ) ⊇ ext_β(τ) for all β < γ makes the recursive behavior explicit.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Automorphism definability is strongly tied to tuple orbits and back-and-forth types. A similar type-extension structure for tuples together with their images under partial automorphisms could help compare computability of automorphisms with Σ^in_1 definability from parameters.

proof_techniques.name: Naming back-and-forth type predicates to lower infinitary complexity
proof_techniques.description: Given a presentation B of the α-back-and-forth structure, the expanded language L^B_α adds relation symbols φ_σ for the predicate σ ≤β (A,x̄). These predicates are equivalent to Π^c_β formulas, recursively defined by forbidding lower-level extension types outside ext_γ(σ). In the expansion, every Σ^in_{α+1} formula over the original language becomes equivalent, on the class K, to a Σ^in_1 formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem explicitly concerns Σ^in_1-definability of automorphisms. This expansion trick suggests a way to separate true Σ^in_1 definability in the original language from Σ^in_1 definability after naming enough back-and-forth information, or to prove that certain graphs become existentially definable once the right type predicates are named.

proof_techniques.name: Π2 finite-extension construction for computable models
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.8 gives a Henkin-style construction for Π^c_2 theories whose finite diagrams are computably enumerable. One builds an increasing sequence of finite substructures, meeting each universal-existential requirement by searching within the c.e. set of finite diagrams known to occur in genuine models. Lemma 4.9 applies this to build a computable model realizing a prescribed α-back-and-forth type.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can axiomatize structures with a desired automorphism or partial automorphism behavior by Π2 conditions and keep finite diagrams c.e., this provides a direct way to construct computable copies or computable automorphisms with controlled type behavior.

proof_techniques.name: Coding non-Σ^0_α information through a perfect set of types
proof_techniques.description: Section 5.1 shows how continuum many α-back-and-forth types allow a perfect set of Σ1 theories in the expanded language. By choosing a path X hyperarithmetic in Y whose lexicographic cut is not Σ^0_α(Y), and forcing the model’s expanded Σ1 theory to be S(X), every copy of the model must compute enough α-jump information to enumerate that cut. The model is still hyperarithmetic in Y, but no Y-computable copy can exist.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a powerful obstruction principle: too many distinguishable infinitary types can be used to code noncomputable information into every copy. For automorphisms, an analogous perfect-set coding of automorphism graphs or orbit maps might show that computable AUT-countability on a cone imposes strong countability or definability restrictions.

proof_techniques.name: Nonstandard overspill of computable back-and-forth structures
proof_techniques.description: In Theorem 5.1, after ensuring that an oracle G computes the β-back-and-forth structure for every β < ω1^G, the proof enters an ω-model of enough ZFC containing all smaller ordinals but omitting α = ω1^G. Overspill gives an ill-founded ordinal α* and a G-computable object that the model believes is the α*-back-and-forth structure. Lemma 4.9 then builds a G-computable model with a prescribed nonstandard type, and Nadel’s theorem converts agreement on all standard computable infinitary formulas into isomorphism.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If automorphism data can be approximated by all standard finite or transfinite back-and-forth levels below ω1^X, a nonstandard overspill argument may produce actual computable automorphisms or copies satisfying an apparently stronger type condition.

proof_techniques.name: Equalizing admissible ordinals using Harrison orderings and generic permutations
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.6 constructs, from Y and Z with the same ω1, a real G such that joining G to either Y or Z does not raise ω1. The construction uses Harrison pseudo-well-orderings of type α·(1+Q), an isomorphism between them, and a hyperarithmetically generic permutation to preserve admissibility under joins.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Cone proofs involving automorphisms may need several oracles to compute different objects while keeping the same admissible ordinal so that Nadel-style comparisons apply. This lemma is a reusable tool for synchronizing such oracles.

proof_techniques.name: High-Scott-rank club agreement
proof_techniques.description: For minimal counterexamples, Lemma 3.3 extracts a club of ordinals α such that all models of Scott rank at least α are indistinguishable by formulas below α. The proof uses minimality to show that at each scattered equivalence level there is at most one class containing uncountably many models, then bounds the ranks of the countable exceptional classes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A similar club-of-agreement argument could be useful if one studies structures whose automorphism groups have many high-complexity orbits but only countably many low-level orbit types.

other_useful_info: The paper uses the convention that infinitary disjunctions count as existential quantifiers and infinitary conjunctions as universal quantifiers, yielding hierarchies Σ^in_α and Π^in_α and their computable versions Σ^c,X_α and Π^c,X_α; every Σ^in_α formula is Σ^c,X_α for some oracle X. The paper notes examples covered by Theorem 2.2: Spector’s theorem for well-orders, hyperarithmetic-is-recursive up to bi-embeddability for linear orderings using Hausdorff rank, and the analogous result for p-groups using Ulm rank. It emphasizes that the cone bases arise from determinacy/strategy information and that the main results are proved under projective determinacy, not in ZFC alone. The cited sources most directly relevant to back-and-forth and infinitary definability are Ash and Knight, Computable Structures and the Hyperarithmetical Hierarchy, and Montalbán’s earlier paper Counting the back-and-forth types.
why_relevant: Develops the on-a-cone methodology and the hyperarithmetic-is-recursive phenomenon for countable structures. Its use of Sigma-in formulas, Scott rank, and cone arguments is closely analogous to computably AUT-countable on a cone.

title: Analytic equivalence relations satisfying hyperarithmetic-is-recursive
overall_summary: Montalbán proves that, assuming ZF plus Σ^1_2-determinacy, an analytic equivalence relation E on 2^ω has no perfect set of pairwise E-inequivalent reals if and only if E satisfies hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on a cone, and equivalently its equivalence-class degree spectra are, after relativizing to one oracle, exactly upper cones of the form {X : ω_1^X ≥ α}. The core proof approximates an analytic equivalence relation by a nested family of Borel equivalence relations E_α, then combines Silver's theorem, Barwise compactness, overspill, and Turing determinacy to turn cofinal constructions into cone statements. The converse direction uses Σ^1_1-boundedness and transfinite Cohen genericity to show that a perfect antichain prevents hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on any cone. For the original automorphism/copy problem, the most useful imports are the general pattern: encode structural data as reals, analyze an analytic equivalence relation or orbit relation via Borel rank approximations, prove a cofinal relative-computability property, and then upgrade it to a cone. The paper also gives a reversal showing that the determinacy/sharp strength behind the cone theorem is real, not just an artifact.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume ZF + Σ^1_2-determinacy. Let E be an analytic equivalence relation on Cantor space 2^ω. For Y ∈ 2^ω, write [Y]_E for its E-equivalence class. For an oracle C, define the relativized degree spectrum Sp^C([Y]_E) = {X ∈ 2^ω : there is W ≤_T X ⊕ C with W E Y}. Then the following are equivalent. (H1) There is no perfect set P ⊆ 2^ω such that any two distinct elements of P are E-inequivalent. (H2) E satisfies hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on a cone: there is C ∈ 2^ω such that for every X ≥_T C and every real Y hyperarithmetic in X, there is W ≤_T X such that W E Y. (H3) Relative to some oracle C, every E-class has a spectrum determined only by a lower bound on admissible ordinals: for every Y ∈ 2^ω there is a countable ordinal α_Y such that Sp^C([Y]_E) = {X ∈ 2^ω : ω_1^{X⊕C} ≥ α_Y}. The paper further notes the proof-theoretic strengths: (H3) ⇒ (H2) and (H2) ⇒ (H1) are proved in ZF; (H1) ⇒ (H2) uses only Σ^1_1-determinacy; (H1) ⇒ (H3) uses Σ^1_2-determinacy.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (H1) ⇒ (H2), use Lemma 2.3 below. Let S_1 = {X : for every Y hyperarithmetic in X, there is W ≤_T X with W E Y}. The set S_1 is Σ^1_1: the universal quantifier over Y ≤_hyp X can be replaced by an existential quantifier over reals, as in higher recursion theory. It is degree invariant. Lemma 2.3 gives a cofinal degree-invariant set S of reals X such that whenever ω_1^{X⊕Y} = ω_1^X, X computes a member of [Y]_E. If Y ≤_hyp X, then by Spector's theorem ω_1^{X⊕Y} = ω_1^X, so S ⊆ S_1. Hence S_1 is cofinal in the Turing degrees. By Σ^1_1-Turing determinacy, which follows from Σ^1_1-determinacy, every cofinal degree-invariant Σ^1_1 set contains a cone; therefore S_1 contains a cone. For (H1) ⇒ (H3), consider the set S from Lemma 2.3. This set is Π^1_2 and degree invariant: the relation ω_1^{X⊕Y} = ω_1^X is Σ^1_1, and X ∈ Sp([Y]_E) is Σ^1_1. Lemma 2.3 says S is cofinal, so by Σ^1_2-Turing determinacy S contains a cone. Relativize to the base of this cone, so every real belongs to S. For each Y define ω_1^{[Y]} = min{ω_1^W : W E Y}. The proof shows Sp([Y]_E) = {X : ω_1^X ≥ ω_1^{[Y]}}. The inclusion from left to right is immediate from the definition of the minimum. Conversely, suppose ω_1^X ≥ ω_1^{[Y]}, and replace Y by an E-equivalent real with ω_1^Y = ω_1^{[Y]}. If ω_1^{X⊕Y} = ω_1^X, then X ∈ S directly yields a W ≤_T X with W E Y. Otherwise, choose Z ≥_T Y with ω_1^Z = ω_1^X. A theorem of Harrington, also cited in an alternative proof by Montalbán, gives a real G such that ω_1^X = ω_1^{X⊕G} = ω_1^G = ω_1^{G⊕Z} = ω_1^Z. Since G ∈ S and Y ≤_T Z, we have ω_1^{G⊕Y} ≤ ω_1^{G⊕Z} = ω_1^G, so G computes Y_1 with Y_1 E Y. Then ω_1^{Y_1⊕X} ≤ ω_1^{G⊕X} = ω_1^X; since X ∈ S, X computes Y_2 with Y_2 E Y_1, hence Y_2 E Y. This gives the spectrum formula. For (H3) ⇒ (H2), if C witnesses (H3), X ≥_T C, and Y ≤_hyp X, then Y ⊕ C is hyperarithmetic in X, so ω_1^{Y⊕C} ≤ ω_1^X = ω_1^{X⊕C}. Since Y itself computes a member of [Y]_E relative to C, α_Y ≤ ω_1^{Y⊕C}; hence X belongs to Sp^C([Y]_E), and because C ≤_T X this gives a member of [Y]_E computable from X. For (H2) ⇒ (H1), argue contrapositively. Suppose R ⊆ 2^{<ω} is a perfect tree whose paths are pairwise E-inequivalent. Relativize so that the oracle under consideration and R are computable. Using the Borel approximations E_α from Lemma 2.1, first find α < ω_1^CK such that all distinct paths through R are already E_α-inequivalent. Indeed, for each distinct pair X,Y ∈ [R], the tree used to define E has a well-founded rank witnessing failure of E_β for some β; the map from pairs of distinct paths to well-founded trees is computable, so Σ^1_1-boundedness bounds all these ranks below some computable ordinal α. Let G be an (α+1)-Cohen-generic real, meaning it decides every Σ^0_{α+1} formula, chosen computable from 0^(α+2), and let R(G) be the path through R obtained by following G at each split. Then R(G) is hyperarithmetic. If R(G) were E-equivalent to a computable X, then because E ⊆ E_α and paths through R are E_α-inequivalent, G would be the unique real H such that R(H) E_α X. This uniqueness property is Σ^0_{α+1}. By (α+1)-genericity some finite condition p would force it, but then any other (α+1)-generic extending p would also satisfy it, contradicting uniqueness. Thus R(G) is hyperarithmetic but not E-equivalent to any computable real, so E cannot satisfy hyperarithmetic-is-recursive relative to that oracle. Since the argument relativizes to every cone base, (H2) fails.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every lightface Σ^1_1 equivalence relation E on 2^ω, there is a decreasing nested sequence of equivalence relations {E_α : α ∈ ω_1} such that each E_α is uniformly Σ^0_{α+1} in α, E_β refines E_α whenever α ≤ β, and E = ⋂_{α∈ω_1} E_α. The construction relativizes to an oracle coding a boldface analytic equivalence relation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start with Kleene normal form: choose a computable tree T ⊆ 2^{<ω} × ω^{<ω} × 2^{<ω} such that, for X,Y ∈ 2^ω, if T_{X,Y} = {σ ∈ ω^{<ω} : (X↾|σ|, σ, Y↾|σ|) ∈ T}, then X E Y iff T_{X,Y} is ill-founded. First symmetrize T by replacing it with T ∪ {(τ,σ,ρ) : (ρ,σ,τ) ∈ T}; because E is symmetric, this preserves the represented relation and ensures T_{X,Y} = T_{Y,X}. To force transitivity at every approximation level, define, for each k ≥ 1, a tree T^k_{X,Y}. A node of T^k_{X,Y} of length n is a tuple (σ_1, τ_1, σ_2, τ_2, ..., τ_{k-1}, σ_k) with σ_i ∈ ω^n and τ_i ∈ 2^n such that (X↾n, σ_1, τ_1) ∈ T, (τ_1, σ_2, τ_2) ∈ T, ..., and (τ_{k-1}, σ_k, Y↾n) ∈ T. Thus T^1_{X,Y} = T_{X,Y}. Let ^hat T_{X,Y} be the disjoint union over k of all T^k_{X,Y}, with roots identified. Then X E Y iff ^hat T_{X,Y} is ill-founded: an infinite path through T^k_{X,Y} gives an E-chain from X to Y of length k, and transitivity of E gives X E Y; conversely, if X E Y then T^1_{X,Y} is ill-founded. Define X E_α Y iff rk(^hat T_{X,Y}) ≥ α, treating ill-founded trees as having rank at least every countable ordinal. The relation rk(^hat T_{X,Y}) ≥ α is uniformly Σ^0_{α+1}, and the E_α are nested with intersection E. Reflexivity follows from reflexivity of E, since T_{X,X} is ill-founded. Symmetry follows from the symmetrization, which gives rk(^hat T_{X,Y}) = rk(^hat T_{Y,X}). For transitivity, suppose X E_α Y and Y E_α Z. Since rk(^hat T_{X,Y}) = sup_k rk(T^k_{X,Y}), for every β < α there are k,l with rk(T^k_{X,Y}) ≥ β and rk(T^l_{Y,Z}) ≥ β. If ρ ∈ T^k_{X,Y} and π ∈ T^l_{Y,Z} have the same length n, concatenate them by inserting Y↾n to get a node of T^{k+l}_{X,Z}. This gives an order-preserving embedding of the same-length product into T^{k+l}_{X,Z}, so rk(T^{k+l}_{X,Z}) ≥ min(rk(T^k_{X,Y}), rk(T^l_{Y,Z})) ≥ β. Since this holds for every β < α, X E_α Z.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be a Σ^1_1 equivalence relation and let {E_α} be the approximating equivalence relations constructed in Lemma 2.1. For any X,Y ∈ 2^ω, if X E_{ω_1^{X⊕Y}} Y, then X E Y, where ω_1^{X⊕Y} is the least ordinal not computable from X ⊕ Y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The tree ^hat T_{X,Y} used to define the approximations is computable in X ⊕ Y. If X were not E-equivalent to Y, then ^hat T_{X,Y} would be well-founded. Any well-founded tree computable in X ⊕ Y has rank strictly below ω_1^{X⊕Y}. Therefore it would be impossible to have rk(^hat T_{X,Y}) ≥ ω_1^{X⊕Y}, i.e. impossible to have X E_{ω_1^{X⊕Y}} Y. Hence X E_{ω_1^{X⊕Y}} Y implies X E Y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an analytic equivalence relation on 2^ω with no perfect set of pairwise E-inequivalent reals. Define Sp([Y]_E) = {X ∈ 2^ω : there is W ≤_T X with W E Y}. Let S = {X ∈ 2^ω : for every Y, if ω_1^{X⊕Y} = ω_1^X, then X ∈ Sp([Y]_E)}. Then S is cofinal in the Turing degrees: for every Z ∈ 2^ω there is X ≥_T Z with X ∈ S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix an arbitrary Z. Relativize all subsequent definitions to Z, so one may assume Z is computable and E is lightface Σ^1_1, with the tree ^hat T from Lemma 2.1 computable. For each countable ordinal α, the Borel equivalence relation E_α has no perfect set of pairwise inequivalent reals, because such a perfect set would also be pairwise E-inequivalent. By Silver's theorem, every Borel equivalence relation without perfectly many classes has only countably many classes. Hence, for each α, choose a sequence ⟨A_{α,n} : n ∈ ω⟩ containing one representative from each E_α-class, and code all these representatives by a single set A ⊆ ω_1 × ω × ω. Choose α ∈ ω_1 such that L_α[A] is admissible and every β < α is coded by a well-ordering of ω inside L_α[A]; for example, take α with L_α[A] an elementary substructure of L_{ω_1^{L[A]}}[A]. By Barwise compactness, build an ill-founded model M = (M; ∈^M, A^M) of KP whose ordinals have well-founded part exactly α, with A^M↾α = A↾α, and satisfying that every ordinal of M is coded by a real of M. Pick a nonstandard ordinal α^* ∈ ON^M \ α, and let X be a real in M coding both α^* and A^M↾α^*. Then ω_1^X = α. The inequality ω_1^X ≥ α holds because X codes every initial segment of α. The reverse inequality holds because any X-computable well-ordering is isomorphic to an ordinal of M, so any genuinely well-founded such ordering has order type below the well-founded part α. Now fix Y with ω_1^{X⊕Y} ≤ α, equivalently with ω_1^{X⊕Y} = ω_1^X. View α^* as the coded pseudo-well-ordering. Define P = {β ∈ α^* : there exists W ≤_T X with W E_β Y}. For nonstandard β, E_β is interpreted using the rank definition from Lemma 2.1: W E_β Y means there is a descending rank witness f from β into ^hat T_{W,Y}, i.e. rk(^hat T_{W,Y}) ≥ β in the coded sense. The set P is Σ^1_1(X,Y). It contains every true β < α, because X computes all representatives A_{β,n}, and one of them lies in the E_β-class of Y. Since ω_1^{X⊕Y} ≤ α, the well-founded part α of α^* is not Σ^1_1(X⊕Y): it is Π^1_1(X), and if it were also Σ^1_1(X⊕Y) it would be Δ^1_1(X⊕Y), contradicting the bound on ω_1^{X⊕Y}. Therefore Σ^1_1 overspill gives a nonstandard β^* ∈ P \ α. Let Y^* ≤_T X witness β^* ∈ P, so Y^* E_{β^*} Y. By nestedness, Y^* E_β Y for every true β < α. Also ω_1^{Y⊕Y^*} ≤ ω_1^{Y⊕X} = α. Applying Remark 2.2 yields Y^* E Y. Thus X computes a member of [Y]_E. Since the initial Z was arbitrary and was absorbed by relativization, S is cofinal. The paper also explains how to avoid the displayed use of choice in selecting the representatives: by Shoenfield absoluteness, for β < ω_1^L the sequence of representatives may be chosen inside L_{ω_1^L} as the <_L-least sequence meeting every E_β-class exactly once, and then one can work with this definable A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Over ZF, the following are equivalent. (O1) Every lightface Σ^1_1 equivalence relation E on 2^ω with no perfect set of pairwise E-inequivalent reals satisfies hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on a cone. (O2) 0♯ exists.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication (O2) ⇒ (O1) follows from the proof of Theorem 1.3's implication (H1) ⇒ (H2): for lightface Σ^1_1 equivalence relations, that proof uses only lightface Σ^1_1-determinacy, and Harrington proved that lightface analytic determinacy is equivalent to the existence of 0♯. For (O1) ⇒ (O2), the paper invokes a lemma of Sami. Define S_Sami = {Y ∈ 2^ω : there exists Z ∈ 2^ω such that ω_1^Z = ω_1^Y and every W hyperarithmetic in Z is Turing computable from Y}. Sami proved that if S_Sami contains a cone, then 0♯ exists. Thus it suffices to build, from (O1), a cone contained in S_Sami. Let R be the set of reals coding structures isomorphic to (L_α(A); ∈) for some countable ordinal α and some A ⊆ ω. This R is Π^1_1, since verifying such a code requires checking well-foundedness and checking that each constructible level is correctly obtained from the previous one. Define a Σ^1_1 equivalence relation E as follows. If X,Y ∈ R code structures (L_{α_X}(A_X); ∈) and (L_{α_Y}(A_Y); ∈), then X E Y iff α_X = α_Y and ω_1^{A_X} = ω_1^{A_Y}. All reals outside R are put into one additional E-class. This E has only ℵ_1 many classes, indexed by possible pairs (α, ω_1^A), and since this remains true in every model of ZF and existence of a perfect set of E-inequivalent reals is a Σ^1_2 statement, E has no perfectly many classes. By (O1), E satisfies hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on a cone with base C. Now take any Y ≥_T C. For each α < ω_1^Y, there is a presentation of (L_α(Y); ∈) hyperarithmetic in Y; by the cone property, Y computes an E-equivalent presentation, namely a presentation of (L_α(Z); ∈) for some Z with ω_1^Z = ω_1^Y. Let α^* be a Y-computable Harrison linear ordering of type ω_1^Y + ω_1^Y · Q. Define P = {β ∈ α^* : Y computes a presentation of (L_β(Z); ∈) for some Z with ω_1^Z = ω_1^Y}. The set P is Σ^1_1(Y), and the preceding paragraph shows that it contains every β in the well-founded part of α^*, namely every β < ω_1^Y. By overspill, P contains some nonstandard β^* ∈ α^* \ ω_1^Y. Let Z^* witness this. If W is hyperarithmetic in Z^*, then W belongs to L_β(Z^*) for some β < ω_1^{Z^*} = ω_1^Y; hence W belongs to the Y-computable presentation of L_{β^*}(Z^*) and therefore W ≤_T Y. Also ω_1^{Z^*} = ω_1^Y. Thus Y ∈ S_Sami. Since every Y on the cone above C lies in S_Sami, Sami's result gives 0♯.

proof_techniques.name: Approximating analytic equivalence relations by Borel equivalence relations
proof_techniques.description: The paper converts a Σ^1_1 equivalence relation E into a nested family {E_α}_{α<ω_1} of genuine equivalence relations, each uniformly Borel at level Σ^0_{α+1}, with E equal to their intersection. The main obstacle is that the naive rank approximation to the tree defining E need not be transitive or symmetric. Symmetry is repaired by symmetrizing the defining tree, and transitivity is repaired by building trees T^k_{X,Y} coding E-chains of length k and then taking their disjoint union ^hat T_{X,Y}. Rank cutoffs of ^hat T_{X,Y} are then equivalence relations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If automorphisms, copies, or tuple-orbits can be coded as reals with an analytic equivalence relation, this gives a systematic way to replace the analytic relation by Borel approximations that behave like equivalence relations. Such approximations may be useful for proving cone computability or for locating the exact rank at which nondefinability/noncomputability appears.

proof_techniques.name: Relative Church-Kleene rank cutoff
proof_techniques.description: Remark 2.2 is a simple but powerful rank estimate: for the approximations E_α of Lemma 2.1, if X E_{ω_1^{X⊕Y}} Y, then X E Y. The reason is that the tree whose rank defines E_α is computable from X ⊕ Y; if it were well-founded, its rank would be below the relative Church-Kleene ordinal ω_1^{X⊕Y}. Therefore persistence of approximate equivalence up to that ordinal forces genuine analytic equivalence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a structural setting, this suggests proving actual equivalence or actual existence of an automorphism by showing that all rank-bounded approximations hold up to the admissible ordinal of the relevant diagrams. It can bridge infinitary/back-and-forth approximations and real automorphisms.

proof_techniques.name: Silver theorem at each Borel approximation level
proof_techniques.description: When E has no perfect set of inequivalent reals, every Borel approximation E_α also has no perfect set of inequivalent reals. Silver's theorem then implies that each E_α has only countably many classes. The proof of Lemma 2.3 chooses, for every α, a countable list of representatives for the E_α-classes and codes all these lists into a single set A ⊆ ω_1 × ω × ω.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an automorphism-orbit or copy-equivalence relation has no perfect antichain, one may try to extract countably many canonical representatives at each approximation rank. These representatives can serve as computable or hyperarithmetic targets for later cone arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Barwise compactness plus overspill for cofinal oracle construction
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Lemma 2.3 uses Barwise compactness to build an ill-founded KP model M whose well-founded ordinals are exactly a chosen admissible α and whose predicate A agrees with the real representative system below α. A real X inside M codes a nonstandard ordinal α^* and the representatives up to α^*. This guarantees ω_1^X = α while letting X compute representatives for all standard E_β-classes. For any Y with ω_1^{X⊕Y}=α, the Σ^1_1(X,Y) set P of β such that X computes a member E_β-equivalent to Y contains all standard β<α, so overspill produces a nonstandard β^*. Nestedness plus the Church-Kleene rank cutoff then converts this nonstandard approximate equivalence into true E-equivalence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a template for constructing high oracles or presentations that compute all standard-stage approximations while still having controlled admissible ordinal. It may be adaptable to build copies whose automorphisms are forced to be computable on a cone, or to create nonstandard witnesses for automorphism approximations.

proof_techniques.name: Cofinality-to-cone upgrade by Turing determinacy
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly defines a degree-invariant set S of oracles satisfying a desired relative-computability property, proves S is cofinal in the Turing degrees by an explicit construction, verifies that S has a pointclass complexity such as Σ^1_1 or Π^1_2, and then applies Martin's Turing determinacy for that pointclass to conclude that S contains a cone. For (H1) ⇒ (H2), the relevant set S_1 is Σ^1_1; for (H1) ⇒ (H3), the relevant set S from Lemma 2.3 is Π^1_2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks for a property on a cone. A viable route is to prove the automorphism-computability property cofinally in the degrees and formulate it as a degree-invariant projective set; determinacy can then turn cofinality into the required cone statement.

proof_techniques.name: Using Spector's theorem to pass from admissible-ordinal equality to hyperarithmetic-is-recursive
proof_techniques.description: The cofinal set S in Lemma 2.3 only promises that X computes a representative of [Y]_E when ω_1^{X⊕Y}=ω_1^X. To obtain hyperarithmetic-is-recursive, the paper uses Spector's theorem in the form: if Y is hyperarithmetic in X, then adjoining Y does not change the relative Church-Kleene ordinal, so ω_1^{X⊕Y}=ω_1^X. Thus the admissible-ordinal condition subsumes all X-hyperarithmetic Y.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For automorphisms or copies that are hyperarithmetic in a diagram, it may be enough to prove computability under an invariant condition about preserving ω_1. This can simplify cone arguments by replacing direct hyperarithmetic quantification with an ordinal-spectrum condition.

proof_techniques.name: Degree-spectrum normalization via an intermediate real G
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of (H1) ⇒ (H3), after obtaining a cone on which every real lies in the cofinal set S, the paper proves exact spectrum formulas. The nontrivial case is when X and Y have compatible admissible ordinals but ω_1^{X⊕Y} is too large. A theorem of Harrington provides an intermediate real G such that several joins preserve the same admissible ordinal: ω_1^X = ω_1^{X⊕G} = ω_1^G = ω_1^{G⊕Z} = ω_1^Z. Applying S first to G and then to X transfers an E-equivalent representative from Y to something X computes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When comparing the complexity of automorphisms across different copies, joins with parameters may unexpectedly raise admissible ordinals. An intermediate-real construction of this kind can neutralize the join and allow two-step transfer of computability.

proof_techniques.name: Perfect-set obstruction by bounded ranks and transfinite Cohen genericity
proof_techniques.description: To prove (H2) ⇒ (H1), the paper assumes a perfect tree R of pairwise E-inequivalent paths. Using Σ^1_1-boundedness, it finds a single computable ordinal α such that distinct paths through R are already E_α-inequivalent. Then it chooses an (α+1)-Cohen-generic path R(G) through R, hyperarithmetic in a high enough jump. If R(G) were E-equivalent to a computable real X, then G would be uniquely characterized by the Σ^0_{α+1} property R(G) E_α X. Genericity makes this impossible, because a finite condition forcing the property would also allow another generic extension.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a way to show that too many mutually inequivalent automorphisms/copies obstruct computable AUT-countability on a cone. If one can produce a perfect family of objects whose equivalence is reflected at a bounded approximation level, a generic member can be hyperarithmetic but not computably equivalent to any computable object.

proof_techniques.name: Harrison pseudo-well-order overspill for reversals
proof_techniques.description: In the reversal theorem, the paper uses a Y-computable Harrison ordering of type ω_1^Y + ω_1^Y·Q. A Σ^1_1(Y) set P of initial segments contains the entire well-founded part because the cone hypothesis supplies computable presentations for all true L_α(Y). Overspill puts a nonstandard β^* into P, yielding a Y-computable ill-founded presentation L_{β^*}(Z^*) that contains every real hyperarithmetic in Z^*. This proves that Y computes all Z^*-hyperarithmetic reals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one needs to build pathological but cone-controlled structures, Harrison-order overspill can produce nonstandard initial segments coding all standard hyperarithmetic information. This may help in constructing examples where relative computability holds for global reasons while definability properties fail locally.

proof_techniques.name: Relativization as cone-base management
proof_techniques.description: Throughout the paper, arbitrary parameters are absorbed into a cone base. For example, Lemma 2.3 starts above an arbitrary degree Z and then relativizes so that Z is computable and the analytic relation is lightface. The final cone statements are obtained by unrelativizing. This keeps technical constructions lightface while preserving the intended on-a-cone meaning.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem allows a fixed cone parameter C. Construction parameters for a computable presentation, coding choices, or analytic definitions of automorphism relations can potentially be placed into C, after which the proof may proceed in a lightface setting.

other_useful_info: The introduction recalls Burgess's theorem: an analytic equivalence relation has either at most ℵ_1 equivalence classes or a perfect set of pairwise inequivalent reals; existence of such a perfect set is a Σ^1_2 assertion and hence absolute in the relevant sense. The proof uses Silver's theorem that a Borel equivalence relation with no perfect set of inequivalent reals has countably many classes. The paper points out that in natural examples, such as well-order isomorphism or bi-embeddability of linear orders or p-groups, one may often prove hyperarithmetic-is-recursive relative to every oracle, whereas the general abstract theorem only guarantees the property on a cone. Cited tools that may be worth consulting separately are Barwise compactness for admissible sets [Bar75], Spector's theorem on hyperarithmetic well-orderings [Spe55], Harrington's theorem connecting analytic determinacy and 0♯ [Har78], Sami's forcing-free proof/reversal method [Sam99], and Montalbán's earlier work on Vaught's conjecture and degree spectra [Mon13].
why_relevant: Provides general cone theorems for analytic equivalence relations without perfectly many classes. The automorphism/copy setting can be viewed through analytic equivalence relations and closed Polish group actions, making this a likely source of proof techniques.

title: Degree Spectra of Relations on a Cone
overall_summary: The paper develops the study of degree spectra of invariant relations on countable structures after relativizing to a Turing cone, with the aim of isolating model-theoretic rather than presentation-dependent behavior. Its main structural results include: Harizanov’s c.e.-degree fullness theorem becomes a clean cone dichotomy; there are nevertheless incomparable d.c.e. degree spectra on every cone; and any degree spectrum on a cone not contained in the Δ^0_2 degrees contains all 2-CEA degrees. The core techniques are movable-tuple coding via free or d-free tuples, finite-injury constructions of copies with prescribed relation degree, and a game argument that maintains coding requirements while approximating a 2-CEA set. For the original automorphism problem, the paper is useful because the graph of a transported automorphism is a binary relation on copies, and the cone-fullness/definability results give routes from failure of infinitary definability of such a relation to the existence of copies where the relation has nontrivial degree, potentially contradicting computable AUT-countability on a cone.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 1.1 and Section 2.3: degree spectrum of a relation on a cone
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let R be an invariant relation on A, so that for every copy B isomorphic to A there is a well-defined corresponding relation R^B on B. For a Turing degree d, define dgSp(A,R)_{≤d} = {deg(R^B) ⊕ d : B is a copy of A with B ≤_T d}. Equivalently, requiring B ≡_T d gives the same set, by Knight’s upward-closure theorem for degree spectra of structures. Two relations R on A and S on B have the same degree spectrum on a cone if dgSp(A,R)_{≤d} = dgSp(B,S)_{≤d} for all degrees d in some Turing cone. More generally, containment and incomparability of these spectra are interpreted on a cone, yielding a partial order of cone-degree-spectra denoted dgSp_rel(A,R). For common relativizable classes Γ, such as c.e. degrees, d.c.e. degrees, Δ^0_α degrees, or α-CEA degrees, Γ is viewed as the map d ↦ Γ(d), and one says that dgSp_rel(A,R) contains Γ if this containment holds on a cone.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The only nontrivial equivalence in the definition is replacing B ≤_T d by B ≡_T d. If B ≤_T d is a copy of A, Knight’s theorem gives a d-computable isomorphism from B to a copy C with C ≡_T d. Transporting R along this isomorphism preserves the degree of R modulo d, so deg(R^C) ⊕ d = deg(R^B) ⊕ d. For comparing two spectra, the set of degrees on which equality, proper containment one way, proper containment the other way, or incomparability holds is Turing invariant; by Borel determinacy in the applications, one of these alternatives holds on a cone. This permits the paper to talk about a single partial ordering of spectra on a cone rather than about individual presentations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.3: intrinsic definability on a cone
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and R an invariant relation on A. For an ordinal α, R is intrinsically Σ^0_α on a cone if, for all degrees d in some Turing cone and every copy B of A with B ≤_T d, the relation R^B is Σ^0_α(d). The paper observes that R is intrinsically Σ^0_α on a cone if and only if R is definable in A by a Σ^in_α formula of L_{ω_1ω}, with no computability restriction on the infinitary disjunctions/conjunctions and with finitely many parameters allowed. Analogous statements hold for Π^0_α, Δ^0_α, and other standard intrinsic definability notions after relativization to a cone.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One direction is direct after choosing a cone whose base computes the infinitary formula and the relevant parameters: evaluating a Σ^in_α formula in any d-computable copy is a Σ^0_α(d) property. For the converse, the paper invokes the standard relatively intrinsic definability theorem of Ash, Knight, Manasse, Slaman, and Chisholm, relativized high enough to compute the structure, relation, parameters, and syntactic searches. On such a cone, all effectivity conditions that appear in nonrelativized Ash-Nerode-style theorems become decidable from the base oracle, so intrinsic complexity on the cone is equivalent to infinitary definability in the underlying structure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.5: Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure and R an invariant relation on A. For a computable ordinal α, the following are equivalent: (1) R is relatively intrinsically Σ^0_α, meaning that for every copy B of A, not necessarily computable, the transported relation R^B is Σ^0_α in the atomic diagram of B; (2) R is definable in A by a computable infinitary Σ^c_α formula with finitely many parameters from A. The analogous equivalence holds for Π^0_α and hence for Δ^0_α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result rather than reproving it. The easy direction evaluates a computable infinitary Σ^c_α formula in any copy B using B as oracle, giving a Σ^0_α(B) definition. The hard direction uses the standard back-and-forth/generic-copy method: if no computable infinitary Σ^c_α definition exists, one constructs a copy in which the relation fails to be Σ^0_α relative to the copy, by forcing tuples with matching lower-level back-and-forth behavior but different R-status. This theorem is the relative analogue of Ash-Nerode and is used in the paper as the bridge between formal infinitary definitions and presentation-independent computability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2 and Corollary 2.7: Harizanov c.e.-degree fullness on a cone
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Harizanov’s theorem, in the form used by the paper, says: let A be a computable structure and R a computable relation on A that is not relatively intrinsically computable. Suppose an effectiveness condition holds: for every tuple of parameters c, one can computably find a tuple a in R such that whenever a quantifier-free formula θ(c,a,b) holds, there are a' not in R and b' with θ(c,a',b') holding. Then for every c.e. set C there is a computable copy B of A such that R^B ≡_T C. After relativizing to a cone, the effectiveness condition is absorbed into the cone base, yielding Corollary 2.7: for every structure A and invariant relation R on A, either dgSp_rel(A,R) = Δ^0_1, i.e. R has computable degree in all copies on a cone, or dgSp_rel(A,R) contains all c.e. degrees.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction underlying Harizanov’s theorem uses Ash-Nerode free tuples. If R is not relatively intrinsically computable, then over any finite tuple of parameters one can find a tuple whose current R-status can later be changed while preserving the finite atomic or existential facts already committed in the copy. To code a c.e. set C, build a computable copy B by a finite-injury construction with designated tuples b_x. Initially b_x is placed so that its R-status codes x not in C. If x enters C, the finite partial isomorphism is adjusted, using freeness, so that b_x now has the opposite R-status while preserving the finite diagram already enumerated. Injury is controlled by ordering the requirements. From R^B one can read whether each x entered C, and from C one can compute the final isomorphism, so R^B ≡_T C. On a cone, the searches for free tuples and witnesses become computable in the base oracle, so the conclusion becomes the clean dichotomy stated in Corollary 2.7.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.3 and Proposition 3.4: d-free tuples and the cone criterion for being strictly above c.e. degree
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R be an m-ary relation on a structure A. For tuples c and a from A, a is d-free over c if for every tuple b and every finitary existential formula φ(c,u,v) true of a,b, there are a',b' satisfying φ(c,u,v) such that the restriction of R to tuples from c,a' differs from the restriction of R to tuples from c,a, and such that for every finitary existential formula ψ(c,u,v) true of a',b', there are a'',b'' satisfying ψ(c,u,v) with the restriction of R to tuples from c,a'',b'' equal to the restriction of R to tuples from c,a,b. In the unary case, this says: a not in R is d-free over c if every existential neighborhood of a can be matched by some a' in R, and every later existential neighborhood of a' can be matched back by some a'' not in R while preserving the R-statuses of the auxiliary tuple b. Proposition 3.4 states: if for every tuple c there is a tuple a that is d-free over c, then the cone-degree-spectrum dgSp_rel(A,R) strictly contains the c.e. degrees. Conversely, if R is formally d.c.e. and dgSp_rel(A,R) strictly contains the c.e. degrees, then for every tuple c there must be a d-free tuple over c.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward direction is Proposition 3.9 relativized to a cone: d-free tuples let one build a copy in which R has degree not equal to any c.e. degree, while Harizanov’s cone theorem supplies all c.e. degrees once nontriviality is known. The reverse direction is Proposition 3.5 relativized: if R is formally d.c.e. and some tuple c has no d-free tuple over it, then the apparent difference of two c.e. definitions can be reorganized in every computable copy into a pair of c.e. sets whose join is Turing-equivalent to R^B, so R^B is always of c.e. degree. Thus, for formally d.c.e. relations, the existence of d-free tuples over all finite parameter tuples is exactly the obstruction to being intrinsically of c.e. degree on a cone.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.5: no d-free tuple implies intrinsically of c.e. degree for formally d.c.e. relations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure and R a formally d.c.e. relation on A, meaning R is definable by a formula α(x) ∧ not β(x) where α and β are computable infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas, with finitely many parameters allowed. Suppose there is a tuple c over which no tuple a is d-free. Assume effectively that, given a and c, one can find witnesses b and an existential formula φ(c,u,v) showing that a is not d-free over c, and further, given a',b' satisfying φ, one can find the existential formula ψ required in the negation of d-freeness. Then for every computable copy B of A, the transported relation R^B is of c.e. Turing degree.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is written for unary R, with the general case requiring only heavier notation. Fix c and incorporate it into the language. Since R is formally d.c.e., write R = α ∧ not β with α,β Σ^c_1. In a computable copy B, α defines a c.e. superset and β a c.e. correction set, but R^B itself need not compute those raw c.e. approximations. Using the failure of d-freeness, for each a not in R choose an existential formula φ_a(a,b_a) such that any attempt to move a into R through φ_a is followed by a formula ψ forcing any later return preserving the auxiliary R-pattern to put the main element back in R. Define a c.e. set P of elements d of B by a stage condition: d appears to satisfy α and not β at two stages s<t, and every relevant φ_a(d,e) seen by stage s has, by stage t, acquired the corresponding ψ-witness. Then R^B ⊆ P. Also P is c.e., and P minus R^B is c.e. because it is exactly the part of P satisfying β. The crucial point is that R^B computes P: if d is in R^B, then d is in P; if d is not in R^B, the no-d-free witness φ_a eventually appears with the auxiliary tuple’s true R-pattern, and this lets R^B recognize that d can never enter P. Hence R^B ≡_T P ⊕ (P minus R^B), a c.e. degree.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.9: d-free tuples build a copy whose relation is not of c.e. degree
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure and R a computable relation on A. Suppose that for every tuple c there is a tuple a not in R that is d-free over c, and suppose these d-free tuples and the witnesses in the definition of d-freeness can be found effectively. Then there is a computable copy B of A such that R^B is not of c.e. Turing degree.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a finite-injury priority construction, presented for unary R. Build a computable copy B through finite partial isomorphisms F_s from an initial segment of ω into A. Requirements R_{e,i,j} diagonalize against the possibility that R^B = Φ_i^{W_e} and W_e = Φ_j^{R^B}, where W_e ranges over c.e. sets. A requirement first chooses a fresh location n and maps it to a d-free a not in R over the current finite range. It waits for computations R^B[0..n] = Φ_i^{W_e}[0..n] with use u and W_e[0..u] = Φ_j^{R^B}[0..u] with use v. It then uses d-freeness to move the image of n to a' in R while preserving the finite diagram, thereby breaking the first computation. If W_e changes below u to repair the first computation, the second half of d-freeness supplies a'' not in R and auxiliary images preserving R^B below v, so W_e cannot still agree with Φ_j^{R^B} below u. Lower-priority requirements are injured when the partial isomorphism is changed. Standard finite-injury verification shows each requirement is eventually satisfied, so no c.e. set is Turing-equivalent to R^B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Examples 3.13 and 3.14; Theorem 3.12 / Theorem 1.6: incomparable d.c.e. degree spectra on every cone
