key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There are computable structures A and M with relatively intrinsically d.c.e. relations R on A and S on M such that neither R nor S is intrinsically of c.e. degree, even relative to any cone, and the degree spectra dgSp_rel(A,R) and dgSp_rel(M,S) are incomparable on every cone. The structures are explicit tree-based multi-sorted structures. In A, one sort is the tree ω^{<ω} with child relation and root; another sort supplies 0,1,or 2 witnesses attached to each tree node. Nodes 0^n have type 0, nodes σˆa have type 1 if a is odd and type 2 if a is even, and R is the set of type-1 nodes. In M, the tree sort is ordered by the tree partial order, there is an analogous type component from a second sort, and a third sort marks most odd-ending nodes; S is again the set of nodes with first type component 1. Both R and S are relatively intrinsically d.c.e. and have d-free tuples over every finite parameter tuple.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The d-free tuples are the long zero strings 0^n above all parameters: they can be moved first to an odd successor to change R-status, and then to an even successor to return while preserving the relevant finite existential information. Thus Proposition 3.9 shows that both spectra strictly contain c.e. degrees. Incomparability is proved by two priority constructions. Proposition 3.15 builds a computable copy N of M such that no computable copy B of A has S^N ≡_T R^B. Requirements diagonalize against pairs of reductions between S^N and R^{B_e}. The construction uses two nested coding locations in N; when a computation forces B_e to mimic a change, the tree geometry of A ensures that its remaining possible d-free nodes are trapped below a node that has already changed type, so a second change in N produces an irreversible disagreement. Proposition 3.17 builds conversely a computable copy B of A such that no computable copy N of M has R^B ≡_T S^N. This direction needs an infinite-injury construction with subrequirements: if diagonalization is not immediately possible, the alleged copy N must provide infinitely many nodes outside a fixed subtree with certain low types, which is impossible in M. Both arguments relativize uniformly to any degree d, giving copies below d and showing incomparability relative to every cone.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3 and Corollary 4.4: Σ^in_1 relations on (ω,<) are well-quasi-ordered and finitary existential
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix finitely many parameters c in the standard structure (ω,<). For each n, the set of n-ary relations on (ω,<) that are definable by Σ^in_1 formulas over c is a well-quasi-order under reverse inclusion: there is no infinite strictly increasing sequence under inclusion and no infinite antichain. Consequently, every Σ^in_1-definable relation on (ω,<) over finitely many parameters is equivalent in (ω,<) to a finitary existential formula and is computable in the standard copy, uniformly from such a finitary existential description and the parameters.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First ignore parameters and restrict attention to increasing n-tuples; the finitely many possible equality/order patterns are handled by a finite product. Any existential condition on an increasing tuple is equivalent to requiring lower bounds p_i on the finite gaps before and between tuple entries. Let D_p be the set of increasing tuples satisfying the gap bounds p. The map p ↦ D_p identifies reverse inclusion among these basic definable sets with the product order on ω^n, a well-quasi-order. A Σ^in_1 relation is a union of such D_p’s, and because finite powersets of well-quasi-orders are well-quasi-ordered, only finitely many maximal basic sets are needed. Parameters are handled by replacing a formula φ(c,y) by φ(x,y) without parameters: an antichain or increasing chain over c would induce one without parameters. Thus the infinitary disjunction collapses to a finite existential disjunction, giving computability in the standard presentation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 4.12, 4.13, and 4.14: α-c.e. relations on (ω,<) are finite-c.e. and of c.e. degree
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R be a relation on (ω,<). If R is intrinsically α-c.e. on a cone for any countable ordinal α, then R is computable in the standard copy and is intrinsically m-c.e. for some finite m. For any computable ordinal α, the following are equivalent for a relation R on (ω,<): R is computable and intrinsically α-c.e.; R is relatively intrinsically α-c.e.; and R is intrinsically α-c.e. on a cone. Moreover, if R is intrinsically m-c.e. for some finite m, then R is intrinsically of c.e. degree: in every computable copy B of (ω,<), R^B has c.e. Turing degree.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The collapse from α-c.e. to finite m-c.e. uses Lemma 4.3. A formal α-c.e. definition is a transfinite alternating union of increasing Σ^in_1 sets A_β and B_β. Since Σ^in_1-definable sets over fixed parameters are well-quasi-ordered under inclusion, the increasing sequences A_β and B_β can change only finitely many times; hence the α-c.e. definition reduces to a finite Boolean alternation of Σ^in_1 sets, each equivalent to a finitary existential formula. The equivalence of intrinsic, relative intrinsic, and cone-intrinsic α-c.e. uses Ash-Knight free-tuple criteria plus the same well-quasi-ordering to make the needed effectiveness conditions automatic for (ω,<). For intrinsic c.e.-degree, write R as a finite alternating difference of intrinsically Σ^0_1 sets A_1 ⊇ A_2 ⊇ ... ⊇ A_m. After ignoring a finite initial segment and parameters, the last nontrivial difference supplies two existential sets B ⊆ A that differ only by increasing one gap bound. From R^B one can recognize successor pairs in the copy: a carefully chosen tuple lands in A minus B exactly when a proposed y is the immediate successor of x. The successor relation is co-c.e. intrinsically and computes the isomorphism with the standard copy, so R^B is Turing-equivalent to a c.e. degree.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.19: a relation on (ω,<) with nonuniform or intermediate spectrum behavior
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a computable binary relation R on the standard copy of (ω,<) such that its degree spectrum on a cone strictly contains the c.e. degrees, but its degree spectrum is not uniformly equal to the Δ^0_2 degrees on a cone. Uniform equality to Δ^0_2 means that on a cone there are computable functions f,g_1,g_2 such that, from any index e for a Δ^0_2 approximation of a set C, f(e) is an index for a computable copy A_e of (ω,<) satisfying R^{A_e} = Φ_{g_1(e)}^C and C = Φ_{g_2(e)}^{R^{A_e}}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The relation R is an explicitly built directed graph on intervals [a_n,b_n), [b_n,c_n), [c_n,a_{n+1}) of ω. The intervals [a_n,b_n) and [c_n,a_{n+1}) carry matching finite cycles, there is a one-way edge connecting the starts of the cycles, and the middle interval [b_n,c_n) repeats the entire earlier pattern below a_n. This recursive self-similarity gives d-free tuples over arbitrary finite parameters, so Proposition 3.9 shows the spectrum strictly contains the c.e. degrees. To prove nonuniformity, the paper constructs, uniformly in indices e,i,j, a Δ^0_2 set C = C_{e,i,j} such that if e indexes a computable copy A of (ω,<), then either C ≠ Φ_i^{R^A} or R^A ≠ Φ_j^C. The construction of C repeatedly waits for computations in both directions, flips one bit of C to force a change in R^A below a use, and uses matching-cycle configurations in R to create links: any later repair in a farther interval forces a new element to be inserted below a fixed earlier element in A. If the reductions were correct, infinitely many elements would be forced below a single element, contradicting A ≅ (ω,<). A recursion-theorem argument then rules out any uniform functions f,g_1,g_2 realizing all Δ^0_2 degrees.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.5, 5.6, and Corollary 5.7: controlled tree approximation of 2-CEA sets
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B be c.e. and A be c.e. in and above B. Lemma 5.5 gives a computable approximation f(x,s) in {0,1} such that x in A iff f(x,s)=0 for all sufficiently large s, and x not in A iff f(x,s)=1 for infinitely many s; moreover B, and hence A, can compute uniformly whether a given stage s is already permanent for f(x,t)=0 for all t≥s. Lemma 5.6 converts this into a computable approximation σ_s in T = (ω ∪ {∞})^{<ω} with a unique true path g in [T], such that a node ρ is an initial segment of g iff ρ is an initial segment of σ_s for infinitely many s, A computes g, and the binary path v(g) obtained by replacing ∞ by 0 and finite entries by 1 is A. The approximation also counts visits: if τ has length n and s_0,s_1,... are the stages with τ ⊂ σ_s, then σ_{s_0}=τ, the next coordinates start at ∞, and finite values count how many times the approximation has switched from ∞ to a finite value. Corollary 5.7 says the same holds relative to any degree d for every set A that is 2-CEA in d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 5.5, write A = W_e^B with B_s a computable enumeration. Set f(x,s+1)=0 when x is seen in W_e^{B_s}; set it to 1 when x is not seen or when the computation witnessing x in W_e^{B_s} differs from the previous one because the B-approximation changed below the use. If the true B-computation for x in A stabilizes, f is eventually 0; if not, either x is absent infinitely often or computations change infinitely often, giving infinitely many 1s. B can decide permanence of a 0 by checking whether the finite B_s segment used in the computation is correct. Lemma 5.6 builds σ_s recursively from f by, at coordinate n, moving to ∞ when f(n,t)=0 and otherwise to a finite counter recording how often the approximation has left ∞. The construction always extends the longest previously compatible string by one new coordinate, and when it moves left of a node it never returns to that node. These properties give a unique leftmost path visited infinitely often, computable from A because A can decide permanent 0-stages.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 5.3 and Proposition 5.4: 2-free tuples and Π^0_2 nondefinability
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a computable structure C with computable relation R, a tuple a not in R is 2-free over a tuple c if for every auxiliary tuple a_1 there are a' in R and a'_1 such that c,a,a_1 ≤_1 c,a',a'_1, where ≤_1 is the first back-and-forth preorder: every finitary existential formula true of the right-hand tuple is true of the left-hand tuple. Proposition 5.4 states: if c is a tuple over which no tuple a not in R is 2-free, then the complement of R is definable by a 0'''-computable Σ^0_2 infinitary formula with parameters c. Consequently, if R is not formally Δ^0_2(0''') and, without loss of generality, R is not 0'''-computably Π^0_2-definable, then over every tuple c there is a 2-free tuple a not in R; moreover, using 0^(4) one can find such tuples.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The relation x ≤_1 y is computable by a 0''-computable Π_1 infinitary condition, because it asserts that every existential formula true of y is true of x. If no a not in R is 2-free over c, then for each a not in R there is an auxiliary a_1 such that no tuple a' in R with any a'_1 satisfies c,a,a_1 ≤_1 c,a',a'_1. Using 0''' one can choose such an a_1 uniformly. Let φ_{a,a_1}(c,x,u) be the 0''-computable Π_1 formula expressing c,a,a_1 ≤_1 c,x,u, and define ψ_a(c,x) = ∃u φ_{a,a_1}(c,x,u), a 0'''-computable Σ_2 formula true of a and false of every element of R. The disjunction over all a not in R defines the complement of R. The contrapositive supplies 2-free tuples whenever R has no formal Δ^0_2 definition at the relevant oracle level.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1 and Corollary 5.2: fullness for 2-CEA degrees
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Theorem 5.1 states: let C be a computable structure and R a computable relation on C. Suppose R is not formally Δ^0_2(0'''), i.e. not definable by both 0'''-computable Σ^0_2 and Π^0_2 infinitary formulas with finitely many parameters. Then for every degree d ≥ 0^(ω+1) and every set A that is 2-CEA in d, there is a copy D of C with D ≡_T d such that R^D ⊕ D ≡_T A. The construction actually gives D ≤_T d, and Knight’s upward-closure theorem is used to replace D by a d-degree copy. Corollary 5.2, in cone language, says: for every structure C and invariant relation R on C, either dgSp_rel(C,R) ⊆ Δ^0_2, or dgSp_rel(C,R) contains all 2-CEA degrees.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume, after possibly replacing R by its complement, that R is not Π^0_2(0''')-definable. Proposition 5.4 supplies 2-free tuples over every finite tuple. Given A 2-CEA in d, use Corollary 5.7 to obtain a d-computable approximation σ_s through T=(ω∪{∞})^{<ω} with true path g and v(g)=A. Build D by finite partial isomorphisms F_s from initial segments of ω into C, plus a finite labeling map L_s from nodes of T to positions in D. If σ ends in ∞, L(σ) is a single coding point; if σ ends in a finite number, L(σ) labels a finite tuple with a sign vector m(σ). The construction maintains: each current approximation node has a coding location; the current F_s makes locations along σ_s have the positive coding status and immediate false extensions have the negative coding status; F_s preserves the previously enumerated atomic diagram; compatible stages agree on the corresponding initial segments; and enough elements of C enter the range for surjectivity. These conditions ensure that along the true path g the limit isomorphism F is computable from A, so A computes R^D⊕D, while R^D⊕d can recover g and hence A by checking the unique sequence of coding locations with the correct R-status. The hard part is preserving future coding flexibility. At every stage the paper maintains a closed game G_s in which Player I plays finite partial isomorphisms and Player II plays both extra elements that must be added to the range and possible future approximation nodes. I loses if the coding, atomic-agreement, or extension requirements fail. A winning strategy for I says the current construction can survive all future approximation changes without adding new coding labels. The game can be reduced to a finite basic game G^b_s: Player II need only play basic lists, because loops in the approximation can be compressed; Lemmas 5.8 and 5.9 show there are finitely many basic lists and that winning the basic game is equivalent to winning the full game. At stage s+1, first use the old winning strategy to move to the new approximation. If the new node ends in ∞, add a fresh 2-free tuple b not in R as the coding point; when a future play asks for the corresponding false finite extension, 2-freeness allows b to be switched to an R-tuple while preserving earlier commitments. If the new node ends in a finite number, enumerate the finitely many basic lists b_1,...,b_m and choose a block of coding tuples with sign vector by auxiliary games H( ... ). Lemma 5.11 says that, from a winning H for the first r lists and any candidate x, one of the two sign choices for appending x remains winning. Lemma 5.12 says that if appending x works and the tuple with x is ≤_1 the tuple with y, then appending y also works. Choose x not in R 2-free over the current range, choose y in R with the required ≤_1 relation, and use these lemmas to append a tuple whose actual R-status matches the chosen sign while preserving a winning strategy. Iterating through all basic lists gives the new coding block and the new winning strategy. This completes the construction and proves that every 2-CEA degree appears in the cone spectrum unless the spectrum is already contained in Δ^0_2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem A.1 and Corollary A.2: stronger relativization of Harizanov for the alternate spectrum
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure and R a computable relation on A that is not intrinsically computable. Assume the following effectiveness condition: the existential diagram of the expanded structure (A,R) is computable, and given any finitary existential formula φ(c,x), one can decide whether it has finitely many or infinitely many solutions in A. Then for any sets X ≤_T Y with Y c.e. in X, there is an X-computable copy B of A such that R^B ≡_T Y, and Y computes an isomorphism between A and B. Corollary A.2 states: if A is any structure and R is a relation on A that is not intrinsically computable on a cone, then the alternate cone spectrum dgSp^*_rel(A,R), defined using dgSp^*(A,R)_{≤d} = {deg(R^B) : B ≅ A and B ≤_T d} without adjoining d to the relation degree, contains the c.e. degrees.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is designed to repair the usual relativization problem in Harizanov’s theorem: if one codes a c.e.-in-X set Y into R^B using an X-computable construction, then R^B⊕X can read the coding locations, but R^B alone may not know them. The appendix solves this by fixing all possible coding locations in advance and using an active-sequence code. Choose an infinite-to-one computable map g:ω→2^{<ω}∪{∅}. The copy B is organized into r-tuples b_i. At any time a unique increasing sequence k_1<k_2<... of active locations has R-status 'on' and g(k_1)≺g(k_2)≺...; the union of the strings is Y. Locations not on the active sequence are made permanently off or arranged never to have an active predecessor, so R^B alone can recover the unique active sequence and hence Y. The proof proceeds by induction on the arity of R and reduces to three assumptions: R only concerns tuples with no repeated entries; both R and its complement are thick, meaning they contain infinitely many pairwise disjoint tuples; and restrictions disjoint from finite parameter tuples remain non-intrinsically-computable. If R or its complement has a thick Σ^c_1 subset, the construction uses two kinds of tuples: free tuples, whose R-status can later be switched, and constrained tuples in the thick Σ^c_1 subset, which are forced to stay in R. When Y changes its current prefix, the old active free tuple is switched off and fresh constrained/free tuples are added until a new location with the current prefix is reached. In the remaining case, neither R nor its complement contains a thick Σ^c_1 subset. The appendix develops a thin/thick analysis: thin existential sets have bounded pairwise-disjoint subfamilies; if all tuples were captured by thin existential neighborhoods, one obtains a Scott family and intrinsic computability; hence there are tuples not in any thin existential set. Lemmas A.7 through A.13 show these tuples behave sufficiently like free tuples while preserving the R-status of later coding tuples. The same active-sequence construction then works. Since Y computes the enumeration changes, it computes the final isomorphism and hence R^B; conversely R^B computes Y from the active sequence.

proof_techniques.name: Relativize to a cone to remove effectivity hypotheses
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly replaces delicate computability assumptions on a structure, relation, or defining formula by working above a sufficiently high Turing degree. On such a cone, the base oracle computes the structure, relation, relevant infinitary syntax, and all searches for witnesses such as free tuples. This turns classical theorems with awkward Ash-Nerode-style effectiveness conditions into clean statements about infinitary definability and cone-degree-spectra.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a computably AUT-countable-on-a-cone structure, one can try to fix the cone base and treat transported automorphism graphs as relations whose spectra are forced to be trivial relative to that base. The paper’s cone framework supplies ready-made dichotomies once the relevant relation is shown not to have the appropriate infinitary definition.

proof_techniques.name: Free and d-free tuple coding
proof_techniques.description: A free tuple is a tuple whose relation status can be changed while preserving all finite existential information over a parameter tuple. A d-free tuple strengthens this by allowing a two-step move: first change the relation status, then later return to the original relation pattern while preserving the statuses of auxiliary elements. The paper uses free tuples for c.e. coding and d-free tuples to diagonalize against c.e. degrees or characterize when formally d.c.e. relations are intrinsically of c.e. degree.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the graph of a chosen automorphism is treated as a binary relation in an expansion by parameters, analogues of free or d-free tuples would witness that the graph can be made noncomputable in some copy. Absence of such tuples, by the paper’s arguments, often forces low-degree behavior and hence suggests a route to definability.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-injury construction by moving a partial isomorphism
proof_techniques.description: Many constructions build a computable copy B of a structure A by maintaining finite partial isomorphisms F_s from an initial segment of ω into A. The finite diagram of B is enumerated according to the current pullback. To satisfy a requirement, the construction changes F_s on a tail, using free/d-free tuples to preserve all already enumerated atomic facts while altering the transported relation on a selected coding tuple. Lower-priority requirements are injured but only finitely often.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the standard mechanism for turning lack of definability or movable tuples into a copy where a transported object, such as an automorphism graph, has a chosen degree. Solver agents can try to adapt the requirements to diagonalize against computations of automorphisms from the copy plus cone oracle.

proof_techniques.name: Coding by a unique active sequence of predetermined locations
proof_techniques.description: Appendix A fixes all potential coding locations in advance and uses an infinite-to-one map g:ω→2^{<ω}∪{∅}. The relation status of locations determines a unique increasing active sequence whose g-labels form longer and longer initial segments of the coded set. This lets R^B alone recover the coded set without needing the oracle used to build the copy, avoiding the usual problem that relativized Harizanov constructions give only R^B⊕d equivalences.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Computable AUT-countability on a cone concerns computability relative to the copy plus a fixed cone oracle, but some arguments may need relation degrees without adjoining the construction oracle. Active-sequence coding is a way to make a transported automorphism graph itself reveal the coded information.

proof_techniques.name: Game maintenance of future coding flexibility
proof_techniques.description: In the 2-CEA fullness theorem, the construction maintains not just finite coding conditions but a winning strategy in a closed game G_s. Player II represents all possible future approximation changes and all extra elements that may need to enter the partial isomorphism; Player I must respond with partial isomorphisms preserving atomic facts and coding conditions. A finite basic subgame suffices because approximation loops can be compressed. New coding blocks are chosen so that the winning strategy persists.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique is useful when a direct finite-injury construction cannot know future behavior of an automorphism or its graph. A solver might define a game whose winning strategies correspond to being able to keep all automorphisms computable, and then derive definability from the existence or failure of such strategies.

proof_techniques.name: Tree approximation for 2-CEA sets
proof_techniques.description: The paper represents a 2-CEA set by a computable approximation σ_s through T=(ω∪{∞})^{<ω}, with a unique true path g. The symbol ∞ represents a stable 0, while finite numbers count repeated departures from ∞. The construction’s coding locations are indexed by tree nodes; the relation status along the true path lets the final relation compute the 2-CEA set, and the set computes the true stages and hence the final isomorphism.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If attempting to show that nondefinability of automorphism graphs yields too many possible automorphism degrees, tree approximations give a controlled way to code higher-level information while preserving copy computability on a cone.

proof_techniques.name: Well-quasi-ordering of existential definitions on (ω,<)
proof_techniques.description: For relations on (ω,<), Σ^in_1 definitions collapse because existential conditions are finite gap lower bounds, and finite sets of gap bounds are well-quasi-ordered under reverse inclusion. This prevents infinite strictly increasing chains or antichains of existentially definable relations and forces transfinite α-c.e. definitions to collapse to finite alternations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although (ω,<) itself has trivial automorphism group, the method shows how strong combinatorial restrictions on existential types can force low degree spectra. Similar WQO phenomena in a candidate structure could obstruct the desired non-Σ^in_1 automorphisms.

proof_techniques.name: Thick/thin analysis of existential sets
proof_techniques.description: Appendix A classifies existentially definable sets by whether they contain infinitely many pairwise disjoint tuples. Thin sets have a bounded number of pairwise disjoint tuples; if all tuples are captured by thin existential neighborhoods, one can build a Scott family and obtain intrinsic computability. If not, tuples outside all thin existential neighborhoods serve as robust movable tuples for coding.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For automorphism graphs, one possible obstacle is that apparent nondefinability may be localized to finitely many coordinates or parameters. Thick/thin analysis gives a way to separate genuinely abundant movable configurations from finite-dimensional artifacts.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that all relations it studies are invariant relations on structures, so applying its theorems to a particular automorphism graph may require working in an expanded structure or with parameters so that the transported graph is well-defined across copies. Several open questions may matter strategically: Question 6.8 asks whether every relation has, on a cone, a maximal degree in its spectrum and whether the resulting function satisfies Martin’s conjecture; Question 6.10 asks whether degree spectra on a cone form an upper semilattice under joins; Question 6.11 asks whether the alternate spectrum dgSp^*_rel agrees above the base degree with dgSp_rel. The paper cites Ash-Nerode for intrinsic c.e. definability, Ash-Knight/Barker for higher intrinsic definability and α-c.e. relations, Harizanov for c.e.-degree spectra, and Slaman for related closure-operator fullness results.
why_relevant: Studies exactly how definability and intrinsic computability of relations behave on cones. The graph of an automorphism is a binary relation/function, so the paper’s cone-spectrum results are directly relevant to turning non-Sigma-in-1 definability into noncomputable copies or proving such a move impossible.

title: Computable Functors and Effective Interpretability
overall_summary: The paper proves that, for countable structures in computable relational languages, effective interpretability of A in B is equivalent to the existence of a computable functor from the isomorphism class of B to the isomorphism class of A. The nontrivial direction constructs, from a computable functor, a parameter-free Δ^c_1 interpretation of the target structure inside the source by coding target elements as finite pieces of source diagrams whose functorial transport is already witnessed by finite computations. The paper further proves an effective uniqueness result: the original functor and the functor reconstructed from the induced interpretation are effectively naturally isomorphic. It extends the equivalence to effective bi-interpretability, showing that two structures are effectively bi-interpretable exactly when they are computably bi-transformable by pseudo-inverse computable functors. For the original problem, the main value is the toolkit for translating between uniform computable behavior on all copies, automorphism transport, and computably infinitary Σ/Δ definability of relations or maps inside structures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Intrinsic computability versus computably infinitary definability, quoted from Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure, and let R be a relation on A^{<ω}. The relation R is uniformly relatively intrinsically computably enumerable, abbreviated u.r.i.c.e., if there is a c.e. operator W such that for every copy (B,R^B) of (A,R), the interpreted relation R^B equals W^{D(B)}, where D(B) is the atomic diagram of B. The relation R is uniformly relatively intrinsically computable, abbreviated u.r.i. computable, if there is a computable operator Ψ such that for every copy (B,R^B) of (A,R), R^B equals Ψ^{D(B)}. The paper recalls the theorem that R is u.r.i.c.e. if and only if R is definable without parameters by a computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formula; R is u.r.i. computable if and only if both R and its complement are definable without parameters by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas. For R⊆A^{<ω}, this means a computable list of Σ^c_1 formulas, one for each arity. A sequence of relations (R_i : i∈ω), with each R_i⊆A^{<ω}, is treated as a single relation on A^{<ω}×ω and is Σ^c_1-definable or u.r.i.c.e. in that uniform sense.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not reprove this characterization; it cites Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm, and Montalbán for the A^{<ω} version. Its role in the paper is foundational: whenever the authors build a relation uniformly by a c.e. or computable operator from atomic diagrams of copies, they can assert the existence of parameter-free computably infinitary Σ^c_1 or Δ^c_1 definitions, and conversely they can compute interpreted domains and relations from diagrams because their definitions are Δ^c_1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be countable structures in computable languages, assumed without loss of generality to be relational. Write Iso(A) for the category whose objects are copies of A with domain ω and whose morphisms are isomorphisms between such copies. A is effectively interpretable in B if there are parameter-free Δ^c_1-definable relations Dom^B_A⊆B^{<ω}, an equivalence relation ∼ on Dom^B_A, and relations R_i on powers of B^{<ω}, closed under ∼, such that the quotient (Dom^B_A/∼; R_0/∼,R_1/∼,...) is isomorphic to A. A computable functor from Iso(B) to Iso(A) is a functor F such that one Turing operator Φ computes the atomic diagram of F(B̂) from D(B̂), and another Turing operator Φ_* computes F(f) from D(B̂)⊕f⊕D(B̃) for every isomorphism f:B̂→B̃. The theorem states that A is effectively interpretable in B if and only if there exists a computable functor from Iso(B) to Iso(A).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward direction is Proposition 13: from a Δ^c_1 interpretation of A in B, each copy of B computes the interpreted quotient and hence a copy of A, and each isomorphism of copies of B acts coordinatewise on tuples and induces an isomorphism of the interpreted quotients. The reverse direction is Proposition 14: from a computable functor F=(Φ,Φ_*), one defines a domain of codes (finite tuples from B together with a natural number i) exactly when a finite identity-transport computation by Φ_* already fixes i; two such codes are equivalent if finite functorial transport computations map i to j and back. Relations of A are interpreted by asking for finite evidence in Φ that the corresponding atomic relation is true on equivalent representatives. Lemmas 15 through 22 verify that these definitions are Δ^c_1, that ∼ is an equivalence relation, that the interpreted relations are well-defined on equivalence classes, and that the quotient is isomorphic to A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A is effectively interpretable in B by parameter-free Δ^c_1 data Dom^B_A⊆B^{<ω}, an equivalence relation ∼, and relations R_i closed under ∼ whose quotient is isomorphic to A, then there exists a computable functor F:Iso(B)→Iso(A).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given a copy B̃ of B with domain ω, the Δ^c_1 definitions of Dom^B_A, ∼, and the R_i allow one to compute, uniformly from D(B̃), the corresponding interpreted domain Dom^{B̃}_A, equivalence relation, and quotient relations. Using a fixed enumeration of ω^{<ω}, choose uniformly a bijection τ^{B̃}:ω→Dom^{B̃}_A/∼, for instance by enumerating equivalence classes in their first occurrence order. Pull the quotient structure back along τ^{B̃} to obtain a structure F(B̃) on domain ω, which is a copy of A. If h:B̃→B̂ is an isomorphism, extend h coordinatewise to tuples in B̃^{<ω}; this maps Dom^{B̃}_A to Dom^{B̂}_A, respects ∼ and all R_i, and therefore induces an isomorphism of quotients. Define F(h)=(τ^{B̂})^{-1}∘h∘τ^{B̃}. The construction is uniform in D(B̃)⊕h⊕D(B̂), and functoriality follows immediately because coordinatewise action of isomorphisms on tuples preserves identities and compositions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose F=(Φ,Φ_*) is a computable functor from Iso(B) to Iso(A). Fix presentations of A and B on domain ω. For a finite tuple b̄ from B, let D(b̄) be the finite partial atomic diagram determined by b̄ under the paper’s fixed coding, and let λ be the identity map on ω. Define Dom^B_A to consist of pairs (b̄,i)∈B^{<ω}×ω such that the finite-oracle computation Φ_*^{D(b̄)⊕λ↾|b̄|⊕D(b̄)}(i) converges with value i. For (b̄,i),(c̄,j)∈Dom^B_A, define (b̄,i)∼(c̄,j) if there is a finite tuple d̄ of elements outside b̄ and c̄ such that, writing c̄′ for the elements of c̄ not in b̄ and b̄′ for the elements of b̄ not in c̄, and taking the finite permutation σ matching the tuple b̄ c̄′ d̄ with c̄ b̄′ d̄, the computations Φ_*^{D(b̄ c̄′ d̄)⊕σ⊕D(c̄ b̄′ d̄)}(i)=j and Φ_*^{D(c̄ b̄′ d̄)⊕σ^{-1}⊕D(b̄ c̄′ d̄)}(j)=i both converge. For each relation symbol P_m of A of arity p(m), define R_m on p(m)-tuples of Dom^B_A by putting ((b̄_1,k_1),...,(b̄_p,k_p)) in R_m if there are a finite tuple c̄ and numbers j_1,...,j_p such that each (b̄_s,k_s)∼(c̄,j_s) and the finite output Φ^{D(c̄)} already asserts P_m(j_1,...,j_p). Then Dom^B_A, ∼, and all R_m form a parameter-free Δ^c_1 effective interpretation of A in B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction uses finite pieces of the functor computations as codes for elements of the functor output. A pair (b̄,i) is admitted into the interpreted domain only when the functorial image of the finite identity map on b̄ already fixes the target element i; this ensures that i is independent of harmless extensions of b̄. Equivalence of two codes is witnessed by a finite partial isomorphism between finite diagrams of B whose functorial image maps i to j and whose inverse maps j to i. Lemma 15 shows Dom^B_A and its complement are Σ^c_1; Lemma 16 does the same for ∼ and its complement by defining a negative relation via finite computations with wrong output and using totality plus functoriality to prove exactly one of the positive and negative alternatives holds; Lemma 17 proves ∼ is an equivalence relation by composing finite witnesses and using functoriality. Relations R_m are first seen to be c.e. from the diagram; Lemma 20 defines the complementary relation Q_m by finite evidence that P_m is false and proves Q_m is exactly the complement of R_m, so each R_m is Δ^c_1. Corollary 21 gives closure of R_m under ∼. Finally, using the fixed copy B, map each i∈ω, viewed as an element of the fixed copy of A, to (B↾n,i), where n is least such that this pair lies in Dom^B_A. Lemmas 18 and 19 show this map is surjective and injective modulo ∼, and the definition of R_m shows it preserves all atomic relations. Hence the quotient is isomorphic to A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14 from a computable functor F=(Φ,Φ_*), the interpreted domain Dom^B_A={(b̄,i): Φ_*^{D(b̄)⊕λ↾|b̄|⊕D(b̄)}(i)=i} and its complement are both definable in B, without parameters, by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each finite tuple length and each i, enumerate all finite partial diagrams δ for which the finite computation Φ_*^{δ⊕λ↾k⊕δ}(i) converges with value i. Each such δ corresponds effectively to a quantifier-free formula describing the atomic facts of a tuple, and the computable disjunction of these formulas defines Dom^B_A. For the complement, enumerate finite partial diagrams δ for which the same finite-oracle computation either converges to a value different from i or demands information outside δ or outside the finite identity λ↾k. The paper uses the convention that this latter kind of finite-oracle failure is a detectable divergence from the finite oracle, not an infinite run. Because Φ_* is total on genuine full oracles D(B^f)⊕id⊕D(B^f), no actual tuple from a copy of B realizes a finite situation in which the computation remains forever undecided without asking beyond the finite oracle. Thus the complementary disjunction defines exactly the complement of Dom^B_A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14, the equivalence relation ∼ on Dom^B_A and its complement within Dom^B_A×Dom^B_A are both definable in B, without parameters, by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The relation ∼ is visibly Σ^c_1: one existentially guesses a finite tuple d̄ and checks the two finite computations by Φ_* witnessing transport of i to j and back. For the complement, define a Σ^c_1 relation ≁ by the same finite setup, but requiring that at least one of the two transport computations converge to a value different from the required one. The main point is to prove that for pairs in Dom^B_A, exactly one of ∼ and ≁ holds. To see at least one holds, extend the relevant finite tuples b̄ and c̄ to bijections f,g:ω→B that agree off a finite initial segment; then h=g^{-1}∘f is an isomorphism between the pullback copies B^f and B^g. Since Φ_* computes the total isomorphism F(h), the images of i and j are determined after finitely much oracle use, giving either a positive ∼ witness or a negative ≁ witness. To see both cannot hold, suppose finite witnesses give contradictory functorial transports. Choose full bijections extending the finite data for the positive and negative witnesses. These yield a diagram of isomorphisms among pullback copies of B. Functoriality says that the image under F of one composite is the composite of the images under F of the pieces. The domain condition for (b̄,i) and (c̄,j) supplies identity actions on the relevant coordinates, while the positive and negative finite witnesses force different values for the same functorial composite, a contradiction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 17
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14, the relation ∼ is an equivalence relation on Dom^B_A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Symmetry is built into the definition, since the witness includes both σ and σ^{-1}; reflexivity follows from the defining condition for membership in Dom^B_A, using the finite identity map. For transitivity, suppose (ā,i)∼(b̄,j) and (b̄,j)∼(c̄,k). Choose finite witnesses for both equivalences, then extend the relevant tuples to bijections f_1,f_2,g_1,g_2,h_1,h_2 from ω onto B arranged so that the finite partial isomorphisms witnessing the two equivalences and the identity behavior at b̄ occur as restrictions of full isomorphisms among pullback copies. Functoriality expresses F(h_2^{-1}∘h_1) as a composite passing through the first witness, the identity at b̄, and the second witness. Applying this composite to i gives k; applying the inverse composite to k gives i. Since these computations use only finitely much of the full oracles, a sufficiently long finite tuple supplies the required finite witness that (ā,i)∼(c̄,k).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14, if (b̄,i)∈Dom^B_A, then there is an initial segment c̄=B↾n of the fixed presentation B and some j∈ω such that (b̄,i)∼(c̄,j). Here B↾n denotes the tuple (0,1,...,n−1) in the fixed presentation of B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose n large enough that every element appearing in b̄ lies in B↾n. Choose a finite permutation σ of {0,...,n−1} sending the first |b̄| positions to the tuple b̄, and extend σ to a permutation f of ω that is the identity on all numbers at least n. The pullback B^f is isomorphic to B via f, and F(f) is computed by Φ_*. Let j=F(f)(i). Since the computation of F(f)(i)=j and the inverse computation use only finitely much oracle information, enlarge n to m if necessary so that B↾m contains all information used. The finite restriction then witnesses (b̄,i)∼(B↾m,j).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14, if (b̄,i),(c̄,j)∈Dom^B_A and b̄ is a subtuple or initial part of c̄ in the sense that the tuple c̄ extends b̄, then (b̄,i)∼(c̄,j) if and only if i=j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because (b̄,i) is in Dom^B_A, the finite identity computation Φ_*^{D(b̄)⊕λ↾|b̄|⊕D(b̄)}(i) converges to i. Any finite witness deciding whether (b̄,i) is equivalent or non-equivalent to (c̄,j) extends this same identity computation on the part corresponding to b̄. Therefore the target value forced by the computation is i. By Lemma 16, exactly one of equivalence and non-equivalence holds; hence equivalence holds precisely when j equals i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 20 and Corollary 21
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a relation symbol P of A of arity p in the construction of Proposition 14, and let R be the interpreted relation defined by finite positive evidence that P holds in Φ^{D(c̄)} on suitable ∼-equivalent representatives. Define Q analogously by requiring finite evidence that P does not hold in Φ^{D(c̄)}. Lemma 20 states that Q is exactly the complement of R inside (Dom^B_A)^p. Consequently each interpreted relation R_m is Δ^c_1-definable, uniformly in m. Corollary 21 states that if (b̄_s,i_s)∼(c̄_s,j_s) for s=1,...,p, then ((b̄_1,i_1),...,(b̄_p,i_p)) lies in R if and only if ((c̄_1,j_1),...,(c̄_p,j_p)) lies in R; that is, R is closed under ∼ coordinatewise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: To prove every tuple is in R or Q, use Lemma 18 to replace each coordinate by an equivalent code based on a sufficiently long common initial segment c̄=B↾n. The full output Φ^{D(B)} is a copy of A, so it decides whether P holds on the resulting tuple of indices. Enlarging c̄ to cover the finite use of that decision gives either a positive R witness or a negative Q witness; Lemma 19 ensures that passing to the longer initial segment does not change the indices. To prove R and Q are disjoint, suppose positive evidence is witnessed by c̄,j_1,...,j_p and negative evidence by d̄,k_1,...,k_p. By transitivity of ∼, each (c̄,j_s) is equivalent to (d̄,k_s). Extend c̄ and d̄ to bijections f,g:ω→B. Then F(g^{-1}∘f) is an isomorphism from F(B^f) to F(B^g), and the equivalences force it to send each j_s to k_s. But the finite Φ-output over c̄ makes P(j_1,...,j_p) true in F(B^f), while the finite Φ-output over d̄ makes P(k_1,...,k_p) false in F(B^g), contradicting preservation of relations by the isomorphism F(g^{-1}∘f). Closure under ∼ follows immediately from this disjointness argument and the definition of R.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 22
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14, define θ:ω→Dom^B_A by θ(i)=(c̄,i), where c̄=B↾n is the least initial segment of the fixed presentation B such that (c̄,i)∈Dom^B_A. Then θ induces an isomorphism between the fixed copy A=(ω;P^A_0,P^A_1,...) and the interpreted quotient (Dom^B_A/∼;R_0/∼,R_1/∼,...).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Injectivity modulo ∼ follows from Lemma 19: two codes based on comparable initial segments with different second coordinates cannot be ∼-equivalent. Surjectivity modulo ∼ follows from Lemma 18, which says every domain code is equivalent to one whose first coordinate is an initial segment of B; then Lemma 19 allows replacement by the least such initial segment. Preservation and reflection of each relation P_m follow directly from the definition of R_m, because the R_m relation was defined by looking for representatives over a common tuple on which Φ has already output the corresponding atomic fact. Thus θ gives the required quotient isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) be a computable functor. Apply Proposition 14 to F to obtain an effective interpretation of A in B, and then apply Proposition 13 to that interpretation to obtain a computable functor I_F:Iso(B)→Iso(A). Then F and I_F are effectively naturally isomorphic. That is, there is a computable Turing functional Λ such that for every copy B̃ of B, Λ^{B̃}:F(B̃)→I_F(B̃) is an isomorphism, and for every isomorphism h:B̃→B̂, the naturality equation I_F(h)∘Λ^{B̃}=Λ^{B̂}∘F(h) holds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each copy B̃, Proposition 14 supplies a computable map F_{B̃}:F(B̃)→Dom^{B̃}_A/∼ as in Lemma 22, sending each element i of F(B̃) to its interpreted code. Proposition 13 constructs I_F(B̃) by choosing a computable bijection τ^{B̃}:ω→Dom^{B̃}_A/∼ and pulling the quotient back to ω. Define Λ^{B̃}=(τ^{B̃})^{-1}∘F_{B̃}. This is uniformly computable from D(B̃). To verify naturality, let h:B̃→B̂ be an isomorphism and suppose F(h)(i)=j. Let F_{B̃}(i)=(ā,i) and F_{B̂}(j)=(b̄,j), after possibly extending the finite tuples so that the computation of F(h)(i)=j is already witnessed by finite oracle information. Since h sends the finite diagram of ā in B̃ to the finite diagram of h(ā) in B̂, the definition of ∼ in B̂ gives (h(ā),i)∼(b̄,j). This is exactly the assertion that the square comparing F(h), I_F(h), and the Λ maps commutes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 23
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be computable structures. Suppose A is effectively interpretable in B with interpreted domain Dom^B_A, and B is effectively interpretable in A with interpreted domain Dom^A_B. For any map q on a structure, write q̃ for the induced coordinatewise map on tuples and interpreted domains, and q̃̃ for the second iterate. Then the following are equivalent. (1) A and B are effectively bi-interpretable using the given interpretations, meaning that for some isomorphisms f^B_A:Dom^B_A→A and f^A_B:Dom^A_B→B, the compositions f^A_B∘f̃^B_A:Dom^{(Dom^B_A)}_B→B and f^B_A∘f̃^A_B:Dom^{(Dom^A_B)}_A→A are u.r.i. computable in B and A respectively. (2) There are u.r.i. computable isomorphisms g:Dom^{(Dom^A_B)}_A→A and h:Dom^{(Dom^B_A)}_B→B, together with isomorphisms α:Dom^B_A→A and β:Dom^A_B→B, such that α∘h̃∘α̃̃^{-1}=g and β∘g̃∘β̃̃^{-1}=h. (3) There are u.r.i. computable isomorphisms g and h as in (2) such that the same two displayed identities hold for every choice of isomorphisms α:Dom^B_A→A and β:Dom^A_B→B. Moreover, in (2) and (3), one may take g and h to be the u.r.i. computable composition maps arising from a bi-interpretation in (1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1) implies (2), take g=f^B_A∘f̃^A_B and h=f^A_B∘f̃^B_A, and take α=f^B_A and β=f^A_B. The displayed identities reduce to cancellations of a map with its induced inverse. For (2) implies (3), fix arbitrary α′:Dom^B_A→A and β′:Dom^A_B→B. Let δ be the automorphism of Dom^B_A with α′∘δ=α. Using h, extend δ to an automorphism γ=h∘δ̃∘h^{-1} of B. Since h is u.r.i. computable, γ fixes the graph of h, giving γ∘h∘γ̃̃^{-1}=h and hence δ∘h̃∘δ̃̃^{-1}=h̃ once one proves γ̃=δ. To prove γ̃=δ, the paper uses the u.r.i. computability of the corresponding transported version of g inside B: automorphisms preserve the graph of g, and this forces the induced actions to agree. Therefore replacing α by α′ leaves α∘h̃∘α̃̃^{-1} unchanged; the same argument with roles reversed handles β. For (3) implies (1), fix any isomorphism f^A_B:Dom^A_B→B and define f^B_A=g∘(f̃^A_B)^{-1}. Then f^B_A∘f̃^A_B=g is u.r.i. computable, and condition (3) gives f^A_B∘f̃^B_A=h, also u.r.i. computable. Hence the original interpretations form an effective bi-interpretation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be countable structures. They are effectively bi-interpretable if and only if they are computably bi-transformable. Here computably bi-transformable means that there are computable functors F:Iso(A)→Iso(B) and G:Iso(B)→Iso(A) such that G∘F is effectively naturally isomorphic to the identity functor on Iso(A), F∘G is effectively naturally isomorphic to the identity functor on Iso(B), and the two natural isomorphisms are coherent in the paper’s pseudo-inverse sense: applying F to the natural isomorphism Ã→G(F(Ã)) gives exactly the natural isomorphism F(Ã)→F(G(F(Ã))), and similarly with A and B interchanged.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume first that A and B are effectively bi-interpretable. Proposition 13 gives computable functors F:Iso(A)→Iso(B) and G:Iso(B)→Iso(A). In the construction of F, there is a uniformly computable map Ω^{Ã}:Dom^{Ã}_B→F(Ã) identifying the interpretation of B inside Ã with the produced copy F(Ã); similarly Γ^{B̃}:Dom^{B̃}_A→G(B̃). The u.r.i. computable composition map from the bi-interpretation gives a computable functional Θ^{Ã}:Ã→Dom^{(Dom^{Ã}_B)}_A. Define Λ^{Ã}_A=Γ^{F(Ã)}∘Ω̃^{Ã}∘Θ^{Ã}:Ã→G(F(Ã)). This is an isomorphism uniformly computable from Ã. Naturality follows from three identities: F(j)=Ω^{Â}∘j̃∘(Ω^{Ã})^{-1} for an isomorphism j:Ã→Â; G(F(j))=Γ^{F(Â)}∘F(j)̃∘(Γ^{F(Ã)})^{-1}; and Θ is u.r.i. computable, so j̃̃∘Θ^{Ã}=Θ^{Â}∘j. The analogous construction gives Λ_B for F∘G. Proposition 23 then supplies the coherence equations required for pseudo-inverses. Conversely, assume pseudo-inverse computable functors F and G are given. Proposition 14 gives effective interpretations in both directions, and Proposition 7 provides the comparison maps Ω and Γ between the original functors and the interpretation-induced functors. From the natural isomorphism Λ_A:Ã→G(F(Ã)), define Θ^{Ã}=(Ω̃^{Ã})^{-1}∘(Γ^{F(Ã)})^{-1}∘Λ^{Ã}_A, viewed as a map Ã→Dom^{(Dom^{Ã}_B)}_A. Naturality implies that for every isomorphism j:A→Ã, the graph of Θ^{Ã} is the image under j of the graph of Θ^A; in particular Θ^A is fixed by automorphisms and the same infinitary definition works in every copy. Since Θ is also computed uniformly by a Turing functional, Θ^A is u.r.i. computable. Build Υ for B analogously. The pseudo-inverse coherence equations imply the identities in Proposition 23(2), so the two interpretations are an effective bi-interpretation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C and D be classes of countable structures on domain ω, viewed as categories whose morphisms are permutations of ω inducing isomorphisms and which are closed under such isomorphisms. The class C is reducible via effective bi-interpretability to D if for every C∈C there is D∈D such that C and D are effectively bi-interpretable and the formulas defining the interpretations and the composition isomorphisms are uniform, independent of the particular C and D. The class C is uniformly transformally reducible to D if there is a subclass D′⊆D and computable functors F:C→D′ and G:D′→C that are pseudo-inverses. Theorem 12 states that C is reducible via effective bi-interpretability to D if and only if C is uniformly transformally reducible to D.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a uniformization of Theorem 9. Every step in the construction from interpretations to functors in Proposition 13 is uniform in the defining formulas, and every step in the construction from functors to interpretations in Proposition 14 is uniform in the Turing operators Φ and Φ_*. Likewise, the passage between bi-interpretations and pseudo-inverse functors in Theorem 9 uses only uniform functionals and uniform definitions. Therefore a classwise family of effective bi-interpretations with fixed formulas yields pseudo-inverse computable functors on the whole class, and pseudo-inverse computable functors on a class yield a uniform family of effective bi-interpretations.

proof_techniques.name: Turning an effective interpretation into a computable functor by canonical quotient enumeration
proof_techniques.description: Given a Δ^c_1 interpretation of A in B, the paper computes the interpreted domain, equivalence relation, and relations inside each copy B̃ from D(B̃). It then chooses a canonical enumeration of the quotient Dom^{B̃}_A/∼ using a fixed enumeration of ω^{<ω}, and pulls the quotient structure back to domain ω. An isomorphism h between copies of B acts coordinatewise on tuples, hence on the interpreted quotient, and after conjugating by the chosen enumerations gives the functorial image F(h).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a precise way to transfer automorphisms through an interpretation: an automorphism of a copy of B induces, computably from the diagram and the automorphism, an automorphism of the interpreted copy of A. It is a natural tool for testing whether computable AUT-countability or definability of automorphism graphs survives an interpretation.

proof_techniques.name: Recovering definable structure from finite uses of a computable functor
proof_techniques.description: The key reverse construction codes an element i of the functor output F(B^f) by a finite tuple b̄ from B when the finite identity-oracle computation already proves that i is fixed. Two codes are identified when a finite partial isomorphism between finite diagrams of B has, through Φ_*, already transported i to j and j back to i. Atomic relations are interpreted by finite evidence from Φ that the output structure satisfies the relevant atomic fact on equivalent representatives.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can organize algorithms computing copies, isomorphisms, or automorphism actions into a computable functor, this technique turns that uniform computability into Σ^c_1 or Δ^c_1 definability inside the original structure. This is directly relevant to arguments that try to derive definability of automorphisms from uniform relative computability, or to identify where non-functoriality is needed to separate those notions.

proof_techniques.name: Defining complements by wrong-output finite witnesses and excluding overlap by functoriality
proof_techniques.description: For both the equivalence relation and the interpreted atomic relations, the paper first defines a positive Σ^c_1 relation by finite successful computations. It then defines a negative Σ^c_1 relation by finite computations that give an incompatible output or finite evidence of the negated atomic fact. Totality of the functorial operators on full copies guarantees that every genuine tuple eventually receives either a positive or negative finite witness, and functoriality of F excludes simultaneous positive and negative witnesses.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a reusable pattern for upgrading c.e. definability to Δ^c_1 definability in settings involving automorphisms: define positive and negative finite computation witnesses, then use preservation under composition or group action to prove consistency. It may help in showing that certain automorphism graphs are intrinsically computable or, conversely, in locating obstructions.

proof_techniques.name: Initial-segment representatives for finite-tuple codes
proof_techniques.description: Lemmas 18 and 19 show that every finite-tuple code is equivalent to a code whose tuple is an initial segment of the fixed presentation B, and that if one code’s tuple extends another, equivalence collapses to equality of the natural-number coordinate. This supplies canonical representatives and makes the final quotient isomorphism explicit: i maps to the least initial-segment code (B↾n,i) that belongs to the domain.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When coding automorphisms or elements by finite information from a presentation, this technique can eliminate dependence on arbitrary finite tuples and replace them by canonical initial-segment data. That can be useful when comparing definability relative to parameters with computability relative to the atomic diagram.

proof_techniques.name: Effective natural isomorphism as uniqueness of the functor induced by an interpretation
proof_techniques.description: After converting a computable functor F into an interpretation and then converting the interpretation back into a functor I_F, Proposition 7 constructs a computable natural isomorphism between F and I_F. The natural isomorphism is built from the canonical map between F(B̃) and the interpreted quotient inside B̃, followed by the quotient enumeration used to define I_F(B̃). Naturality is verified using the finite equivalence witnesses from the reverse construction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This shows that the coding choices in an effective interpretation do not change automorphism actions except by computable conjugacy. For a problem about whether automorphisms are computable or Σ-definable, this gives a way to move between different effective presentations of the same interpreted content while controlling the induced automorphisms.

proof_techniques.name: Pseudo-inverse computable functors as a replacement for effective bi-interpretations
proof_techniques.description: The paper’s bi-interpretability theorem replaces syntactic back-and-forth interpretations with computable functors F and G whose composites are effectively naturally isomorphic to the identity in a coherent way. The proof constructs the natural isomorphisms from the u.r.i. computable composition maps of a bi-interpretation, and conversely reconstructs those composition maps from the natural isomorphisms of pseudo-inverse functors.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides a route to transfer structural properties through computable bi-transformations rather than directly manipulating formulas. If an example for the original problem is easier to build in an effectively bi-interpretable coding, this theorem suggests how to track automorphisms and definability through the coding.

proof_techniques.name: Using u.r.i. computability as the bridge between algorithms on copies and infinitary definitions
proof_techniques.description: Throughout the paper, relations computed uniformly from every copy’s atomic diagram are treated interchangeably with parameter-free computably infinitary Δ^c_1 definitions. This is not merely a convenience: the proof of Proposition 14 repeatedly constructs relations by finite oracle computations and then invokes the intrinsic computability/definability correspondence to obtain formulas.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks about Σ^{in}_1-definability relative to finite parameters. A natural adaptation is to expand the language by constants for the chosen parameters and then ask whether the relevant automorphism graph becomes u.r.i.c.e. or u.r.i. computable in the expanded structure; this paper supplies the algorithm-to-definition mechanism in the parameter-free case.

proof_techniques.name: Uniformizing interpretations and functors over classes
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 12 observes that all constructions in Theorem 9 are uniform, so a classwise effective bi-interpretation with fixed formulas is equivalent to pseudo-inverse computable functors between the corresponding categories of structures. This allows reductions not just between individual structures but between entire classes, preserving the same effective interpretation scheme.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate structure is obtained by coding another structure into graphs, orders, lattices, fields, or another universal class, this uniform version indicates how to keep the coding effective and functorial across all copies. That can matter for cone-style hypotheses, where behavior must be controlled uniformly over presentations.

proof_techniques.name: Relationalization and finite-tuple domains
proof_techniques.description: The paper assumes all languages are computable and says that, without loss of generality, they may be taken relational. It also allows interpreted domains to be subsets of B^{<ω}, not just B^n for fixed n, and notes that finite objects and even relations on B^{<ω}×ω can be effectively coded by relations on B^{<ω}. This gives enough room to represent arbitrary finite computational data inside the structure.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem allows constants, functions, and relations. This paper’s setup suggests first replacing functions by their graph relations and using finite-tuple domains to encode auxiliary computational data, so that the effective-interpretability machinery can be applied without changing the essential automorphism behavior.

other_useful_info: The introduction states that effective interpretability is equivalent to the parameterless version of Ershov-style Σ-definability over HF(B), with a reference to Montalbán [Mon12, Section 4]. A footnote explains that requiring the interpreted domain to be Δ^c_1 rather than merely Σ^c_1 is no loss: if a Σ-domain is given by formulas ∃s̄ φ_i(x̄,s̄), replace the domain by tuples (x̄,s̄,i) satisfying φ_i and adjust the equivalence relation to identify tuples whose x̄-components were equivalent in the original interpretation. The introduction also cites [Mon, Lemma 5.3] for the fact that effectively bi-interpretable structures share many computability-theoretic invariants: degree spectrum, computable dimension, Scott rank, index set up to Turing equivalence for infinite structures, computable categoricity, rigidity, the c.e. extendability condition, relational degree spectra, and effective bi-interpretability of jumps. The paper notes that undirected graphs, partial orderings, and lattices are universal for effective bi-interpretability using constructions from Hirschfeldt-Khoussainov-Shore-Slinko; with finitely many constants added, integral domains, commutative semigroups, and 2-step nilpotent groups are also universal; and fields are universal by work of Miller, Park, Poonen, Schoutens, and Shlapentokh. All of these are only reported as background or consequences, not proved in detail in this paper.
why_relevant: Equates computable functorial reductions with effective interpretations. These tools help transfer computability of automorphisms, definability of relations, and cone behavior between coded structures.

title: A Computable Functor from Graphs to Fields
overall_summary: The paper constructs a fully faithful functor F from the category of symmetric irreflexive graphs with induced-subgraph embeddings to the category of fields with field homomorphisms. The construction codes graph vertices and edges into function fields of carefully chosen high-genus algebraic curves whose rational-map rigidity lets one recover the graph, and in particular its automorphism group, from the field. For countable structures with domain ω, the authors refine the construction to a type-2 computable equivalence between graphs on ω and the essential image of the functor among fields on ω, including computable transformations of both objects and morphisms. This is directly useful for transferring graph-based computable-model-theoretic constructions to fields, especially constructions involving automorphisms, automorphism spectra, relative computability of automorphisms, and definability/interpretability phenomena.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.1 and Definition 3.4: computable functors and computable equivalence of categories
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C and C′ be categories whose objects are first-order structures with domain ω in computable languages, and whose morphisms are maps between these domains. A functor F:C→C′ is computable if there are Turing functionals Φ and Ψ such that, for every object S of C, Φ with oracle the atomic diagram of S computes the atomic diagram of F(S), and, for every morphism g:S→T in C, Ψ with oracle S⊕g⊕T computes the morphism F(g):F(S)→F(T). If F1,F2:C→C′ are computable functors, a computable natural transformation τ:F1→F2 is one for which a Turing functional computes τ(S):F1(S)→F2(S) uniformly from S. A computable equivalence of categories between C and C′ consists of computable functors F:C→C′ and G:C′→C together with computable isomorphisms of functors G∘F≅1_C and F∘G≅1_C′.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are definitions, not theorems. The important technical choice is that morphism computation is type-2: the oracle includes the source structure, the morphism as a function on ω, and the target structure. This is used later because the image of a field homomorphism, for example, can be computed only with access to the field operations and the relevant presentations of source and target.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Appendix A: algebraic-geometry facts used in the coding
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k be a field, V and W varieties over k, and W integral. Lemma A.1 says V(k(W)) is naturally in bijection with rational maps W⇢V. Let k have characteristic 0, and let C and D be geometrically integral curves over k with equal genus g_C=g_D>1. Lemma A.2 says every nonconstant rational map C⇢D is birational. Let V1,...,Vn be geometrically integral k-varieties over a characteristic-0 field k, and let C be a geometrically integral k-curve of genus greater than 1. Lemma A.3 says every rational map V1×...×Vn⇢C factors through one of the projections V1×...×Vn→Vi.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma A.1, a k(W)-point of V is given by finitely many rational functions on W satisfying the equations of V, hence is regular on some dense open subset of W; conversely a rational map gives such a point. Lemma A.2 is the standard Hurwitz formula argument: a nonconstant map between smooth projective models of curves of the same genus greater than 1 must have degree 1, hence is birational. Lemma A.3 is proved by induction on n, reducing to n=2 and base-changing to an algebraically closed field. A rational map V1×V2⇢C is viewed as an algebraic family, parametrized by V2, of rational maps V1⇢C. By the de Franchis-Severi theorem, there are only finitely many nonconstant rational maps V1⇢C, so such maps cannot vary in an algebraic family. Therefore either the family is constantly one nonconstant map, so the original map factors through V1, or all maps in the family are constant, so the original map factors through V2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1: rigid curves X and Y_t with controlled rational points
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define p(u,v)=u^4+16uv^3+10v^4+16v−4 in Q[u,v] and q(T,x,y)=x^4+y^4+1+T(x^4+xy^3+y+1) in Q[T,x,y]. Let X be the affine plane curve p(u,v)=0 over Q. For a field F and t in F, let Y_t be the affine plane curve q(t,x,y)=0 over F; let Y denote Y_T over Q(T). Then: (1) X and Y are geometrically integral; (2) g_X=g_Y>1, in fact both have genus 3; (3) after any base-field extension, neither X nor Y has a nontrivial birational automorphism; (4) after any base-field extension, Y is not birational to any curve definable over a finite extension of Q; (5) X(Q)=∅; (6) the u-coordinate map X(R)→R is surjective; (7) there is an open neighborhood U of 0 in R such that Y_t(R)=∅ for every t in U; and (8) if t∈[20,∞), then Y_t has a real point whose x-coordinate lies in [1,2].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The projective closure of X reduces modulo 5 to u^4+uv^3+vw^3+w^4=0 over F_5. The projective closure of Y specializes at T=0 to x^4+y^4+z^4=0 and at T=∞ to x^4+xy^3+yz^3+z^4=0. A result of Poonen on hypersurfaces without extra automorphisms implies that the relevant reductions are smooth, projective, geometrically integral plane quartics of genus 3 with no nontrivial birational automorphisms after base extension; this gives geometric integrality, genus 3, and rigidity for X and Y. For (4), if Y were birational to a curve over a finite extension of Q, then the specializations at T=0 and T=∞ would become isomorphic over Q-bar; but the T=0 curve has a nontrivial automorphism (x,y,z)↦(−x,y,z), while the T=∞ curve is rigid, contradiction. For (5), the projective model of X modulo 8 is u^4+2v^4+4w^4=0, which has no point in P^2(Z/8Z), so X(Q)=∅. For (6), fix u∈R. Since p(u,v)→+∞ as v→+∞, it suffices to find v with p(u,v)<0. If |u|<√2, v=0 works; if |u|≥√2, v=−u gives p(u,−u)=−5u^4−16u−4<0. For (7), the compact real projective curve at T=0 has no real points, so nearby fibers have no real points. For (8), for t≥20 and x∈[1,2], one has q(t,x,−3)<0 and q(t,x,0)>0, so the intermediate value theorem gives y with q(t,x,y)=0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Construction 5.A: the field F(G) attached to a graph G
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a graph G=(V,E), let K=Q(∏_{i∈V} X), interpreted as the direct limit of function fields over finite products of copies of X. Let u_i,v_i∈K be the rational functions u,v on the i-th copy of X. For each unordered pair {i,j}⊆V, define a K-curve Z_{i,j} by Z_{i,j}=Y_{u_i u_j} if {i,j} is an edge of G, and Z_{i,j}=Y_{u_i+u_j} if {i,j} is not an edge. Define F(G)=K(∏_{ {i,j}∈(V choose 2)} Z_{i,j}), again as a direct limit over finite products. A graph morphism G→G′, i.e. an injection preserving and reflecting adjacency, sends each u_i,v_i and each edge/nonedge coding curve to the corresponding objects for G′, inducing a field homomorphism F(G)→F(G′). This defines a functor F from graphs to fields. If G is finite then F(G) is countable; if G is infinite then |F(G)|=|G|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction first adds one independent copy of X for each vertex, so the vertex i is represented by the point (u_i,v_i). Then for each pair {i,j}, it adds one function-field point on exactly one of two possible curves: Y_{u_i u_j} encodes adjacency, and Y_{u_i+u_j} encodes non-adjacency. A graph morphism preserves the edge/nonedge dichotomy, so the same rational functions and curve equations are respected after applying the induced map on vertex indices. The cardinality assertion follows because adjoining function fields over finitely or countably many curves keeps a finite graph construction countable, while an infinite graph contributes one transcendence generator pair per vertex and no larger cardinality than the vertex set.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2: no unwanted rational maps among the coding curves
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a graph G=(V,E) and let K=Q(∏_{i∈V}X) as in Construction 5.A. For any field extension L of K, consider over L the base changes of X and all curves Y_{u_i u_j} and Y_{u_i+u_j} for unordered pairs {i,j}⊆V. The only nonconstant rational maps between any two curves in this list are the identity maps from a curve to itself.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: All these curves have the same genus greater than 1. By Lemma A.2, any nonconstant rational map between two of them would have to be birational. Lemma 4.1 gives that X and Y have no nontrivial birational automorphisms, so a nonconstant self-map is the identity. Lemma 4.1(4) rules out birational equivalence between X and a Y_t with t transcendental. If t and t′ are algebraically independent over Q and Y_t became birational to Y_t′ after base extension, one could specialize t′ to an algebraic value while keeping t transcendental, contradicting Lemma 4.1(4). The parameters u_i u_j and u_i+u_j occurring in the construction are distinct and have the needed algebraic independence properties, so no two distinct curves in the list become birational.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3: field points of X and Y_t recover exactly the graph data
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a graph and F(G) the field of Construction 5.A. For each pair {i,j}, let x_{ij},y_{ij} be the rational functions x,y on the curve Z_{i,j} adjoined in the construction. Then: (i) X(F(G)) consists exactly of the points (u_i,v_i) for i∈V. (ii) If {i,j}∈E, then Y_{u_i u_j}(F(G))={(x_{ij},y_{ij})} and Y_{u_i+u_j}(F(G))=∅. (iii) If {i,j}∉E, then Y_{u_i u_j}(F(G))=∅ and Y_{u_i+u_j}(F(G))={(x_{ij},y_{ij})}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because F(G) is the direct limit of fields K(Z) for finite products Z of the pair-coding curves, an F(G)-point of X corresponds by Lemma A.1 to a rational map from such a finite product Z to X_K. Lemmas A.3 and 5.2 force every such rational map to be constant, so X(F(G))=X(K). Now a K-point of X corresponds to a rational map from a finite product of vertex copies of X to X. Lemmas A.3 and 5.2 imply that such a map is either a projection to one of the factors or constant. A constant map would yield a Q-point of X, impossible by Lemma 4.1(5), so the only points are the coordinate points (u_i,v_i). For the Y-statements, the same rational-map analysis shows that a point on the matching curve arises only from the identity map on the corresponding adjoined Z_{i,j}, giving the single point (x_{ij},y_{ij}), while the nonmatching curve gets no such identity contribution. It remains to exclude K-rational points on either Y_{u_i u_j} or Y_{u_i+u_j}; using Lemma 4.1(6), embed K into R so that all u_i are algebraically independent real numbers sufficiently close to 0, and then Lemma 4.1(7) gives no real points on those Y_t, hence no K-points.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.1: recovering a graph from its coded field
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be the essential image of F in the category of fields. For F in E, define a graph G(F) as follows. Its vertex set is X(F). If i=(u_i,v_i) and j=(u_j,v_j) are two distinct vertices, put an edge between i and j exactly when Y_{u_i u_j}(F) has an F-point; Lemma 5.3 ensures that exactly one of Y_{u_i u_j}(F) and Y_{u_i+u_j}(F) has an F-point. For every graph G, the map ε_G:G→G(F(G)) sending i to (u_i,v_i) is a graph isomorphism, functorially in G. Thus G∘F≅1_Graphs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 5.3(i) says that the vertices X(F(G)) are precisely the points (u_i,v_i), so ε_G is a bijection on vertices. Lemma 5.3(ii)-(iii) says that for a pair {i,j}, Y_{u_i u_j} has a point in F(G) exactly in the edge case, while Y_{u_i+u_j} has a point exactly in the nonedge case. Therefore ε_G preserves and reflects adjacency. Functoriality is immediate because graph morphisms send each vertex generator to the corresponding vertex generator in the target.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.2: the inverse graph functor is faithful on the essential image
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be the essential image of F. For fields F,F′ in E, the map Hom_E(F,F′)→Hom_Graphs(G(F),G(F′)) induced by the graph-recovery functor G is injective. Equivalently, a field homomorphism f:F→F′ in E is uniquely determined by its induced map X(F)→X(F′) on recovered graph vertices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After replacing F by an isomorphic field of the form F(G), the field F is generated by the vertex coordinates u_i,v_i and the pair-coding coordinates x_{ij},y_{ij}. The induced graph map G(f) gives f(u_i),f(v_i) for every vertex i. For an edge {i,j}, f must send (x_{ij},y_{ij})∈Y_{u_i u_j}(F) to an F′-point of Y_{f(u_i)f(u_j)}; by Lemma 5.3, that target point is unique. For a nonedge, the same argument uses Y_{u_i+u_j}. Therefore f is determined on all generators and hence on all of F.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1: a fully faithful functor from graphs to fields
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Graphs be the category of symmetric irreflexive graphs with morphisms the injections that are isomorphisms onto induced subgraphs, and let Fields be the category of fields with field homomorphisms. The functor F:Graphs→Fields of Construction 5.A is fully faithful. Thus for any graphs G and H, the induced map Hom_Graphs(G,H)→Hom_Fields(F(G),F(H)) is a bijection. In particular, Aut(F(G)) is naturally isomorphic to Aut(G).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 6.1 gives a functor G on the essential image of F with G∘F≅1_Graphs. Proposition 6.2 says G is faithful. To see fullness, let h:F(G)→F(H) be a field homomorphism. Applying G gives a graph morphism G(h):G(F(G))→G(F(H)), and using ε_G and ε_H from Proposition 6.1 this corresponds to a graph morphism G→H. The field homomorphism induced by this graph morphism has the same image under G as h, so Proposition 6.2 forces it to equal h. Faithfulness of F follows similarly from G∘F≅1. The automorphism-group statement is the special case G=H.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 7 construction: standard presentations F_ω(G) on domain ω
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For graphs G=(ω,E) with domain ω, the paper modifies F(G) to a field F_ω(G) whose domain is exactly ω. It partitions ω into computable infinite columns ω[n] using a standard pairing function. The first column is made into a computable copy of Q. Even-numbered columns are used uniformly to adjoin the vertex copies of X, producing generators u_i,v_i for each vertex i. Odd-numbered columns are used, in lexicographic order of pairs (i,j) with i>j, to adjoin the pair-coding curve Y_{u_i u_j} if {i,j} is an edge and Y_{u_i+u_j} if {i,j} is a nonedge. The resulting union field is F_ω(G), and the action on morphisms is the same as for F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The underlying effective algebraic step is that, given a field k with domain a subset of ω and an irreducible polynomial f(x,y)∈k[x,y] of positive y-degree d, the function field k(x)[y]/(f) can be presented uniformly on a disjoint new copy of ω as k(x)⊕k(x)y⊕...⊕k(x)y^{d−1}. Iterating this construction over the computable column partition gives a uniform presentation on all of ω. The construction is arranged so that the pairing-code positions of the vertex points (u_i,v_i) increase with i, which is later used to identify the recovered vertex enumeration.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.1: computable vertex embedding into F_ω(G)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each graph G∈Graphs_ω, let μ:G→F_ω(G) be the injection sending a vertex i to the field element u_i adjoined for that vertex. Then μ is computable uniformly, its range μ(G) is a computable subset of the domain F_ω(G)=ω, and μ^{-1} on μ(G) is computable. Moreover μ varies functorially with G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The columns and the elements u_i are chosen by a uniform construction independent of the edge relation of G. Given a field-domain element j∈ω, one can determine effectively which column contains j. Only the vertex-adjoining columns can contain a u_i. On such a column, the construction lets one compute u_i and check whether j=u_i; if yes, i is recovered effectively. Since graph morphisms send vertex i to vertex g(i), the corresponding field morphisms send u_i to u_{g(i)}, giving functoriality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 7.2 and 7.3: uniform enumeration of recovered vertices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For F in the essential image E_ω of F_ω among fields with domain ω, view X(F) as a subset of F×F=ω×ω and order it by the standard pairing function π:ω×ω→ω. Lemma 7.2 says that X(F) is infinite countable and there is an order-preserving bijection δ_F:ω→X(F) such that δ_F and δ_F^{-1} are computable uniformly from the atomic diagram of F.
