Lemma 7.3 says that if F=F_ω(G) is the canonical field built from a graph G, then the natural vertex map ε_G:i↦(u_i,v_i) is order-preserving, hence ε_G=δ_{F_ω(G)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If F∈E_ω, then F≅F_ω(G) for some graph G, and Lemma 5.3 gives X(F_ω(G)) in bijection with the vertices of G, so X(F) is countably infinite. Given the field operations of F, one can enumerate pairs (a,b)∈ω×ω in increasing π-order and test p(a,b)=0, thereby enumerating X(F); the i-th discovered point is δ_F(i). This gives δ_F and its inverse relative to F. In the canonical construction, the vertex generators were deliberately placed so that π(u_i,v_i) increases with i, so ε_G is the same order-preserving enumeration.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.6: computing a field morphism from the recovered graph morphism
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a Turing functional which, given fields F,F′ in E_ω and a graph morphism g:G_ω(F)→G_ω(F′), computes the unique field morphism f:F→F′ such that G_ω(f)=g.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence and uniqueness follow from full faithfulness. To compute f, first use δ_F and δ_F′ to compute the vertex coordinates (u_i,v_i)=δ_F(i) in F and their images δ_F′(g(i)) in F′. For each pair {i,j}, determine from G_ω(F) whether it is an edge. If it is an edge, the pair-coding point (x_{ij},y_{ij}) lies on Y_{u_i u_j}; if it is a nonedge, it lies on Y_{u_i+u_j}. Its image under f must be the unique point on the corresponding target curve with parameters f(u_i),f(u_j), and this point can be found by searching F′ because Lemma 5.3 guarantees uniqueness and existence. Finally, for an arbitrary z∈F, search for finitely many generators among the u_i,v_i,x_{ij},y_{ij} and a rational function expression for z in those generators; such an expression exists because these elements generate F. Evaluate the same rational function at the computed images to obtain f(z).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2: computable equivalence between graphs on ω and the essential image in fields
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Graphs_ω be the category of symmetric irreflexive graphs with domain ω and induced-subgraph embeddings, let Fields_ω be the category of fields with domain ω and field homomorphisms, and let E_ω be the full subcategory of Fields_ω consisting of fields isomorphic to F_ω(G) for some G∈Graphs_ω. Then F_ω:Graphs_ω→E_ω and the recovered-graph functor G_ω:E_ω→Graphs_ω are computable functors. Moreover G_ω∘F_ω is exactly the identity functor on Graphs_ω, and F_ω∘G_ω is computably isomorphic to the identity functor on E_ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For G∈Graphs_ω, the column-by-column construction computes the atomic diagram of F_ω(G) from the edge relation of G, giving the object part of F_ω. For F∈E_ω, compute δ_F by Lemma 7.2. To decide whether {i,j} is an edge of G_ω(F), compute δ_F(i)=(u_i,v_i) and δ_F(j)=(u_j,v_j), then search in parallel for an F-point on Y_{u_i u_j} and on Y_{u_i+u_j}; Lemma 5.3 ensures exactly one search succeeds, and which one succeeds determines edge versus nonedge. This computes the object part of G_ω. A field morphism f:F→F′ is sent by G_ω to δ_F′^{-1}∘f∘δ_F, computable from F⊕f⊕F′. Lemma 7.3 gives G_ω(F_ω(G))=G on objects, and the same functorial identification gives equality on morphisms. For the morphism part of F_ω, given computable access to graphs G,G′ and a graph morphism g:G→G′, use Proposition 7.6 with the fact that G_ω(F_ω(g))=g to compute F_ω(g). Finally, for F∈E_ω, Proposition 7.6 applied to the identity graph isomorphism G_ω(F_ω(G_ω(F)))=G_ω(F) computes mutually inverse field isomorphisms F_ω(G_ω(F))↔F, uniformly in F; this is the computable natural isomorphism F_ω∘G_ω≅1_Eω.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 1.3 and 1.4: computing a graph presentation from any field copy in the image
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a field F∈Fields_ω is isomorphic to a field in the image of F_ω, then from the atomic diagram of F one can compute a graph G∈Graphs_ω and an isomorphism F→F_ω(G). In particular, every computable field isomorphic to a field in the image of F_ω is computably isomorphic to F_ω(G) for some computable graph G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take G=G_ω(F), which is computable from F by Theorem 1.2. The computable natural isomorphism F_ω∘G_ω≅1_Eω gives, uniformly from F, an isomorphism between F and F_ω(G) in one direction and its inverse in the other. If F is computable, then G_ω(F) is computable and the isomorphism is computable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.5: degrees of graph presentations corresponding to a fixed field in the image
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F∈E_ω, and let I={G∈Graphs_ω : F_ω(G)≅F}. Then: (a) I is exactly one graph isomorphism class in Graphs_ω. (b) Either every graph in I is automorphically trivial and computable, or there exists a graph in I whose Turing degree is equal to the Turing degree of F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), Theorem 1.2 gives a fully faithful equivalence between Graphs_ω and E_ω, so isomorphism of the fields F_ω(G) and F_ω(H) is equivalent to isomorphism of G and H. For (b), Corollary 1.3 gives some G∈I with G≤_T F. If G is automorphically trivial, then every graph in its isomorphism class is automorphically trivial; for such graphs, a finite set S_0 controls all structure, so whether {i,j} is an edge is determined by finitely many equalities involving S_0, and the graph is computable. If G is not automorphically trivial, Knight's theorem says the spectrum of G is upward closed under Turing reducibility. Since deg(G)≤deg(F), the degree deg(F) is in the spectrum of G, so there is an isomorphic copy G′∈I with deg(G′)=deg(F).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 9.1: Turing degrees of objects and morphisms are preserved
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every graph G∈Graphs_ω, the atomic diagram of G and the atomic diagram of F_ω(G) are Turing equivalent: G≡_T F_ω(G). If g:G→G′ is a morphism between computable graphs in Graphs_ω, then g and the induced field homomorphism F_ω(g):F_ω(G)→F_ω(G′) are Turing equivalent: g≡_T F_ω(g).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since F_ω is a computable functor, F_ω(G) is computable from G. Since G_ω is computable and G_ω(F_ω(G))=G, the graph G is computable from F_ω(G). This proves object-degree equivalence. If G and G′ are computable, then F_ω(g) is computable from g by the morphism part of the computable functor F_ω. Conversely, applying the computable functor G_ω to F_ω(g) recovers g because G_ω∘F_ω is the identity, so g is computable from F_ω(g).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.2: Turing degree spectra of graphs and their coded fields
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For G∈Graphs_ω, let Spec(M) denote the set of Turing degrees of presentations on domain ω isomorphic to M. If G is automorphically trivial, then Spec(G)={0}, while Spec(F_ω(G)) contains all Turing degrees. If G is not automorphically trivial, then Spec(G)=Spec(F_ω(G)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If G is automorphically trivial, every presentation of G is computable, so Spec(G)={0}. The field F_ω(G) is computable but is not automorphically trivial; Knight's upward-closure theorem for non-automorphically-trivial structures then implies that the spectrum of F_ω(G) contains every Turing degree. If G is not automorphically trivial, Proposition 9.1 applied to every copy G′≅G gives Spec(G)⊆Spec(F_ω(G)). Conversely, Proposition 1.5(b) applied to a field copy F≅F_ω(G) gives a graph copy in the same isomorphism class with the same degree as F, yielding Spec(F_ω(G))⊆Spec(G).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.7: degree spectra of relations transfer to fields
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure that is not automorphically trivial, and let R be an n-ary relation on A, not necessarily in the language. The degree spectrum DgSp_A(R) is the set of Turing degrees of images of R under isomorphisms from A to computable copies of A. Then there is a computable field F and an n-ary relation S on F such that DgSp_A(R)=DgSp_F(S).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By the graph-completeness theorem of Hirschfeldt, Khoussainov, Shore, and Slinko, replace A by a computable graph G with a relation R having the same relation-degree spectrum. Let F=F_ω(G), and let S=μ(R), applying the computable vertex embedding μ from Lemma 7.1 coordinatewise. If g:G→G′ is an isomorphism to a computable graph, then functoriality gives a commutative diagram with μ on the horizontal arrows and F_ω(g) on fields. The image R′ of R in G′ and the image S′ of S in F_ω(G′) are Turing equivalent because μ and μ^{-1} on its range are computable. Hence every degree arising from R arises from S. Conversely, if f:F→F′ is an isomorphism to a computable field copy, Corollary 1.4 supplies a computable graph G′ and a computable isomorphism F′→F_ω(G′); composing with this computable isomorphism does not change the degree of the image relation. By full faithfulness, the resulting field isomorphism is F_ω(g) for some graph isomorphism g:G→G′. The first half of the argument then shows that the degree of the image of S is the degree of the corresponding image of R.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.9: automorphism spectra transfer to fields
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every computable structure A, there is a computable field F with the same automorphism spectrum as A, where the automorphism spectrum is the set of Turing degrees of nontrivial automorphisms of the structure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes the result of Harizanov, Miller, and Morozov, using the graph-coding theorem of Hirschfeldt, Khoussainov, Shore, and Slinko, that every automorphism spectrum is realized by a computable graph G. Then take F=F_ω(G). Full faithfulness of F_ω gives a bijection between automorphisms of G and automorphisms of F, and Proposition 9.1(b) says each automorphism and its image under F_ω have the same Turing degree when the underlying graph presentations are computable. Nontriviality is preserved by the faithful functor, so the automorphism spectra are equal.

proof_techniques.name: Rigid high-genus curve coding of graph structure into fields
proof_techniques.description: The construction represents each graph vertex by a point (u_i,v_i) on a copy of a carefully selected genus-3 curve X, and each pair of vertices by adjoining a point on one of two curves Y_t: the parameter t=u_i u_j codes an edge, while t=u_i+u_j codes a nonedge. The curves are chosen so that there are no unwanted rational maps among X and the various Y_t curves. This rigidity ensures that the only X-points in the resulting field are the intended vertex points and that exactly one of the two Y-curves for a pair has a field point.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate example for the original problem is built as a graph, this technique gives a field with the same automorphism group and tightly controlled automorphism correspondence. The edge/nonedge dichotomy by existential solvability of curves also gives a concrete interpretation of the graph inside the field.

proof_techniques.name: Full faithfulness as automorphism-group preservation
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves not merely that graph isomorphism types are encoded into fields, but that every field homomorphism between coded fields comes from a unique graph morphism. For automorphisms, this yields a natural group isomorphism Aut(G)≅Aut(F(G)), and the proof identifies automorphisms through their action on the definable set X(F(G)) of vertex points.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Properties quantified over automorphisms can be transferred through this functor if the relevant computability or definability notions are preserved by the interpretation. A nondefinable automorphism of a graph candidate should correspond to a unique automorphism of the coded field, with no extra field automorphisms to check.

proof_techniques.name: Type-2 computable functoriality for both objects and morphisms
proof_techniques.description: The authors formalize computability of a functor by requiring uniform Turing functionals for transforming atomic diagrams of objects and for transforming morphisms when given the source, morphism, and target as oracles. This is stronger than merely mapping computable structures to computable structures: it gives uniform control over noncomputable copies and over individual isomorphisms or automorphisms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem involves relative computability of automorphisms in arbitrary presentations, including on a cone. A type-2 computable equivalence is the right tool for transferring such relative automorphism-computability statements between graphs and fields or other coded classes.

proof_techniques.name: Computable inverse via enumerating a definable vertex set
proof_techniques.description: For a field F in the essential image, the inverse functor G_ω computes the graph by enumerating X(F) in the order induced by the standard pairing function on F×F. To decide adjacency between two recovered vertices, it searches in parallel for a field point on Y_{u_i u_j} and on Y_{u_i+u_j}; the rigidity lemma guarantees exactly one search terminates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a uniform way to pull an arbitrary field copy back to a graph copy. For on-a-cone arguments, one can analyze automorphisms or parameter tuples in the recovered graph and then push results back to the field.

proof_techniques.name: Unique-point forcing for computing morphisms
proof_techniques.description: Once a graph morphism between recovered graphs is known, the corresponding field morphism is computable because the images of vertex generators determine the parameters of each pair-coding curve, and Lemma 5.3 gives a unique target point on the appropriate curve. Arbitrary field elements are handled by searching for rational expressions in the generators and evaluating those expressions at the computed images.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique proves equality of Turing degrees for automorphisms under the coding, not just reducibility in one direction. It is especially relevant for showing that if every graph automorphism is computable relative to a presentation plus a cone oracle, then the corresponding field automorphism is too.

proof_techniques.name: Column-based standard presentations on ω
proof_techniques.description: The field F_ω(G) is built on a computable partition of ω into columns, with Q on the first column, vertex-function-field extensions on even columns, and pair-coding extensions on odd columns. This makes the presentation uniform and provides computable handles such as the map i↦u_i and its computable inverse on its range.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When constructing a computable presentation intended to have delicate automorphism properties, this method separates coding regions and gives computable decoding maps. Such computable handles are useful when comparing finite parameter tuples in the original structure and its coded version.

proof_techniques.name: Relation-spectrum transfer using a functorial computable embedding of vertices
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 9.7 transfers degree spectra of arbitrary relations by mapping a graph relation R to μ(R) inside F_ω(G), where μ sends vertices to their corresponding field elements u_i. Functoriality ensures that images under graph isomorphisms correspond to images under field isomorphisms, and computability of μ and μ^{-1} on its range preserves Turing degrees.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The graph of an automorphism can be treated as a binary relation when studying its degree behavior under copies. This suggests a way to transfer not only automorphism groups but also complexity information about particular automorphisms.

proof_techniques.name: Effective interpretation viewpoint
proof_techniques.description: The paper notes that the graph G is effectively interpretable in F(G): the interpreted domain is the existentially definable set X(F(G)), and the edge relation is existentially definable by the existence of a point on Y_{u_i u_j}. It also notes that F(G) is effectively interpretable in G, less directly, and cites later work showing that computable functors correspond to uniform effective interpretability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem involves Σ^in_1-definability of automorphisms from finite parameters. Effective bi-interpretability is a plausible mechanism for translating definability or nondefinability of automorphism graphs between a graph construction and its field coding.

other_useful_info: Section 3.C explicitly connects this construction to effective interpretations: the graph domain inside the field is uniformly existentially definable by the curve X, and adjacency is uniformly existentially definable by the existence of a point on the appropriate Y-curve. The same section cites Harrison-Trainor, Melnikov, Miller, and Montalbán, Computable functors and effective interpretability, arXiv:1506.02737v1, for the general equivalence between computable functors and uniform effective interpretability. Remark 9.8 states that functoriality also transfers relative intrinsic definability classes of relations, including relatively intrinsically Σ^0_α, Π^0_α, and Σ^1_m, between a graph G and the corresponding field F_ω(G), and that every definable relation on G is mapped to a definable relation on F_ω(G) when the construction is viewed as a bi-interpretation. The paper relies on the graph-completeness theorem of Hirschfeldt, Khoussainov, Shore, and Slinko, Annals of Pure and Applied Logic 115 (2002), to transfer many computable-model-theoretic properties from arbitrary non-automorphically-trivial structures to graphs before applying F_ω.
why_relevant: Constructs computable functors preserving essential computable-model-theoretic properties, including automorphism spectra. This is useful for transferring any graph-based construction solving the problem into fields or other algebraic settings.

title: Borel Functors and Infinitary Interpretations
overall_summary: The paper introduces infinitary interpretations of countable structures, allowing domains made of tuples of arbitrary finite lengths and all defining relations to be L_{ω1,ω}-definable. Its main theorem says that every Baire-measurable functor between categories of copies of countable structures is naturally isomorphic to one induced by such an infinitary interpretation, with preservation of Borel/infinitary complexity; a forcing construction with finite partial bijections is used to build the interpretation from the functor. As consequences, every continuous homomorphism between automorphism groups of countable structures is induced by an infinitary interpretation, and continuous isomorphisms of automorphism groups correspond to infinitary bi-interpretations. The paper also characterizes continuous quotients of an automorphism group onto S∞ or Aut(Q,<) in terms of definable imaginary indiscernibles. For the original problem, the most relevant material is the bridge between descriptive or computability-theoretic uniformity of maps on copies and infinitary definability inside a structure, especially the complexity-preserving forcing construction and the use of automorphism-group topology to produce or rule out definable imaginary indiscernibles.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 1-2: infinitary interpretation and induced automorphism homomorphism
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A=(A;P^A_0,P^A_1,...) and B be countable relational structures, with P_i of arity a(i). An infinitary interpretation I of A in B consists of L_{ω1,ω}-definable without parameters relations Dom^B_A⊆B^{<ω}, an equivalence relation ∼ on Dom^B_A, and relations R_i⊆(Dom^B_A)^{a(i)} closed under ∼, together with a map f^B_A:Dom^B_A→A inducing an isomorphism (Dom^B_A/∼;R_0/∼,R_1/∼,...)≅(A;P^A_0,P^A_1,...). If I is such an interpretation and h∈Aut(B), then h acts coordinatewise on tuples, inducing a map ~h on Dom^B_A/∼, and the interpretation induces a homomorphism G_I:Aut(B)→Aut(A) by G_I(h)=f^B_A∘~h∘(f^B_A)^{-1}. The paper notes that G_I is continuous. The interpretation is called Δ^{in}_α, respectively Δ^c_α, when all of Dom^B_A, ∼, and the R_i are definable by formulas of that infinitary complexity.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because Dom^B_A, ∼, and R_i are parameter-free L_{ω1,ω}-definable in B, every automorphism h of B preserves each of them. Thus h acts on Dom^B_A, respects ∼, and preserves every quotient relation R_i/∼. Conjugating the induced quotient automorphism by the chosen quotient isomorphism f^B_A gives an automorphism of A. Functoriality of coordinatewise action gives G_I(h_1h_2)=G_I(h_1)G_I(h_2). Continuity follows from the usual topology of pointwise convergence on automorphism groups: to determine the value of G_I(h) on a finite subset of A, choose finitely many tuple representatives in Dom^B_A, and it is enough to know h on the finitely many coordinates occurring in those representatives.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3: continuous automorphism-group homomorphisms come from infinitary interpretations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any countable structures A and B, every continuous group homomorphism H:Aut(B)→Aut(A), where the automorphism groups have the pointwise-convergence Polish topology, is equal to G_I for some infinitary interpretation I of A in B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper proves this using Theorem 13 and Theorem 9. First extend H to a Borel functor G:Iso(B)→Iso(A) with G(B)=A and whose restriction to Aut(B) is H. This is done by choosing, Borelly in each copy ^B of B, an isomorphism Γ_{^B}:^B→B with Γ_B=id_B, and setting G(^B)=A and G(f)=H(Γ_{~B}∘f∘Γ_{^B}^{-1}) for isomorphisms f:^B→~B. Then Theorem 9 constructs from G an infinitary interpretation I of A in B and an isomorphism F^B:G(B)=A→Dom^B_A/∼ such that for every h∈Aut(B), the diagram with F^B, G(h), and the coordinatewise action ~h commutes. Equivalently, G(h)=(F^B)^{-1}∘~h∘F^B, which is exactly the induced homomorphism G_I(h) after taking f^B_A=(F^B)^{-1}. Since G(h)=H(h) on Aut(B), H=G_I.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4: quotients onto S∞ and definable absolute indiscernibles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A, the following are equivalent. (1) There is a continuous onto homomorphism from Aut(A) onto S∞, the full permutation group of a countably infinite set with its usual Polish topology. (2) There are an integer n, an L_{ω1,ω}-definable set D⊆A^n, and an L_{ω1,ω}-definable equivalence relation E⊆D^2 with infinitely many equivalence classes, such that the E-classes are absolutely indiscernible: every permutation of the set D/E of E-equivalence classes is induced by some automorphism of A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (2)⇒(1), identify the countably infinite set D/E with ω. Each automorphism of A induces a permutation of D/E, because D and E are definable. Absolute indiscernibility makes the resulting homomorphism Aut(A)→Sym(D/E) onto. It is continuous because the stabilizer of a class [a] contains the pointwise stabilizer of a finite tuple representative a, hence is open; basic open conditions in Sym(D/E) pull back to cosets/intersections of such open subgroups. For (1)⇒(2), let B be the pure countable structure with no relations, so Aut(B)=S∞. By Theorem 3, the given onto homomorphism H:Aut(A)→S∞ is induced by an infinitary interpretation of B in A. Let D=Dom^A_B⊆A^{<ω} and let E be the interpretation equivalence relation ∼. Any permutation h of D/E corresponds, via the interpretation isomorphism f^A_B, to an automorphism of B; since H is onto, choose g∈Aut(A) mapping to that automorphism. The equality H=G_I implies that g realizes the original permutation h on E-classes, so the classes are absolutely indiscernible. Finally, D may initially lie in A^{<ω}; choose some arity n with D∩A^n nonempty. If a∈D∩A^n and x=f^A_B(a), then using infinitely many distinct y_i∈B and automorphisms of B sending x to y_i, lift these automorphisms through the onto homomorphism to get automorphisms g_i of A. The tuples g_i(a) all lie in D∩A^n and represent pairwise distinct E-classes, giving infinitely many classes at one fixed arity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6: infinitary bi-interpretability is equivalent to Baire-measurable isomorphism of automorphism groups
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For countable structures A and B, A and B are infinitarily bi-interpretable if and only if their automorphism groups Aut(A) and Aut(B), with the pointwise-convergence Polish topology, are Baire-measurably isomorphic as groups. Moreover, every continuous isomorphism H:Aut(B)→Aut(A) is of the form G_I for some infinitary bi-interpretation I of A in B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If A and B are infinitarily bi-interpretable, Theorem 11 produces a Borel adjoint equivalence between the categories Iso(B) and Iso(A) of copies and isomorphisms. Theorem 16 then says that the restriction of the induced functor to Aut(B) is a group isomorphism Aut(B)≅Aut(A); because the functor is Borel, this is Baire-measurable, and in fact continuous by the general theorem that Baire-measurable homomorphisms between Polish groups are continuous. Conversely, if Aut(B) and Aut(A) are Baire-measurably isomorphic, the homomorphism and its inverse are continuous as homomorphisms between Polish groups. Theorem 17 extends a continuous isomorphism H:Aut(B)→Aut(A) to a Borel adjoint equivalence of Iso(B) and Iso(A). Theorem 12 then turns that Borel adjoint equivalence into an infinitary bi-interpretation. The final assertion follows by tracing these constructions and using Theorem 12 so that the interpretation inducing the functor has restriction exactly H.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9: Baire-measurable functors are induced by infinitary interpretations, with complexity preservation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be countable structures, possibly in different countable relational languages. Let Iso(B) be the category whose objects are copies of B with domain ω and whose morphisms are isomorphisms, and similarly for Iso(A). If F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) is a Baire-measurable functor, then there is an infinitary interpretation I of A in B such that F is naturally isomorphic to the functor F_I induced by I. Furthermore, if F is Δ^0_α in the lightface Borel hierarchy, meaning that its object and morphism maps are given by Δ^0_α operators on codes for structures and isomorphisms, then I may be chosen Δ^c_α and the natural isomorphism F⇒F_I may be chosen Δ^0_α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First use Corollary 14 to replace the Baire-measurable functor by a naturally isomorphic Borel one. The proof then builds an interpretation by forcing. Let B^* be the forcing of finite one-to-one tuples from B, ordered by extension; a generic g:ω→B yields a copy B^g of B by pullback, with g:B^g→B an isomorphism. In the product forcing (B^*)^ℓ, the forcing language can express atomic facts about the generic copies B^{g_i}, the transition isomorphisms g_j^{-1}∘g_i, and statements such as F(B^{g_1},g_2^{-1}∘g_1,B^{g_2})(i)=j or F(B^g)⊨P(j). Lemma 26 shows that, in the restricted forcing language not naming external values g_i(m)=n, forcing a Σ_α or Π_α formula is Σ^c_α- or Π^c_α-definable in B. Define Dom^B_A⊆B^*×ω by putting (bbar,i) in Dom when the condition (bbar,bbar) forces F(B^{g_1},g_2^{-1}∘g_1,B^{g_2})(i)=i. Define (bbar,i)∼(cbar,j) when (bbar,cbar) forces F(B^{g_1},g_2^{-1}∘g_1,B^{g_2})(i)=j. For each relation symbol P_s of A, define R_s on Dom by saying that representatives are in R_s if, after replacing them by ∼-equivalent representatives over one common condition cbar, that condition forces F(B^g)⊨P_s of the corresponding tuple of indices. Functorial identities for F, forced by the empty condition, imply that ∼ is an equivalence relation. For a generic g, one defines F_g(i) to be the least tuple of the form (g↾n,i) in Dom. Lemmas 29-31 show that F_g is a bijection from the domain of F(B^g) onto Dom^B_A/∼; Lemma 32 shows that it preserves the relations, hence is an isomorphism. Composing F_g with F(B,g^{-1},B^g):F(B)→F(B^g) gives an isomorphism from A=F(B) to the quotient; Lemma 33 shows that this is independent of the generic g and characterizes it by forcing over B. For each copy ^B, the same construction gives an isomorphism F^{^B}:F(^B)→Dom^{^B}_A/∼. After pulling back the interpreted quotient to domain ω by a canonical bijection τ_{^B}:ω→Dom^{^B}_A/∼, the maps η_{^B}=τ_{^B}^{-1}∘F^{^B} form a natural isomorphism F⇒F_I. Naturality follows from the commutative square F^{~B}∘F(h)=~h∘F^{^B} for every isomorphism h:^B→~B, proved using the forcing characterization. If F has Δ^0_α complexity, the forcing formulas expressing the values of F have Δ^c_α complexity, Lemma 26 transfers this to the definitions of Dom, ∼, and the R_s, and the same forcing characterization gives a Δ^0_α natural isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 11: bi-interpretations induce Borel adjoint equivalences
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be countable structures. If I is an infinitary interpretation of A in B and J is an infinitary interpretation of B in A, and these interpretations form an infinitary bi-interpretation in the sense that the two induced composition isomorphisms are L_{ω1,ω}-definable in the relevant structures, then the associated functors F_I:Iso(B)→Iso(A) and F_J:Iso(A)→Iso(B) form a Borel adjoint equivalence of categories. The paper also states that the relevant definability/Borel complexities are maintained.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The functor induced by an interpretation sends each copy to the interpreted quotient, pulled back to domain ω by a canonical bijection, and sends isomorphisms by the induced coordinatewise action on interpreted tuples. Let τ_{^B}:F_I(^B)→Dom^{^B}_A/∼ and ρ_{^A}:F_J(^A)→Dom^{^A}_B/∼ be the canonical quotient identifications. The bi-interpretation hypotheses give L_{ω1,ω}-definable isomorphisms φ_{^B}:Dom^{Dom^{^B}_A}_B→^B and ψ_{^A}:Dom^{Dom^{^A}_B}_A→^A, uniformly in copies. Define the natural isomorphism η_{^B}:F_J(F_I(^B))→^B by η_{^B}=φ_{^B}∘~τ_{^B}∘ρ_{F_I(^B)}, and similarly define ε_{^A}:F_I(F_J(^A))→^A by ε_{^A}=ψ_{^A}∘~ρ_{^A}∘τ_{F_J(^A)}. Naturality follows because φ and ψ are L_{ω1,ω}-definable and therefore commute with isomorphisms between copies; the definitions of F_I and F_J make the τ and ρ squares commute. The adjoint, or triangle, identities F_I(η_{^B})=ε_{F_I(^B)} and F_J(ε_{^A})=η_{F_J(^A)} are verified by comparing the two possible composition maps inside the bi-interpretation. The proof reduces the required equality to identities such as ~φ_B∘~~τ_B=τ_B∘ψ_{F_I(B)}, first in the fixed copy and then in arbitrary copies by conjugating with isomorphisms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 12: Borel adjoint equivalences induce infinitary bi-interpretations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be countable structures. Suppose F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) and G:Iso(A)→Iso(B), together with natural isomorphisms η:id_{Iso(B)}→G∘F and ε:id_{Iso(A)}→F∘G, form a Borel adjoint equivalence of categories. Then there are infinitary interpretations I of A in B and J of B in A forming an infinitary bi-interpretation, and F and G are naturally isomorphic to the induced functors F_I and F_J. The paper further proves preservation of complexity: for Δ^0_α or Δ^0_α adjoint equivalences, the resulting definitions have the corresponding computable infinitary or infinitary complexity.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 9 separately to F and G to obtain interpretations of A in B and B in A, together with isomorphisms F_{^B}:F(^B)→Dom^{^B}_A/∼ and G_{^A}:G(^A)→Dom^{^A}_B/∼ satisfying the naturality square of Remark 35. Assume A=F(B), by replacing A with an isomorphic copy if necessary. To show that the two interpretations form a bi-interpretation, one must prove that the two composition maps are L_{ω1,ω}-definable. Consider, in B, the isomorphism ~F_B∘G_{F(B)}∘η_B:B→Dom^{Dom^B_A}_B. For any automorphism h of B, naturality of η and the naturality squares for F_B and G_{F(B)} give a commutative diagram showing that this map is invariant under h. Since in countable structures automorphism-invariant relations/functions are L_{ω1,ω}-definable, the composition is definable. The same argument applies on the A side to ~G_A∘F_{G(A)}∘ε_A. For complexity, the same diagrams work uniformly in arbitrary copies ^B and ^A; if F, G, η, and ε are Δ^0_α or Δ^0_α operators, the composition maps are relatively intrinsically of that complexity and hence definable by Δ^c_α or Δ^{in}_α formulas. Finally define the quotient isomorphisms for the bi-interpretation by g^B_A=F_B:A→Dom^B_A and g^A_B=G_{F(B)}∘η_B:B→Dom^A_B, taking inverses for the f maps in the definition of interpretation. The adjoint identity F(η_B)=ε_{F(B)} and the naturality squares identify the formal compositions of these interpretation maps with the invariant definable maps just constructed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 13: extending a continuous automorphism homomorphism to a Borel functor
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For countable structures A and B and every continuous homomorphism H:Aut(B)→Aut(A), there is a Borel functor G:Iso(B)→Iso(A) such that G(B)=A and, for every h∈Aut(B), G(h)=H(h).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a map Γ assigning to every copy ^B of B an isomorphism Γ_{^B}:^B→B with Γ_B=id_B. The paper obtains Γ Borelly using Montalbán's robust Scott-rank theorem: if α is the Scott rank of B in that sense, then B is uniformly Δ^0_α-relatively categorical on a cone, giving a Borel procedure for selecting isomorphisms to the fixed copy. Define G on objects by G(^B)=A for every copy ^B. For an isomorphism f:^B→~B, define G(f)=H(Γ_{~B}∘f∘Γ_{^B}^{-1}), an automorphism of A. Identity morphisms and composition are preserved because Γ_{^B}^{-1} and Γ_{^B} cancel in the middle and H is a homomorphism. Borelness follows from the Borel choice of Γ and continuity of H. If f∈Aut(B), then Γ_B=id_B, so G(f)=H(f).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 14: Baire-measurable functors are naturally isomorphic to Borel functors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every Baire-measurable functor F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) between categories of copies of countable structures is naturally isomorphic to a Borel functor.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix copies B and A=F(B). The restriction H=F↾Aut(B) is a Baire-measurable homomorphism Aut(B)→Aut(A), hence continuous by the standard theorem for Polish groups. Apply Theorem 13 to H to obtain a Borel functor G:Iso(B)→Iso(A) with G(^B)=A for all ^B and G↾Aut(B)=H. Use the same Borel choice Γ_{^B}:^B→B as in Theorem 13 and set η_{^B}=F(Γ_{^B}):F(^B)→F(B)=A=G(^B). For an isomorphism f:^B→~B, the computation G(f)∘η_{^B}=H(Γ_{~B}∘f∘Γ_{^B}^{-1})∘F(Γ_{^B})=F(Γ_{~B})∘F(f)=η_{~B}∘F(f) proves naturality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 15: rank of a continuous automorphism-group homomorphism
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a continuous homomorphism H:Aut(B)→Aut(A), the paper defines the rank of H to be the least ordinal α such that there is a Δ^0_α functor F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) whose restriction to Aut(B) is H, equivalently such that there is a Δ^{in}_α interpretation I of A in B with H=G_I. The proof of Theorem 13 gives the bound that the rank of H is at most the Scott rank of B, in the robust sense used by Montalbán.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence in the definition is exactly the complexity-preserving part of Theorem 9 combined with the fact that interpretations induce functors. The upper bound follows because Theorem 13 constructs a functor extending H by choosing isomorphisms Γ_{^B}:^B→B using the uniform relative categoricity of B at its Scott rank; the complexity of this choice controls the Borel complexity of the resulting functor. The paper remarks that this rank depends on the underlying structures A and B, not only on their automorphism groups.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 16: adjoint equivalences restrict to automorphism-group isomorphisms
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F:Iso(B)→Iso(A), G:Iso(A)→Iso(B), η:id_{Iso(B)}→G∘F, and ε:id_{Iso(A)}→F∘G form a Borel adjoint equivalence of categories, and assume F(B)=A. Then the restriction of F to Aut(B) gives a group isomorphism Aut(B)→Aut(A).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define H_1:Aut(B)→Aut(A) by H_1(h)=F(h). Define H_2:Aut(A)→Aut(B) by H_2(g)=η_B^{-1}∘G(g)∘η_B, where η_B:B→G(F(B))=G(A). The adjoint-equivalence identities F(η_B)=ε_{F(B)}=ε_A and G(ε_A)=η_{G(A)} imply that H_1∘H_2 and H_2∘H_1 are the respective identity maps. For instance, F(η_B^{-1})∘F(G(g))∘F(η_B)=ε_A^{-1}∘F(G(g))∘ε_A=g by naturality of ε. Thus H_1 is a group isomorphism with inverse H_2. Since F and G are Borel, this isomorphism is Baire-measurable and hence continuous as a homomorphism between Polish groups.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 17: extending a continuous automorphism-group isomorphism to a Borel adjoint equivalence
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every continuous isomorphism H:Aut(B)→Aut(A) between automorphism groups of countable structures, there is a Borel adjoint equivalence of categories between Iso(B) and Iso(A), with a functor F satisfying F(B)=A and F↾Aut(B)=H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose Borel isomorphisms Γ_{^B}:^B→B for copies of B and Γ_{^A}:^A→A for copies of A, with Γ_B=id_B and Γ_A=id_A. Define F as in Theorem 13: F(^B)=A and F(h)=H(Γ_{~B}∘h∘Γ_{^B}^{-1}). Define G dually by G(^A)=B and G(k)=H^{-1}(Γ_{~A}∘k∘Γ_{^A}^{-1}). Let η_{^B}=Γ_{^B}:^B→B=G(F(^B)) and ε_{^A}=Γ_{^A}:^A→A=F(G(^A)). Direct computation gives G(F(h))=Γ_{~B}∘h∘Γ_{^B}^{-1}, so G(F(h))∘η_{^B}=η_{~B}∘h; similarly F(G(k))∘ε_{^A}=ε_{~A}∘k. Hence η and ε are natural isomorphisms between the composites and the identity functors. The adjoint identities follow because F(η_{^B})=H(Γ_B∘Γ_{^B}∘Γ_{^B}^{-1})=id_A=ε_{F(^B)} and similarly G(ε_{^A})=η_{G(^A)}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 20-22: basic forcing facts for finite-injection forcing
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B^* be the forcing whose conditions are finite one-to-one tuples from B, ordered by extension, and let (B^*)^ℓ be its product. The forcing language has atomic formulas describing transition maps ˙g_i^{-1}∘˙g_j(m)=n, atomic and negated relation facts R^B(˙g_i(a_1),...,˙g_i(a_r)), optional absolute value facts ˙g_i(m)=n, and countable conjunctions and disjunctions. For the forcing relation defined in the paper: Lemma 20 says that if p forces φ and q⊇p, then q forces φ. Lemma 21 says that for every condition p and every sentence φ of the forcing language, some extension q⊇p decides φ, i.e. forces φ or its formal negation. Lemma 22 says no condition forces both φ and its formal negation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: All three facts are proved by induction on the complexity of φ. For finitary atomic and Boolean combinations, the claims follow directly from the definition of forcing and the monotonicity of finite partial injections. For a countable disjunction φ=∨_n ψ_n, p forces φ exactly when it forces some ψ_n, so monotonicity and consistency follow from the induction hypothesis. For a countable conjunction φ=∧_n ψ_n, p forces φ when for every n and every q⊇p there is r⊇q forcing ψ_n; this definition is designed to give monotonicity. To decide a disjunction, if some extension forces a disjunct use it; otherwise every extension fails to force every disjunct, and the induction hypothesis lets one show p forces the conjunction of the negations. The conjunction case is dual. Consistency for infinitary formulas uses the fact that forcing both a disjunction and the conjunction of the negations would produce, after extending, a condition forcing both some ψ_n and its negation, contradicting the induction hypothesis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 24: restriction of product forcing
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let φ be a computable sentence of the forcing language for (B^*)^ℓ that does not mention the generic symbol ˙g_i. If p=(bbar_1,...,bbar_ℓ) is a condition, then p forces φ in (B^*)^ℓ if and only if the condition with the i-th coordinate removed, (bbar_1,...,bbar_{i-1},bbar_{i+1},...,bbar_ℓ), forces φ in (B^*)^{ℓ-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a direct induction on formulas. Atomic formulas not mentioning ˙g_i are evaluated using only the remaining coordinates. The clauses for finite and countable disjunctions and conjunctions preserve the equivalence because extensions in the irrelevant coordinate do not affect whether formulas omitting that coordinate can be forced.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 25: forcing lemma for hyperarithmetically generic tuples
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix X⊆ω. An X-generic tuple g=(g_1,...,g_ℓ) for (B^*)^ℓ means a tuple of mutually (X⊕B)-hyperarithmetically generic functions ω→B. If φ is an X-computable sentence of the forcing language, then: (1) for X-generic g, the interpreted statement φ[g] holds if and only if some finite condition p⊂g forces φ; (2) if φ begins with a countable conjunction, then a condition p forces φ if and only if every X-generic g⊇p satisfies φ[g].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), the forward direction from forcing to truth is proved by induction. Atomic cases follow from the definitions of forcing for partial injections and pullback structures. Disjunctions are immediate. If p forces ∧_n ψ_n, then for each n and each extension q⊇p there is r⊇q forcing ψ_n; since g is generic, take q⊂g deciding ψ_n, then use consistency to conclude q forces ψ_n, and hence ψ_n[g] holds by induction. Conversely, if φ[g] holds, genericity gives p⊂g deciding φ. If p forced the formal negation, the first half would imply neg(φ)[g], contradiction; hence p forces φ. For (2), if p forces φ then every generic extension satisfies φ by (1). Conversely, if every generic g⊇p satisfies a conjunctive φ, no extension q⊇p can force neg(φ), since a generic through q would then satisfy the negation. Writing φ=∧_n ψ_n and neg(φ)=∨_n neg(ψ_n), it follows that for every n and q⊇p, q does not force neg(ψ_n); by the decision lemma, some r⊇q forces ψ_n. This is exactly the forcing definition for p⊩φ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 26: definability of forcing
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For α≥1, if φ is a Σ_α formula in the restricted forcing language L' for (B^*)^ℓ, where L' omits absolute atoms of the form ˙g_i(m)=n and ˙g_i(m)≠n, then the set of conditions p∈(B^*)^ℓ such that p⊩φ is Σ^c_α-definable in B. If φ is Π_α in L', then the forcing set is Π^c_α-definable in B. The result relativizes to oracle parameters.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on formula complexity. For finitary formulas in L', the forcing relation is definable in B by finitary formulas: statements such as bbar_i(n)=bbar_j(m), inequality alternatives witnessing ˙g_i^{-1}∘˙g_j(m)≠n, and R^B(bbar_i(a_1),...,bbar_i(a_r)) are all first-order definable over B from the tuple entries. For a countable disjunction φ=∨_n ψ_n, p⊩φ iff p⊩ψ_n for some n; if each ψ_n has lower complexity, this gives a Σ^c_α definition. For a countable conjunction φ=∧_n ψ_n, Lemmas 20-22 imply p⊩φ iff for all n and all q⊇p, q does not force neg(ψ_n). By the induction hypothesis, the inner non-forcing relation has the appropriate lower-level complexity, and the universal quantification over finite extensions q produces the Π^c_α definition. The restriction to L' is essential because absolute facts ˙g_i(m)=n refer to the external presentation of B rather than to relations intrinsically definable in B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 27 and Lemmas 28-34: the explicit interpretation built from a Borel functor
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) is a Borel functor and, for the fixed copies, A=F(B). Using finite-injection forcing over B, define Dom^B_A⊆B^*×ω by (bbar,i)∈Dom^B_A iff (bbar,bbar) forces F(B^{˙g_1},˙g_2^{-1}∘˙g_1,B^{˙g_2})(i)=i. Define (bbar,i)∼(cbar,j) iff (bbar,cbar) forces F(B^{˙g_1},˙g_2^{-1}∘˙g_1,B^{˙g_2})(i)=j. For a relation symbol P_s of A of arity r, define R_s on Dom^B_A by declaring ((bbar_1,k_1),...,(bbar_r,k_r))∈R_s iff there are cbar∈B^* and j_1,...,j_r∈ω such that each (bbar_t,k_t)∼(cbar,j_t) and cbar forces F(B^{˙g})⊨P_s(j_1,...,j_r). Lemma 28 says ∼ is an equivalence relation on Dom^B_A. Lemma 29 says that for every generic g:ω→B and every i∈ω, some initial segment (g↾n,i) lies in Dom^B_A. Lemma 30 says every ∼-class in Dom^B_A has a representative of the form (g↾n,i) for a fixed generic g. Lemma 31 says that if (cbar,i),(dbar,j)∈Dom^B_A and cbar⊆dbar, then (cbar,i)∼(dbar,j) iff i=j. Lemma 32 says that, for generic g, the map F_g sending i to the ∼-class of the least (g↾n,i) in Dom^B_A is an isomorphism from F(B^g) onto (Dom^B_A/∼;R_s/∼)_s. Lemma 33 says that the induced map from A=F(B) to Dom^B_A/∼ is independent of the generic: for i∈A and (cbar,j)∈Dom^B_A, its value on i is ∼(cbar,j) iff cbar forces F(B,˙g^{-1},B^{˙g})(i)=j. Lemma 34 says these maps, uniformly over copies of B, yield a natural isomorphism between F and the functor induced by this interpretation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 28 uses only functorial identities forced by the empty condition: identity morphisms give reflexivity, inverse morphisms give symmetry, and composition of the generic transition maps gives transitivity. Lemma 29 fixes a generic g_1=g and a second generic g_2 over it; if j=F(B^{g_1},g_2^{-1}∘g_1,B^{g_2})(i), then finite initial conditions force both this value and the inverse value. Composing the forced morphisms and then applying the restriction lemma to remove the unused middle generic gives an initial segment bbar⊂g with (bbar,i)∈Dom. Lemma 30 is similar: extend a given cbar to a generic g_2 over g_1=g, choose i mapping to the target j, and remove the unused middle coordinate after composing with the fact that (cbar,j) is in Dom. Lemma 31 follows because if cbar⊆dbar, the condition (cbar,dbar) extends (cbar,cbar); since (cbar,i)∈Dom forces i to map to i, consistency forces any value j to equal i. Lemma 32 proves preservation of relations in two directions: if F(B^g) satisfies P_s on indices j_t, choose a common initial segment cbar⊂g forcing this relation and lying in Dom for all j_t, then use Lemma 31 to compare with F_g(j_t); conversely, if the interpreted relation holds via a common cbar and indices k_t, use the ∼ witnesses to transfer the forced P_s-statement along a generic transition to the generic g. Lemma 33 compares two generics g_1 and g_2 extending relevant finite conditions; if the quotient value is ∼(cbar,j), compose the forced map from B to B^{g_1} with the forced transition from B^{g_1} to B^{g_2} and restrict away the unused coordinate, and conversely use the same composition plus Lemma 31. Lemma 34 defines, for each copy ^B, an isomorphism F^{^B}:F(^B)→Dom^{^B}_A/∼ by the forcing characterization of Lemma 33, then pulls back the quotient to ω via a canonical bijection τ_{^B}. The natural isomorphism η_{^B}=τ_{^B}^{-1}∘F^{^B} is natural because for every isomorphism h:^B→~B, the square F^{~B}∘F(h)=~h∘F^{^B} follows by applying h to finite conditions in the forcing characterization.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 36: complexity of the constructed interpretation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) is a Δ^0_α functor, then the forcing construction of Theorem 9 gives a Δ^c_α interpretation I of A in B such that F is naturally isomorphic to the associated functor F_I. Moreover, the natural isomorphism between F and F_I is Δ^0_α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If F is Δ^0_α, then the forcing-language assertions used to define Dom^B_A, ∼, and the relations R_s, such as F(B^{˙g_1},˙g_2^{-1}∘˙g_1,B^{˙g_2})(i)=j and F(B^{˙g})⊨P_s(j), are Δ^c_α in the forcing language. Lemma 26 converts the corresponding forcing sets into Δ^c_α definitions inside B. The map giving the natural isomorphism is characterized by whether a finite condition cbar forces F(^B,˙g^{-1},^B^{˙g})(i)=j; determining this is Δ^0_α in the atomic diagram of ^B uniformly in ^B. Hence the natural isomorphism has Δ^0_α complexity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 37: quotients onto Aut(Q,<) and definable order indiscernibles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A, the following are equivalent. (1) There is a continuous onto homomorphism from Aut(A) onto Aut(Q,<), the automorphism group of the countable dense linear order without endpoints. (2) There are an integer n, an L_{ω1,ω}-definable set D⊆A^n, an L_{ω1,ω}-definable equivalence relation E⊆D^2 with infinitely many equivalence classes, and an L_{ω1,ω}-definable order on the E-classes, such that the E-equivalence classes are order indiscernible: every order-preserving permutation of the E-classes extends to an automorphism of A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is stated to be analogous to Theorem 4. For (2)⇒(1), identify the ordered set D/E with (Q,<); the action of Aut(A) on the definable ordered quotient gives a continuous homomorphism to Aut(Q,<), and order indiscernibility gives surjectivity. For (1)⇒(2), apply Theorem 3 to interpret the ordered structure (Q,<) inside A from the given continuous onto homomorphism. The interpretation domain and equivalence relation give D and E, and the interpreted order gives the definable order on the E-classes. Surjectivity of the homomorphism lifts every order automorphism of Q to an automorphism of A inducing the corresponding permutation of classes. As in Theorem 4, one restricts to a fixed arity n by taking a tuple in the interpretation domain and lifting infinitely many order automorphisms moving its interpreted point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 38: automorphism group continuously isomorphic to S∞
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A, the following are equivalent. (1) There is a continuous isomorphism between Aut(A) and S∞. (2) There are an integer n, an L_{ω1,ω}-definable set D⊆A^n, and an L_{ω1,ω}-definable equivalence relation E⊆D^2 with infinitely many equivalence classes such that the E-classes are absolutely indiscernible, and every other element of A is definable from this set of classes. More explicitly, after adding relations naming each E-equivalence class, every element of A is L_{ω1,ω}-definable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as a consequence of considering isomorphisms rather than merely onto homomorphisms. One direction follows by applying Theorem 6 to a continuous isomorphism Aut(A)≅S∞, where S∞ is the automorphism group of the pure countable set. The resulting bi-interpretation gives the definable imaginary set of absolutely indiscernible classes as in Theorem 4. Because the group map is an isomorphism rather than only a quotient, the kernel of the action on those classes is trivial; by the countable-structure fact that L_{ω1,ω}-definability corresponds to invariance under automorphisms, every element fixed by all automorphisms fixing the named classes is definable from the named classes. Conversely, if such an absolutely indiscernible definable quotient exists and naming its classes makes every element definable, then restriction of automorphisms to the set of classes gives a continuous onto homomorphism to S∞, and the definability-from-classes condition makes the kernel trivial, hence gives a continuous isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 39: automorphism group continuously isomorphic to Aut(Q,<)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A, the following are equivalent. (1) There is a continuous isomorphism between Aut(A) and Aut(Q,<). (2) There are an integer n, an L_{ω1,ω}-definable set D⊆A^n, an L_{ω1,ω}-definable equivalence relation E⊆D^2 with infinitely many equivalence classes, and an L_{ω1,ω}-definable order on the E-classes, such that the E-classes are order indiscernible and every other element of A is definable from this ordered set of classes. Equivalently, after adding relations naming each E-class, every element of A is L_{ω1,ω}-definable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the ordered analogue of Theorem 38 and is stated as an immediate consequence of considering isomorphisms. From a continuous isomorphism Aut(A)≅Aut(Q,<), Theorem 6 yields a bi-interpretation with (Q,<), producing an L_{ω1,ω}-definable imaginary copy of the ordered set of rationals inside A; the isomorphism, not merely quotient map, implies that automorphisms fixing all interpreted ordered classes are trivial, so all remaining elements are L_{ω1,ω}-definable from those classes. Conversely, an ordered indiscernible quotient whose named classes define all elements gives a continuous action homomorphism Aut(A)→Aut(Q,<) that is onto by order indiscernibility and injective because any automorphism acting trivially on all classes fixes all elements definable from them.

proof_techniques.name: Extend automorphism-group homomorphisms to functors by Borel choice of copies
proof_techniques.description: Given a continuous homomorphism H:Aut(B)→Aut(A), the paper chooses, Borelly in each copy ^B of B, an isomorphism Γ_{^B}:^B→B. It then defines a functor that sends every copy of B to the fixed copy A and sends an isomorphism f:^B→~B to H(Γ_{~B}∘f∘Γ_{^B}^{-1}). This converts a topological group homomorphism into a Borel categorical object to which the main functor-to-interpretation theorem can be applied.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If computable AUT-countability on a cone can be organized as a uniform or Borel assignment involving automorphisms of copies, this technique suggests passing from automorphism-group data to a functor and then to infinitary definitions. It is a way to turn external descriptive uniformity into internal interpretability.

proof_techniques.name: Forcing with finite injective tuples to internalize a Borel functor
proof_techniques.description: The central construction uses the forcing B^* of finite one-to-one tuples from B. A generic g:ω→B yields a generic copy B^g, and product generics yield canonical transition isomorphisms g_j^{-1}∘g_i between generic copies. Statements about the Borel functor F on these generic copies and transition maps are expressed in an infinitary forcing language. The interpretation domain consists of finite conditions that force a point to be fixed by the transition between two identical finite approximations; equivalence is forced equality of images under generic transition maps; relations are forced relation facts in F(B^g), transported to common representatives.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a template for converting uniform computations on all copies into formulas inside the original structure. It may be useful for proving that some cone-uniform automorphism property yields L_{ω1,ω} or computable-infinitary definitions, or for identifying exactly where the original problem's desired failure of Σ^{in}_1-definability can occur.

proof_techniques.name: Definability of forcing in the source structure
proof_techniques.description: A crucial technical point is that forcing in the restricted language L', which avoids absolute atoms naming the external presentation values g_i(m)=n, is definable inside B with the same infinitary complexity as the formula being forced. The proof is an induction using a nonstandard forcing clause for countable conjunctions: p forces ∧_n ψ_n if every extension can be further extended to force each ψ_n. This makes Π-level forcing definable by universal quantification over finite extensions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem distinguishes computability relative to a copy from Σ^{in}_1-definability with parameters. This technique gives a precise mechanism for preserving or estimating formula complexity when translating uniform external procedures into internal infinitary definitions.

proof_techniques.name: Use generic copies to prove quotient correctness
proof_techniques.description: After defining the interpretation domain and equivalence relation by forcing, the paper verifies correctness by fixing a generic g and showing that every element of F(B^g) has a representative (g↾n,i), every interpreted equivalence class has such a representative, and nested representatives are equal exactly when their indices agree. This turns the quotient into a concrete copy of F(B^g), and preservation of relations follows by moving forced relation facts through generic transition maps.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When constructing a candidate structure or interpreting automorphisms internally, generic-copy arguments can prove that a syntactically defined quotient really captures the intended external object. This can help isolate whether an automorphism's graph should be definable from finite parameter data.

proof_techniques.name: Natural isomorphism via forcing-characterized maps
proof_techniques.description: The map from F(^B) to the interpreted quotient in ^B is characterized by finite conditions forcing values of F(^B,˙g^{-1},^B^{˙g}). This characterization is independent of the generic and is stable under isomorphisms h:^B→~B, giving the commutative square F^{~B}∘F(h)=~h∘F^{^B}. This square is the key to proving naturality and to showing that restrictions to automorphism groups are exactly the induced homomorphisms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For automorphisms, a graph being definable with parameters often amounts to invariance under stabilizers of those parameters. The paper's method gives a robust way of proving such invariance/naturality statements from forced finite approximations.

proof_techniques.name: Automorphism-invariance implies L_{ω1,ω}-definability in countable structures
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the classical Kueker-Makkai fact that, in a countable structure, a relation on finite tuples is L_{ω1,ω}-definable exactly when it is invariant under all automorphisms. In Theorem 12, composition maps arising from an adjoint equivalence are shown to commute with every automorphism; this invariance is then converted into L_{ω1,ω}-definability, giving a bi-interpretation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: To prove non-definability of a particular automorphism graph from parameters, it is enough at the L_{ω1,ω} level to find an automorphism fixing those parameters but not preserving the graph. Conversely, to prove definability, invariance under the relevant stabilizer is a central route.

proof_techniques.name: Recover definable imaginary indiscernibles from quotients onto S∞
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 4 applies the homomorphism-to-interpretation theorem to a continuous onto map Aut(A)→S∞. Interpreting the pure countable set inside A yields a definable quotient D/E of finite tuples; surjectivity exactly says every permutation of the quotient classes lifts to an automorphism of A. A fixed-arity argument then ensures the indiscernibles can be taken in A^n for some n rather than in all finite tuple lengths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proposed example has a large automorphism-group quotient, this theorem converts that topological information into concrete definable imaginary indiscernibles, which often generate many automorphisms and can affect computable AUT-countability. Conversely, ruling out such quotients can constrain the automorphism-group structure of candidate examples.

proof_techniques.name: Bi-interpretability from adjoint equivalence by invariant composition maps
proof_techniques.description: The paper translates categorical equivalence data F,G,η,ε into two interpretations via Theorem 9, then proves the composition maps required for bi-interpretability are definable by showing they are invariant under automorphisms. The adjoint identities ensure that the two possible ways of comparing double interpretations agree. This is a systematic way to pass from equivalence of categories of copies to syntactic bi-interpretability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original property is easier to analyze after replacing a structure by an infinitarily bi-interpretable one with a simpler automorphism group or presentation, this theorem explains exactly when such replacement preserves the topological automorphism-group data.

proof_techniques.name: Complexity ranks for homomorphisms and interpretations
proof_techniques.description: The paper defines the rank of a continuous homomorphism H:Aut(B)→Aut(A) as the least α for which H is induced by a Δ^{in}_α interpretation, equivalently extends to a Δ^0_α functor. The rank is bounded above by the Scott rank of B via the Borel choice of isomorphisms used in Theorem 13.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original question asks about computability of automorphisms on a cone versus low infinitary definability of automorphism graphs. Rank considerations may help separate global computability/uniformity from specific Σ^{in}_1 definability, or show that certain constructions necessarily produce definitions only at higher complexity.

other_useful_info: The paper assumes countable relational signatures, usually computable, and remarks that noncomputable countable signatures should be handled by relativizing to the degree of the signature. It uses the classical fact due to Kueker and Makkai that for countable structures, L_{ω1,ω}-definability is equivalent to invariance under automorphisms. It also uses the descriptive-set-theoretic facts that automorphism groups of countable structures are Polish and that Baire-measurable homomorphisms between Polish groups are continuous. The introduction points to the effective predecessor HTMMM, which gives a one-to-one correspondence between computable functors and effective interpretations; effective interpretations there use computable infinitary Σ_1 formulas, a notion especially close to the Σ^{in}_1-definability appearing in the original problem. The paper notes that abstract group isomorphism of automorphism groups is insufficient without topology, citing Evans-Hewitt examples of groups isomorphic algebraically but not topologically. It also notes that imaginary equivalence classes are genuinely needed for indiscernible-set characterizations, citing HTIK for the fact that every structure is bi-interpretable with one having no triple of actual indiscernibles.
why_relevant: Connects Baire-measurable functors and homomorphisms of automorphism groups with infinitary interpretations, with preservation of complexity. This is useful for the descriptive-set-theoretic side of automorphism groups and representations.

title: On Optimal Scott Sentences of Finitely Generated Algebraic Structures
overall_summary: The paper studies the optimal infinitary Scott-sentence complexity of finitely generated structures, especially groups, by relating d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentences to the absence of proper existentially elementary self-copies. Its central characterization says that a finitely generated structure has a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence exactly when it is not self-reflective, equivalently when the orbit of a generating tuple is Π^0_1-definable. The main technical method is an index-set construction: a self-reflective computable finitely generated structure has Σ^0_3 many-one complete index set, using a chain of existentially elementary self-copies and a priority-style collapse construction. The paper then constructs a finitely generated computable self-reflective group using small cancellation theory and an HNN extension, giving a finitely generated computable group with no d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the finite-generation/orbit-definability analysis: if a structure has a finite generating tuple with definable orbit, automorphisms are controlled by finite images, while self-reflective constructions show how existential self-similarity can obstruct low-complexity descriptions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let X be an oracle. Let I^X(A) be the set of indices e such that the X-oracle Turing machine Φ_e^X computes the atomic diagram of a countable structure isomorphic to A. If A has an X-computable Scott sentence of infinitary complexity Σ^0_α, respectively Π^0_α, respectively d-Σ^0_α, then I^X(A) belongs to the corresponding pointclass Σ^0_α(X), respectively Π^0_α(X), respectively d-Σ^0_α(X). Here d-Σ^0_α means a conjunction of a Σ^0_α sentence and a Π^0_α sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as the standard connection between Scott sentences and index sets. Given an index e for an X-computable atomic diagram, satisfaction of an X-computable L_{ω1ω} sentence of complexity Σ^0_α or Π^0_α by the structure computed by Φ_e^X has exactly the corresponding arithmetical/hyperarithmetical complexity relative to X, by induction on the formula complexity and the definitions of the infinitary hierarchy. Since a Scott sentence for A is true exactly in the copies of A, membership e ∈ I^X(A) is equivalent to satisfaction of that sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finitely generated countable structure. Say that a substructure B ⊆ M is Σ^0_1-elementary in M, written B ≺_1 M, if for every existential first-order formula φ and tuple b from B, M ⊨ φ(b) if and only if B ⊨ φ(b). Say that M is self-reflective if M contains a proper substructure B ⊊ M such that B ≺_1 M and B is isomorphic to M. Then the following are equivalent: (1) M has a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence; (2) M is not self-reflective; (3) for all generating tuples of M, equivalently for some generating tuple of M, the automorphism orbit of that tuple is definable by a Π^0_1 formula.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper proves the equivalence of (1) and (2), while citing Alvir, Knight, and McCoy for the equivalence between (1) and (3). The implication from non-self-reflection to a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence is Theorem 2.1: choose a finite generating tuple g, collect the quantifier-free formulas witnessing that any tuple satisfying the atomic type of g but not generating the whole structure fails to be existentially elementary, and use these witnesses to build a Σ^0_2 plus Π^0_2 Scott sentence. Conversely, if M were self-reflective and had a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence, choose an oracle X computing the sentence and M. Proposition 1.2 would put the relativized index set I^X(M) in d-Σ^0_2(X), while Theorem 2.2 says that self-reflection makes I^X(M) Σ^0_3(X) many-one complete, a contradiction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a finitely generated structure. If A is not self-reflective, meaning that A has no proper Σ^0_1-elementary substructure isomorphic to A, then A has a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a finite generating tuple g for A, and let p be the atomic type of g. Any tuple g′ satisfying p generates a substructure isomorphic to A. If such a tuple g′ does not generate all of A, then the generated copy of A is a proper substructure. Since A is assumed not self-reflective, this proper copy cannot be Σ^0_1-elementary. Hence there are a tuple a from A and a quantifier-free formula ψ(x,y) such that A ⊨ ψ(g′,a), but there is no tuple b from A with A ⊨ ψ(g,b). Let S be the set of all quantifier-free formulas ψ with this property for some non-generating tuple g′ satisfying p. The Scott sentence is the conjunction of two parts. The Σ^0_2 part says: there exists a tuple x satisfying p such that for every ψ ∈ S and every tuple z, ψ(x,z) fails. The Π^0_2 part says: for every tuple x satisfying p, either x generates the whole structure, i.e. every y is equal to t(x) for some term t, or there are ψ ∈ S and z with ψ(x,z). The original A satisfies both parts using x = g. If a countable structure M satisfies the conjunction, the Σ^0_2 part gives a tuple h satisfying p and avoiding every S-witness. The Π^0_2 part then forces h to generate M, because the alternative would realize some ψ ∈ S. Since h satisfies the atomic type p of the generating tuple g, the map t(g) ↦ t(h) gives an isomorphism A ≅ M. Thus the conjunction is a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence for A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be an X-computable finitely generated self-reflective structure. Then the relativized index set I^X(A) is Σ^0_3(X) many-one complete relative to X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is given for computable A and relativizes to X. Fix an arbitrary Σ^0_3 set S, written in the form n ∈ S iff ∃e such that W_{f(e,n)} is infinite, with f computable and with the enumeration arranged so that at each stage at most one e receives a new element. Uniformly in n the construction builds a computable structure B_n such that n ∈ S implies B_n ≅ A, while n ∉ S implies B_n is not finitely generated and hence not isomorphic to A. Since A is self-reflective, there is an effective chain A_0 ≺_1 A_1 ≺_1 A_2 ≺_1 ... whose members are all isomorphic to A, each A_i is a proper Σ^0_1-elementary substructure of A_{i+1}, and the union A* is not finitely generated. The construction builds B as an increasing union of finite partial structures, viewing the language relationally, together with nested unary predicates R_0[s] ⊆ ... ⊆ R_{k_s}[s] and partial embeddings j[s]: B[s] → A* satisfying j[s](R_i[s]) ⊆ A_i. At stages 3t+1, for every active i ≤ k_s, the construction extends the copy R_i so that the range of j includes the next unused element of A_i; this ensures eventual surjectivity onto A_i. At stages 3t+2 it starts a new empty relation R_{k_s+1}. At stages 3t+3, if some W_{f(e,n)} with e < k_s receives an element, the construction collapses all current higher R_j into R_e: writing u for the tuple from R_e[s] and v for all other current elements, let ψ(u,v) be the finite atomic diagram of B[s]. Since j(u) lies in A_e and A_e ≺_1 A_{k_s}, the existential formula ∃y ψ(j(u),y), realized in A_{k_s} by j(v), is realized in A_e by some tuple a. The construction redefines j on v to a and puts all of v into R_e, setting k_{s+1}=e. This is the only place where Σ^0_1-elementarity is used. Let k = liminf_s k_s. If n ∈ S, then k is the least e for which W_{f(e,n)} is infinite; if n ∉ S, then k = ∞. For each fixed i ≤ k after all lower-priority collapses stop, R_i[t] is increasing, R_i = ⋃_t R_i[t] is a substructure of B, and j_i = ⋃_t j[t] restricted to R_i is an isomorphism R_i ≅ A_i. Also B = ⋃_{i≤k} R_i. If k is finite, infinitely many collapses to R_k ensure B = R_k ≅ A. If k = ∞, then every finite tuple of B is contained in some R_i, but R_i is properly contained in R_{i+1}; choosing an element of A_{i+1} outside A_i and pulling it back shows the tuple cannot generate B. Thus B is not finitely generated. This gives a many-one reduction from S to I(A), proving Σ^0_3-hardness; membership in Σ^0_3 follows for finitely generated computable structures from their computable Σ^0_3 Scott sentences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every finitely generated field has a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof shows that a finitely generated field F is not self-reflective, and then applies Theorem 2.1. Let F have characteristic p, possibly p = 0, and let F_p be its prime field. Write F = F_p(a_1,...,a_m,b_1,...,b_n), where a_1,...,a_m is a transcendence basis and the b_j are algebraic over F_p(a_1,...,a_m). Suppose φ: F → E is an isomorphism from F onto a proper subfield E ⊊ F. Let a_i′ = φ(a_i) and b_j′ = φ(b_j), so E = F_p(a′,b′). Since F and E have the same finite transcendence degree, the original tuple a,b is algebraic over F_p(a′,b′). Therefore the atomic type of a,b over F_p(a′,b′) is isolated by a formula ψ(a′,b′,x,y). This existential formula is realized in F by x,y = a,b. It cannot be realized in E by any c,d ∈ E, because then E(c,d) = E would be isomorphic to F over E, impossible when E is a proper subfield of F. Thus E is not Σ^0_1-elementary in F. No proper self-copy of F is Σ^0_1-elementary, so F is not self-reflective, and Theorem 2.1 yields a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2 (Britton's Lemma)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = ⟨S | R⟩ be a group, let H,K ⊆ G be subgroups, and let α: H → K be an isomorphism. The HNN extension of G by α is G*_α = ⟨S,t | R, t h t^{-1} = α(h) for all h ∈ H⟩. If a word w in G*_α has normal form w = g_0 t^{e_1} g_1 t^{e_2} ... t^{e_n} g_n with each g_i ∈ G and each e_i ∈ {1,-1}, and if w represents the identity, then either n = 0 and g_0 is the identity in G, or there is a pinch t g_k t^{-1} with g_k ∈ H, or there is a pinch t^{-1} g_k t with g_k ∈ K. Consequently, the natural homomorphism G → G*_α is an embedding.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes Britton's Lemma as a standard tool. Its role in the later construction is to force any nontrivial trivial word in the HNN extension either to come from the base group K or to contain a reducible subword t u t^{-1} or t^{-1} u t where u belongs to the appropriate associated subgroup generated by the b_i. This lets the authors combine HNN normal-form reductions with small-cancellation reductions inside the base group.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4 (Greendlinger's Lemma)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = ⟨S | R⟩ be a group with a symmetrized presentation satisfying the small-cancellation condition C′(λ), where 0 ≤ λ ≤ 1/6. If w is a nontrivial freely reduced word representing the identity in G, then some cyclic permutation r of a relator from R or its inverse can be written r = u v such that u is a subword of w and |u| > (1 - 3λ)|r|. In particular, for a C′(1/6) presentation, a Dehn-minimal word, meaning a word containing no subword longer than half of a relator, represents the identity if and only if it is the empty word.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper uses Greendlinger's Lemma as the small-cancellation engine. If a nonempty freely reduced word equals the identity, then it has a long overlap with some defining relator. Replacing that long overlap by the complementary shorter part of the relator strictly decreases word length. Iterating this yields Dehn's algorithm: reduce until no more long relator subwords occur; the resulting Dehn-minimal word is trivial exactly when the original word was trivial. This reduction principle is combined with Britton's Lemma to solve the word problem in the constructed HNN extension.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4.3 small-cancellation observation for K
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be the directed tree with vertices V(T) = {(n,τ) : n ∈ ω and τ ∈ Z^{<ω}}, with parent (n,τ)^- = (n,τ^-) when τ is nonempty and (n,⟨⟩)^- = (n+1,⟨⟩). Let B = {b_i : i ∈ Z}, let a be another generator, and let u(x,y) = x y x^2 y ... x^{100} y. Define K with generators V(T) ∪ {a} ∪ B and relators u((n,τ),a) = (n,τ)^- and u((n,τ),b_i) = (n,τ⌢⟨i⟩), for all (n,τ) ∈ V(T) and i ∈ Z. The paper notes that this presentation is C′(1/10). It also notes that any reduced word in the letters B is Dehn-minimal, and hence B freely generates a free subgroup of K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The C′(1/10) verification is recorded without detailed proof in the paper and is based on the long rigid relators built from u(x,y). Since no relator contains two consecutive b_i letters, a reduced word using only B-letters cannot contain a subword longer than half of any relator. Thus a nonempty reduced B-word is Dehn-minimal. By Greendlinger's Lemma, a nonempty Dehn-minimal word cannot represent the identity in the C′(1/10) presentation. Therefore the subgroup generated by B is a free group on the b_i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Claim 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With K as in Section 4.3, let F(B) be the subgroup generated by B = {b_i : i ∈ Z}. If v is a Dehn-minimal word in the alphabet V(T) ∪ {a} ∪ B, then the element represented by v lies in F(B) if and only if v itself is a word using only letters from B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward implication is the content. Suppose v is Dehn-minimal and represents the same element of K as a reduced B-word v′. Remove any common prefix so that v^{-1}v′ is freely reduced. If v′ is empty, then v represents the identity, so Dehn-minimality and Greendlinger's Lemma force v to be empty. Otherwise v^{-1}v′ is a nontrivial word representing the identity. Greendlinger's Lemma gives a subword u of v^{-1}v′ which is also a subword of some relator r and has length |u| > 0.7|r|, since the presentation is C′(1/10). Relators in K have no two consecutive B-letters, so the long subword u cannot lie substantially inside the B-word v′; at most its last letter can be in v′. Thus the part u′ of u lying in v^{-1} has length at least |u|-1, which is still greater than half of |r| because the relators have length greater than 100. This contradicts the assumption that v is Dehn-minimal. Hence v must have been a B-word.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the small-cancellation group from Section 4.3. Let G be the HNN extension G = ⟨K,t | t b_i t^{-1} = b_{i+1} for every i ∈ Z⟩. Then G is self-reflective. More explicitly, G is finitely generated by (0,⟨⟩), a, b_0, and t, and the subgroup H generated by (1,⟨⟩), a, b_0, and t is a proper Σ^0_1-elementary subgroup of G that is isomorphic to G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First, H is isomorphic to G. Define ι on generators by ι((n,τ)) = (n+1,τ) and by fixing a, every b_i, and t. The relators are preserved: u((n+1,τ),a) = (n+1,τ)^- = ι((n,τ)^-), u((n+1,τ),b_i) = (n+1,τ⌢⟨i⟩) = ι((n,τ⌢⟨i⟩)), and t b_i t^{-1} = b_{i+1} is fixed. Thus ι is a homomorphism onto H. To prove injectivity, take a nontrivial word v of minimal length among words representing its group element and suppose ι(v)=1. By Britton's Lemma, either ι(v) lies in K or it contains a pinch t u t^{-1} or t^{-1} u t with u ∈ F(B). If a pinch occurs, minimality of v implies u is Dehn-minimal; otherwise a Dehn reduction in u would give a shorter word for v. Claim 4.5 then says u is actually a B-word, so the corresponding pinch already existed in v and could be shortened using the HNN relation, contradicting minimality. Hence no t occurs. Then Greendlinger's Lemma applied in K gives a long relator subword of ι(v); every such relator is the ι-image of a relator for v, so v itself would be Dehn-reducible, again contradicting minimality. Thus ι is injective. Second, H is proper. If (0,⟨⟩) lay in H, choose a shortest spelling v for it using the generators ι(V(T)), a, B, and t. Applying Britton's Lemma to (0,⟨⟩)^{-1}v and arguing as above removes t. Greendlinger's Lemma then gives a long relator subword. This subword cannot involve the initial (0,⟨⟩)^{-1} in the required way, because the only relevant relation containing (0,⟨⟩)^{-1} without other level-0 vertices is u((0,⟨⟩),a) = (1,⟨⟩), whose long subwords contain more than one occurrence of (0,⟨⟩). Hence the reducible subword lies inside v, contradicting shortest spelling. Third, H is Σ^0_1-elementary. It suffices to show a retraction property: for every finite set S ⊂ G with 1 ∉ S, there is a homomorphism κ: G → G with κ restricted to H equal to the identity, κ(G)=H, and 1 ∉ κ(S). Fix shortest spellings of elements of S. Choose an integer n much larger than all lengths and B-subscripts appearing, define κ by fixing the generators of H and sending (0,⟨⟩) to (1,⟨n⟩), equivalently sending each (0,τ) occurring through the tree relations to (1,⟨n⟩⌢τ).
