Then: (1) A has a Π_β Scott sentence iff Φ_α(A) has a Π_{α+β} Scott sentence; (2) A has a computable Π_β Scott sentence iff Φ_α(A) has a computable Π_{α+β} Scott sentence. The analogous equivalences also hold for Σ Scott sentences.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Effective bi-interpretability preserves the existence and computable existence of Scott sentences at a fixed complexity level. The α-canonical structural jump shifts Scott-sentence complexity by α: the jump of a structure has a Π_β Scott sentence exactly when the original structure has a Π_{α+β} Scott sentence, and similarly in the computable setting. Since A is effectively bi-interpretable with the α-jump of Φ_α(A), applying these two preservation/shift facts gives both equivalences, and the same argument applies to Σ formulas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every computable ordinal α, there is a computable structure with a Π_{α+2} Scott sentence but with no computable Σ_{α+4} Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take the computable structure A from Theorem 1.2 with a Π2 Scott sentence and no computable Σ4 Scott sentence. Apply the uniform jump-inversion construction Φ_α. By Proposition 5.10, Φ_α(A) has a Π_{α+2} Scott sentence. If Φ_α(A) had a computable Σ_{α+4} Scott sentence, then A would have a computable Σ4 Scott sentence, contradicting the choice of A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (A_i)_{i∈ω} be an effective list of possibly partial computable structures in a sufficiently rich language. For every computable ordinal α, the index set {i : A_i has a computable Π_{α+2} Scott sentence} is Π1^1-m-complete.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start with the many-one reduction from a Π1^1 set X to computable structures A_i such that i∈X iff A_i has a computable Π2 Scott sentence, supplied by Theorem 1.3. Uniformly replace A_i by Φ_α(A_i). By Proposition 5.10, A_i has a computable Π2 Scott sentence iff Φ_α(A_i) has a computable Π_{α+2} Scott sentence. This composes the original Π1^1-hardness reduction with jump inversion. The Π1^1 upper bound is the same kind of upper bound used for Theorem 1.3.

proof_techniques.name: C.e. labelled bouquet/flower graphs
proof_techniques.description: The constructions represent structures as bouquet graphs whose central elements carry c.e. sets of labels, with a label implemented by adding a loop of a specified length. Labels can be added but never removed, matching the monotonicity of computable enumerations. Sort labels u_e partition the domain into independent regions, while ordinary labels ℓ_i, ℓ_σ, and dagger labels ℓ_i^† or ℓ_σ^† distinguish elements or code tree positions. The cited bouquet-graph criterion reduces having a Π2 Scott sentence to a finite-discreteness check on label sets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a natural template for building computable structures with very explicit automorphism orbits: automorphisms are forced to preserve label sets and can only permute intended duplicates. One could try to arrange finite orbit isolation, hence low infinitary orbit definability, while coding enough global information into labels or duplicates to affect computability or definability of individual automorphisms.

proof_techniques.name: One sort per diagonalization requirement
proof_techniques.description: Both main constructions allocate a separate sort U_e to the eth computable infinitary sentence θ_e. The rest of the structure is shared, but diagonalization against θ_e happens only inside U_e. This isolates requirements so that satisfying or injuring one diagonalization does not destroy label-discreteness or the behavior needed for other θ_m.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a construction needing to defeat all candidate Σ1-infinite definitions of automorphism graphs, a similar sort-by-index organization could reserve independent regions where each candidate definition is made wrong while preserving global computability-on-a-cone properties.

proof_techniques.name: Stagewise-isomorphic direct limits with nonisomorphic limits
proof_techniques.description: A central method is to build A and, for each e, a comparison structure B_e such that A_s ≅ B_{e,s} at every finite stage, but the embeddings from stage to stage differ. In the limit, B_e may contain a special element c that has followed a moving target and accumulated a label pattern not realized in A. Because computable infinitary formulas of the relevant complexity only see finite or controlled approximations at the stages used, the construction can force θ_e to hold in B_e whenever it holds in A, while B_e remains nonisomorphic to A.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For automorphism-definability questions, an analogous construction might build two stagewise indistinguishable maps or partial automorphisms whose limit behavior differs from every proposed Σ1-infinite definition. The method is useful whenever one wants finite evidence to agree but limit objects to diverge.

proof_techniques.name: Expansionary stages for Π2 candidates
proof_techniques.description: For a computable Π2 sentence θ_e = ∧_i ∀x_i φ_i(x_i) with φ_i Σ1, a stage is expansionary if the current finite approximation verifies the first k conjuncts on the relevant bounded tuples with bounded witnesses. If A satisfies θ_e, expansionary stages occur infinitely often, because each finite set of Σ1 witnesses eventually appears. At each expansionary stage the construction takes a step that makes the comparison structure B_e more different from A while preserving enough finite agreement to transfer θ_e.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When diagonalizing against a countable list of existential infinitary definitions, expansionary stages can be used to wait until a candidate has committed finite positive information and then act in a controlled labelled region.

proof_techniques.name: Priority rollback for Π3 candidates
proof_techniques.description: The Π3 construction handles θ_e = ∧_i ∀x_i ∨_j ∃y_{i,j} φ_{i,j}(x_i,y_{i,j}) with φ_{i,j} Π1. Requirements R^e_{i,b} watch for finite evidence that a tuple b in B_e witnesses failure of the inner Σ2 disjunction. When such evidence appears, the construction rolls A and B_e back to the finite active pattern present when the requirement began, merges labels, and injures lower-priority requirements. If a negative witness is real, some requirement acts infinitely often and A ≅ B_e, making A fail θ_e too; if B_e satisfies θ_e, all requirements act finitely often and the diagonalizing special element c separates B_e from A.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This rollback mechanism may be portable to automorphism constructions where a candidate definition can be injured by collapsing or duplicating finite labelled configurations, while true global computability requirements are preserved by finite-injury or controlled infinite-injury outcomes.

proof_techniques.name: Finite distinguishing labels preserve ∃-atomicity
proof_techniques.description: After every diagonalizing or rollback action, the constructions check that each eventual element type retains a finite label distinguishing it from all non-duplicates. This check is local to each sort and is what keeps the final structure ∃-atomic despite complicated priority behavior. In the tree construction, non-leaves are distinguished by dagger labels ℓ^†_σ and leaves by their node labels ℓ_σ; in the Π3 construction, newly split active elements are separated by fresh labels.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the target structure must have low orbit complexity but complicated automorphism behavior, finite distinguishing labels are a way to make tuple orbits simple while leaving room for nontrivial permutations among deliberately duplicated components.

proof_techniques.name: Tree/path coding of Π1^1 hardness
proof_techniques.description: The index-set construction codes a computable tree T into labelled sorts. If T is well-founded, the limit labelled tree T_∞ is well-founded and c.e., allowing an explicit computable Π2 Scott sentence that rules out extra infinite-branch elements. If T has an infinite path π, that path defines a noncomputable comparison structure B_e with a path-limit element c; any computable Π2 sentence true of A_T is also true of some nonisomorphic B_e. Thus well-foundedness is exactly the condition for a computable Π2 Scott sentence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests a way to separate cone-level or oracle-relative computability from parameter-free definability: well-founded parts can give effective control, while ill-founded paths or path-like limit elements can witness failure of proposed definitions.

proof_techniques.name: Parameter-to-complexity shift via A·ω
proof_techniques.description: The Section 2 observation uses the fact that a Σ_{α+1} Scott sentence is equivalent to a Π_α Scott sentence after naming finitely many parameters. Passing from A to infinitely many disjoint copies A·ω preserves positive Π_α Scott complexity but turns absence of Π_α Scott sentences into absence of Σ_{α+1} Scott sentences. This is a clean way to convert parameterized lower bounds into unparameterized syntactic lower bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem explicitly allows finite parameter sets and their images under an automorphism. This parameter/complexity shift is directly relevant for understanding how finite parameters can lower infinitary complexity, and for designing examples where adding finitely many parameters still does not define certain maps.

proof_techniques.name: Index-set complexity as a non-characterization tool
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that the property 'has a computable Π2 Scott sentence' is Π1^1-m-complete on computable indices, even though the boldface property 'has a Π2 Scott sentence' is much simpler. It then uses this complexity gap to derive existence results, such as a computable structure with a computable Π2 Scott sentence but no c.e. Scott family of computable Σ1 formulas.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proposed criterion for computable AUT-countability on a cone or for definability of automorphisms gives too low an index-set complexity, a similar argument may refute it or force counterexamples.

proof_techniques.name: Pseudo-Scott characterization over hyperarithmetic copies
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 5.5 uses descriptive complexity rather than a direct construction to obtain a computable structure with a Π2 sentence characterizing it among hyperarithmetic structures, but not among all countable structures. This separates hyperarithmetic/pseudo-effective classification from genuine computable Scott sentences.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem involves computability relative to presentations and cones. This kind of hyperarithmetic-versus-all-copies separation may be useful when a structure behaves well on a cone but still lacks certain absolute infinitary definitions.

proof_techniques.name: Jump inversion / Marker extension to shift complexity
proof_techniques.description: Using Φ_α(A), a uniform jump-inversion construction effectively bi-interpretable with the α-canonical structural jump, the paper shifts examples and index-set hardness from Π2 to Π_{α+2}. Effective bi-interpretability preserves Scott-sentence existence, and the structural jump adds α to the complexity level.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an automorphism-definability construction works at one infinitary level but the desired phenomenon needs to be placed at another level, jump inversion or Marker-style extensions may shift the complexity while preserving computability-theoretic behavior.

proof_techniques.name: Relativization to Mod(L) and Wadge completeness
proof_techniques.description: The paper notes that the tree construction relativizes: the class of structures A with an A-computable Π2 Scott sentence is Π1^1-Wadge-complete in Mod(L). The proof is the same as the index-set construction, but with the tree/source real supplied as an oracle.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Cone arguments usually require robust relativization. This shows that the labelled-tree machinery is not merely computable-index-specific; it can be run relative to arbitrary presentations/oracles, which is relevant for on-a-cone formulations.

other_useful_info: The paper uses the term ∃-atomic for structures with Π2 Scott sentences: by Montalbán [Mon17], this is equivalent to having a Scott family of existential formulas, and also equivalent to the condition that for each tuple ā there is an existential formula φ(x) such that every universal formula ψ true of ā is implied by φ inside A. The introduction emphasizes a schematic versus non-effective gap: Chen-Gonzalez-Harrison-Trainor are cited as showing that the set of pairs (A,B) with A ≤_n B is Π^0_{2n}-complete, while for fixed A the set {B : A ≤_n B} is Π^0_{n+2} by a non-effective argument using definability of Π_n-types. Open questions recorded in the paper include whether for every n there is a computable structure with a Π_n Scott sentence but no computable Σ_{2n} Scott sentence, and whether the index set of computable structures with a Π2 Scott sentence and a computable Π3 Scott sentence is Π1^1-m-complete. Question 5.4 asks whether the Scott-family complexity bounds in Propositions 5.2 and 5.3 are optimal. The construction can be carried out in any sufficiently rich language by universality/effective bi-interpretability, although the paper presents it concretely through bouquet graphs with loop labels.
why_relevant: Separates low boldface Scott complexity from low computable Scott complexity. This is useful because the problem mixes computability on a cone with non-effective infinitary definability.

title: Effectively Closed Subgroups of the Infinite Symmetric Group
overall_summary: The paper studies subgroups of the infinite symmetric group S∞ that are effectively closed, comparing this notion with automorphism groups of computable structures, computable Polish presentations, and profinite presentations. Its central results include a priority construction of a Π^0_1 presentation of the two-element group Z2 that is not literally equal to Aut(M) for any computable structure M, contrasted with the theorem that every effectively closed discrete group is topologically isomorphic to Aut(M) for some computable structure M. The paper also proves that certain effectively closed compact subgroups have no computable Polish presentation, while every profinite computable Polish group embeds as an effectively closed subgroup of S∞, and it constructs a Σ^1_1 closed oligomorphic group whose orbit relation is not uniformly hyperarithmetic. For the original problem, the most useful content is the effective-closed viewpoint on automorphism groups of computable structures, the discrete-group/orbit-equivalence analysis via hyperarithmetic bounds, and the coding methods that replace hyperarithmetic or ∅′-computable structures by computable structures while preserving automorphism groups.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Introductory observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If C is a computable countable first-order structure with domain ω, in a computable language with computable interpretations of its constants, functions, and relations, then Aut(C), viewed as a subgroup of S∞, is effectively closed. Here S∞ is the group of all permutations of ω, and effectively closed means that the complement is a computably enumerable union of basic open neighborhoods determined by finite partial maps.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as clear. A permutation f is not an automorphism of C exactly when some finite part of f witnesses failure of preservation of the atomic diagram: a constant is sent incorrectly, a relation is not preserved on a finite tuple, or a function value is not preserved on a finite tuple. Because the atomic diagram of C is computable, one can enumerate all finite injective partial maps σ that already violate one of these finite atomic preservation conditions, and enumerate the basic open set of all permutations extending σ into the complement of Aut(C). Conversely, every non-automorphism has such a finite witness. Thus Aut(C) is Π^0_1 as a subset of S∞.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.3 and Claim 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S∞ be the group of all permutations of ω. Let d be the usual ultrametric on ω^ω, and define a complete metric D on S∞ by D(σ,τ) = (d(σ,τ) + d(σ^{-1},τ^{-1}))/2. With the computable dense set of finite-support permutations, this is the natural computable Polish presentation of S∞. A subgroup G ≤ S∞ is effectively closed in the sense that G = P ∩ S∞ for some Π^0_1 subset P of ω^ω if and only if G is an effectively closed subset of the computable Polish space (S∞,D). Equivalently, the metric D on S∞ is effectively compatible with the standard Baire-space metric d on ω^ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof gives uniform translations between basic open sets in ω^ω and basic D-open sets in S∞. If U ⊆ ω^ω is a basic open set specified by a finite partial map σ, then if σ is not injective, U ∩ S∞ is empty. If σ is injective, enumerate all finite injective extensions τ_i of σ^{-1} whose domains are finite initial segments of ω. The pairs (τ_i^{-1},τ_i) specify basic D-open neighborhoods in S∞, and their union is exactly U ∩ S∞. Conversely, if V is a basic D-open set described by finite information about a permutation and its inverse, in the simplified form of a finite permutation σ of an initial segment together with σ^{-1}, then the corresponding basic open U in ω^ω is the set of all extensions of σ, and U ∩ S∞ = V. These translations are effective, so an enumeration of the complement of P in ω^ω can be transformed into an enumeration of the complement of P ∩ S∞ in (S∞,D), and conversely.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Fact 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every Σ^0_2 set S ⊆ ω, there is a uniform procedure which, given x ∈ ω, outputs a computable linear order isomorphic to ω if x ∈ S and outputs a computable linear order isomorphic to ω^2 if x ∉ S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as folklore from computable structure theory and does not prove it. It is used as a uniform coding device: membership in a Σ^0_2 predicate is represented by choosing between two rigid computable linear order types, ω and ω^2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every ∅′-computable relational structure A, with computable underlying set and computable language, there is a computable structure B such that Aut(A) and Aut(B) are topologically isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction is a Marker-style blow-up using rigid linear orders. For each n-ary relation symbol P of A and each tuple ā ∈ A^n, add a new infinite set C^P_ā of elements, with these sets pairwise disjoint. Add relations to B that say y ∈ C^P_x̄, thereby linking the blow-up fiber to the tuple x̄. For each tuple ā, put a linear order L^P_ā on C^P_ā. Using Fact 2.4 and the fact that P(ā) is ∅′-computable, hence Δ^0_2 and in particular expressible for this purpose by a Σ^0_2 condition, choose L^P_ā to be a computable copy of ω if A ⊨ P(ā), and a computable copy of ω^2 otherwise. Add to B the relation saying y1,y2 ∈ C^P_x̄ and y1 <_{L^P_x̄} y2. The resulting B has a computable copy because the linear orders are produced uniformly. Both ω and ω^2 are rigid, so an automorphism of B is completely determined by its restriction to the copy of A, and every automorphism of A extends uniquely fiber-by-fiber to an automorphism of B, since it sends tuples satisfying P to tuples satisfying P and non-satisfying tuples to non-satisfying tuples. The restriction map Φ: Aut(B) → Aut(A) is therefore a group isomorphism. It is topological: continuity is immediate from restriction, and openness is checked on subbasic clopen sets of Aut(B). If a finite condition maps an element q ∈ C^P_ā to p ∈ C^P_b̄, then its image under Φ imposes the finite condition ā ↦ b̄ on Aut(A); because the fiber orders are rigid, this exactly captures the effect of mapping q to p whenever the condition is consistent.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 0.2(1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists an effectively closed subgroup P ≤ S∞ which is topologically isomorphic to the two-element cyclic group Z2, but such that P is not equal, as a concrete subgroup of S∞, to Aut(C) for any computable structure C on domain ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof builds a Π^0_1 subgroup P by enumerating basic neighborhoods into its complement while diagonalizing against a computable list (M_e)_{e∈ω} of all partial computable structures on domain ω. A finite injective partial map is identified with the basic neighborhood of permutations extending it. A finite map x̄ → ȳ is called nice if it is a finite permutation of an initial segment of ω and has order 2; the notation x̄ ↔ ȳ emphasizes this. The construction starts with a nice neighborhood of the identity, for example (0,1) ↔ (0,1), and a disjoint nice neighborhood intended to contain the nonidentity element, for example (0,1) ↔ (1,0). It continuously forbids all subneighborhoods of the identity side that do not contain Id_ω. Each diagonalization strategy for M_e works inside a currently assigned nice neighborhood σ_e = x̄ ↔ ȳ on the nonidentity side. Let n = length(x̄). The strategy first forbids the basic neighborhood x̄ n → x̄(n+1). It then waits for M_e to separate some equal-length extensions x̄ā of x̄ n and x̄b̄ of x̄(n+1) by a first-order atomic formula φ. While waiting, it lets lower-priority strategies work inside the nice neighborhood x̄ n ↔ ȳ n, and it gradually forbids other subneighborhoods of σ_e of the form x̄ n → ȳ k with k ≠ n. If M_e is total but no such atomic separation ever appears, then a back-and-forth argument extends x̄ n → x̄(n+1) to an automorphism of M_e; since this neighborhood has already been forbidden from P, this ensures P ≠ Aut(M_e). If a separating atomic formula φ is found, the strategy chooses a tuple c̄ of very large fresh numbers, of the same length as ā and b̄, so that the partial maps x̄ā → ȳc̄ and ȳc̄ → x̄b̄ have not yet been forbidden and can be extended to nice finite involutions. Let N1 and N2 be the corresponding nice subneighborhoods. The strategy stops the previous slow forbidding, forbids the rest of x̄ n ↔ ȳ n, begins forbidding extensions of N2 one by one, and allows lower-priority work inside N1. It then waits for M_e to evaluate φ on ȳc̄. If φ witnesses x̄ā not mapping to ȳc̄ in Aut(M_e), then Aut(M_e) ∩ N1 = ∅, so the construction keeps the eventual nonidentity element inside N1 while eventually forbidding N2. If instead φ witnesses ȳc̄ not mapping to x̄b̄, then Aut(M_e) ∩ N2 = ∅; the construction stops forbidding N2, forbids N1, chooses a nice subneighborhood τ ⊆ N2, and moves all lower-priority work into τ. If M_e never evaluates φ, then M_e is not a total computable structure, so it need not be diagonalized against. Priority is handled by ordering strategies by e and initializing all weaker strategies whenever a stronger strategy changes the neighborhood in which weaker strategies may act. On initialization, a weaker strategy chooses a fresh nice subneighborhood inside the currently permitted region, with diameter at most 2^{-i} for the i-th strategy. The verification uses three key points: first, if M_e is total and x̄ n → x̄(n+1) cannot extend to an automorphism, then some finite extensions are separated by an atomic formula; second, the tuple c̄ can be chosen large enough that the relevant nice involutive neighborhoods have not been forbidden; third, each strategy eventually stabilizes in one nice neighborhood. The nested stable neighborhoods have diameters tending to 0, so their intersection contains a single permutation Θ. Each finite approximation is an involution, hence Θ^2 = Id_ω. The final Π^0_1 set is P = {Id_ω, Θ}, so P is a subgroup isomorphic to Z2, and every total computable M_e is diagonalized against.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Claims 3.2, 3.3, and 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Within the proof of Theorem 0.2(1), the following claims are established. Claim 3.2: If M_e is total and a finite partial map x̄ n → x̄(n+1) does not extend to an automorphism of M_e, then there are finite equal-length extensions x̄ā and x̄b̄ of x̄ n and x̄(n+1), respectively, and a first-order atomic formula φ separating x̄ā from x̄b̄ in M_e. Claim 3.3: If the strategy reaches the stage where such φ, ā, and b̄ have been found, then one can choose a tuple c̄ of fresh sufficiently large numbers so that the partial maps x̄ā → ȳc̄ and ȳc̄ → x̄b̄ extend to nice finite involutions N1 and N2 that have not yet been forbidden. Claim 3.5: If the e-th strategy is never initialized after a stage s, then after some later stage s′ it has a stable nice neighborhood N_e such that all weaker-priority strategies act only inside N_e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Claim 3.2 is a finite back-and-forth argument: if no finite atomic separation existed, then every finite partial isomorphism extending x̄ n → x̄(n+1) could be extended back and forth, yielding an automorphism of the total structure M_e extending the forbidden map, contrary to the hypothesis. Claim 3.3 uses that at any finite stage only finitely many basic neighborhoods have been forbidden. By taking c̄ with entries sufficiently large and fresh, the construction avoids all already forbidden finite restrictions. The partial maps can then be completed to finite involutions by adding reciprocal pairs whenever one direction has already been specified and fixing all remaining points of the finite initial segment; this produces nice neighborhoods inside the ambient x̄ ↔ ȳ. Claim 3.5 follows from the finite-outcome nature of the strategy: it may wait forever at the first waiting stage, in which case the stable neighborhood is x̄ n ↔ ȳ n; or it may find φ and either stay forever in N1 or make one final switch to a subneighborhood of N2. Thus a strategy that is no longer initialized eventually stops changing the region assigned to lower-priority strategies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Facts 2.1 and 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fact 2.1, cited from Melnikov: every computable Polish presentation of a profinite group P can be transformed into a 0′-computable inverse system F0 ← F1 ← F2 ← ⋯ of finite groups with surjective bonding maps whose inverse limit is topologically isomorphic to P. Fact 2.2, cited from La Roche: every recursively presented profinite group is isomorphic to Gal(K/N), where K is a computably presented algebraic extension of Q and N is a computable subfield of K. The paper uses a fully relativized form of Fact 2.2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Both facts are used as black boxes, and their proofs are not included in the paper. Fact 2.1 converts computable Polish information about a profinite group into an effective inverse-limit presentation, with one jump. Fact 2.2 realizes an effective inverse system of finite groups as a Galois group of a computable algebraic field extension over a computable subfield; the relativized version allows 0′-computable fields and subfields.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 0.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Part (1): There exists an effectively closed compact, equivalently profinite, subgroup of S∞ that has no computable Polish presentation and hence no recursive profinite presentation. Part (2): Every profinite group that has a computable Polish presentation is topologically isomorphic to an effectively closed subgroup of S∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For part (1), for a set S of prime numbers define P_S = ∏_{p∈S} Z_p, the product of cyclic groups of order p. If P_S had a computable Polish presentation, Fact 2.1 would give a 0′-computable inverse system for P_S. Using a result cited from Melnikov, the paper then uniformly obtains a 0′-computable presentation of the discrete Pontryagin dual ⊕_{p∈S} Z_p. From this dual one can 0′-computably list the prime orders of elements, showing that S is Σ^0_2. Therefore choose a Π^0_2-complete set S of primes, so S is not Σ^0_2. The paper builds a computable graph M with Aut(M) topologically isomorphic to P_S. For each prime p, include a connected component C_p consisting of a p-cycle x^p_0 - x^p_1 - ⋯ - x^p_{p-1} - x^p_0, and attach to each cycle node x^p_i a finite or infinite chain. The lengths are controlled by a computable approximation to the Π^0_2 predicate for p: if p ∉ S and the predicate fires only finitely many times, say s, the i-th chain has finite length s+i, making C_p rigid; if p ∈ S, each of the p chains is infinite, so rotations of the p-cycle give Aut(C_p) ≅ Z_p. Since components do not interact, Aut(M) ≅ ∏_{p∈S} Aut(C_p) ≅ P_S, and the product topology agrees with the permutation topology because finite values determine subbasic clopen conditions. Since M is computable, Aut(M) is effectively closed, but P_S cannot have a computable Polish presentation by the previous paragraph. For part (2), start with a profinite computable Polish group P. Fact 2.1 gives a 0′-recursive inverse system. By the relativized La Roche theorem, obtain a 0′-computable algebraic field K and 0′-computable subfield N with Aut(K/N) ≅ P. By passing to a 0′-computably isomorphic copy, assume N is computable. Name each element of N by a singleton unary predicate and replace field operations by their relational graphs, obtaining a 0′-computable relational structure F with Aut(F) ≅ P. Apply Proposition 2.3 to obtain a computable structure F̂ with Aut(F̂) ≅ Aut(F). Hence P is topologically isomorphic to Aut(F̂), an effectively closed subgroup of S∞.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G ≤ S∞ be a discrete effectively closed subgroup. Define the orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on finite tuples by ā ∼_G b̄ if ā and b̄ have the same length and there exists σ ∈ G such that σ(ā) = b̄. Then ∼_G is hyperarithmetic, uniformly in the tuple length.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 1.3, G is a Π^0_2 subset of ω^ω. Since G is discrete as a subspace of the second-countable Polish group S∞, it is countable and hence has no perfect subset. The proof uses the effective perfect set theorem for Σ^1_1 subsets of ω^ω: if a Σ^1_1 set A has no perfect subset, then all elements of A are hyperarithmetic; by Spector Σ^1_1-bounding, there is a computable ordinal γ such that every element of A is computable from 0^(γ). Applying this to G, all elements of G are 0^(γ)-computable. Because G is Π^0_2 as a subset of ω^ω and all its members are bounded in this way, 0^(γ+2) computes a listing of all elements of G. Then 0^(γ+3) decides whether a given pair of finite tuples lies in the same G-orbit by searching this listing for a permutation sending one tuple to the other and using one more jump to decide the resulting existential question. Thus ∼_G is hyperarithmetic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every closed subgroup G ≤ S∞, there is a countable structure M such that G = Aut(M), and M is computable from the orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on finite tuples.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the standard construction realizing a closed subgroup as an automorphism group. For each n and for every ∼_G-equivalence class of n-tuples, introduce an n-ary relation symbol whose interpretation is exactly that orbit. The resulting structure M has domain ω and is computable relative to ∼_G because membership in each relation is determined by orbit equivalence. Every element of G preserves all these orbit relations, so G ⊆ Aut(M). Conversely, if π ∈ Aut(M), then for every finite tuple ā, the tuple π(ā) lies in the same G-orbit as ā, so there is some g_ā ∈ G agreeing with π on ā. Thus every finite restriction of π is realized by an element of G. Since G is closed in S∞, π belongs to G. Therefore Aut(M) = G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every hyperarithmetic structure M, there is a computable structure N such that Aut(N) is topologically isomorphic to Aut(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof generalizes Proposition 2.3. Suppose M is Δ^0_γ for some computable ordinal γ. The paper invokes a generalization of the linear-order coding fact: membership in a Σ^0_α class can be uniformly coded into the isomorphism type of one of two rigid linear orders that agree on all computable Π_γ infinitary formulas but are separated at a higher level; for the purposes of the argument, one may use a copy of ω^{γ+1} if a relevant Σ^0_γ fact holds and a copy of ω^{γ+2} otherwise. Then repeat the blow-up construction from Proposition 2.3: for each atomic relation and each tuple, attach a rigid linear-order fiber whose type records whether that relation holds of that tuple. The fibers are rigid, so automorphisms of the expanded computable structure are determined exactly by their restrictions to the original domain, and every automorphism of M extends uniquely to the expansion. The same clopen-neighborhood argument as in Proposition 2.3 gives a topological isomorphism of automorphism groups.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If G ≤ S∞ is an effectively closed subgroup and the orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on finite tuples is hyperarithmetic, then there is a computable structure M such that G is topologically isomorphic to Aut(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Lemma 4.3 to the closed subgroup G. This gives a structure A with Aut(A) = G and with atomic diagram computable from ∼_G. If ∼_G is hyperarithmetic, then A is a hyperarithmetic structure. Lemma 4.4 then produces a computable structure M with Aut(M) topologically isomorphic to Aut(A). Hence Aut(M) is topologically isomorphic to G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 0.2(2)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every effectively closed discrete group, meaning every discrete topological group presented as an effectively closed subgroup of S∞, is topologically isomorphic to Aut(M) for some computable structure M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let G ≤ S∞ be effectively closed and discrete. By Proposition 4.1, its orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on finite tuples is hyperarithmetic. By Proposition 4.2, any effectively closed subgroup of S∞ with hyperarithmetic orbit equivalence relation is topologically isomorphic to the automorphism group of a computable structure. Combining these two propositions yields the theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If G is an effectively closed discrete group, then G has a hyperarithmetical presentation as a countable discrete group in the sense of computable structure theory. Conversely, the paper outlines that a countable discrete group with a hyperarithmetical presentation can be represented, up to topological isomorphism, as the automorphism group of a computable structure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the converse direction sketched in the paper, let G be a countable discrete group with hyperarithmetical diagram. Use Cayley's theorem: map each g ∈ G to the permutation σ_g of the underlying set of G given by σ_g(h) = gh. The image H ≤ S∞ is topologically isomorphic to G and is discrete, because σ_g is isolated by the basic condition sending the identity element e to g. Moreover H is arithmetical in the diagram of G, so its orbit equivalence relation ∼_H is hyperarithmetic. Lemma 4.3 gives a hyperarithmetic structure M with H ≅ Aut(M), and Lemma 4.4 gives a computable structure N with Aut(N) ≅ Aut(M). Thus G ≅ H ≅ Aut(N).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 0.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists a Σ^1_1 closed oligomorphic subgroup G ≤ S∞ such that its orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on finite tuples is not hyperarithmetic. Oligomorphic means that for each n there are only finitely many G-orbits on n-tuples.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction defines an orbit equivalence relation first, then takes the closed group preserving it. For a nondecreasing sequence k = ⟨k_n⟩ of natural numbers, define an equivalence relation ∼_k on finite tuples. For injective tuples ā,b̄ of the same length n, set ā ∼_k b̄ if for every m < n there are at least n−m coordinates i < n such that a_i ≡ b_i mod 2^{k_m}. For non-injective tuples, the paper says to extend this in the obvious equality-pattern-respecting way. The paper verifies that ∼_k is an equivalence relation, is invariant under coordinate permutations, is preserved under taking corresponding subsequences, has the back-and-forth property, and has only finitely many classes of n-tuples for each n. Let G_k be the set of all f ∈ S∞ such that whenever f(ā)=b̄, one has ā ∼_k b̄. Then G_k is a closed subgroup of S∞; the listed properties imply that ∼_k is exactly the orbit equivalence relation of G_k and that G_k is oligomorphic. To make ∼_k non-hyperarithmetic but Σ^1_1, the proof works over the admissible structure L_{ω_1^ck}. Since the Σ_1-projectum of L_{ω_1^ck} is ω, choose a Δ_2(L_{ω_1^ck}) increasing cofinal sequence ⟨α_n⟩_{n<ω} in ω_1^ck with a finite-change approximation ⟨α_{n,s}⟩_{n<ω,s<ω_1^ck}: it is Δ_1(L_{ω_1^ck}), continuous at limit stages, and for each n changes only finitely many times. For s ≤ ω_1^ck define k_{n,s} = ∑_{m≤n} #{t < s : α_{m,t} ≠ α_{m,t+1}}. Let k = k_{ω_1^ck}. The use of moduli 2^{k_m} gives the needed monotone nesting of the relations as s increases, so the final relation ∼_k is Π_1 over L_{ω_1^ck}, which the paper identifies here as Σ^1_1. Hence G_k is Σ^1_1. Finally, ∼_k is not hyperarithmetic: from ∼_k, over the appropriate admissible set, one can recover the cofinal sequence ⟨α_n⟩. Specifically, for each n and s < ω_1^ck, if the number of ∼_{k_s}-equivalence classes of n-tuples is the same as the final number of ∼_k-classes, then α_n = α_{n,s}. Thus ω_1^{∼_k} > ω_1^ck, so ∼_k is not hyperarithmetic.

proof_techniques.name: Automorphism groups as Π^0_1 subgroups of S∞
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the basic fact that a computable structure has an effectively closed automorphism group: non-automorphisms are detected by finite failures of atomic preservation, and these finite failures enumerate basic open neighborhoods in the complement. This allows computable-structure questions about automorphisms to be recast as questions about Π^0_1 closed subgroups of the infinite symmetric group.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a computable representation A in the original problem, Aut(A) can be studied as a Π^0_1 subgroup of S∞. If computable AUT-countability forces this closed group to be countable/discrete, the paper's discrete effectively closed group analysis becomes directly relevant.

proof_techniques.name: Effective compatibility of Baire-space and permutation-space presentations
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 1.3 shows how to move effectively between Π^0_1 subsets of ω^ω and Π^0_1 subsets of the natural computable Polish presentation of S∞. The proof explicitly translates finite partial maps in ω^ω into finite information about a permutation and its inverse, and conversely.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful whenever one wants to define or diagonalize against automorphisms by finite partial maps while still claiming that the resulting automorphism set is effectively closed in the correct topology.

proof_techniques.name: Priority diagonalization inside nested involutive neighborhoods
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 0.2(1) constructs a Π^0_1 subgroup isomorphic to Z2 by maintaining a nested sequence of finite involutive neighborhoods for the intended nonidentity element while enumerating all incompatible neighborhoods into the complement. Each requirement diagonalizes against one computable structure by forcing either a forbidden finite partial automorphism to exist in that structure or forcing the eventual nonidentity permutation to lie in a neighborhood disjoint from that structure's automorphism group. Initialization and shrinking neighborhoods ensure finite injury and convergence to a single involution.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A similar finite-injury construction could be adapted to build computable structures or automorphism groups satisfying requirements against all candidate Σ^in_1 definitions of automorphism graphs, while preserving that the final nontrivial automorphisms remain computable or otherwise controlled.

proof_techniques.name: Intermediate fresh tuple splitting trick
proof_techniques.description: A key move in the Z2 priority construction is: once a finite map x̄ā → x̄b̄ is known not to be an automorphism of a target structure, choose a very large fresh tuple c̄ and factor the obstruction through two possible maps x̄ā → ȳc̄ and ȳc̄ → x̄b̄. When the target structure eventually evaluates the atomic formula on ȳc̄, at least one of these two maps is impossible; the construction then keeps the final permutation inside the impossible side and forbids the other side as needed. The largeness of c̄ ensures that both candidate neighborhoods are still available and can be extended to finite involutions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This trick is a portable way to delay commitment until an opponent reveals finite information. It may help in diagonalizing against existential infinitary definitions: introduce a fresh tuple so that any eventual witness or evaluation determines which branch can be used to avoid definability.

proof_techniques.name: Rigid-order blow-up preserving automorphism groups
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 2.3 and Lemma 4.4 use a Marker-style expansion: attach to each tuple a rigid fiber whose isomorphism type encodes whether a relation holds of that tuple. Because the fibers are rigid, automorphisms are exactly the original automorphisms extended uniquely to the fibers. By choosing the fiber types using effective coding of Σ^0_2 or hyperarithmetic information, one converts a noncomputable presentation into a computable structure without changing the automorphism group up to topological isomorphism.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a powerful way to first design a structure with the desired automorphism group using higher-level information, then replace it by a computable structure while preserving automorphisms. It may be especially useful for building examples on a cone or for coding parameter-dependent information without adding unwanted automorphisms.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical orbit-relation structure for a closed subgroup
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.3 realizes any closed subgroup G ≤ S∞ as Aut(M) by adding one relation for each orbit of G on finite tuples. The closure of G is crucial: any permutation preserving all orbit relations has each finite restriction realized by some element of G, and hence belongs to G. The resulting structure is computable from the orbit equivalence relation ∼_G.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can first define a suitable closed group of permutations with controlled computability properties, this lemma gives a canonical structure realizing it. The complexity of ∼_G then determines whether Lemma 4.4 can turn that structure into a computable one.

proof_techniques.name: Effective perfect set and Spector boundedness for discrete Π^0_1 groups
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 4.1 applies the effective perfect set theorem and Spector Σ^1_1-bounding to show that a discrete effectively closed subgroup of S∞ has all its elements hyperarithmetically bounded, and therefore has hyperarithmetic orbit equivalence. The argument uses discreteness to rule out perfect subsets and effective closedness to place the group low enough in the projective hierarchy.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Computably AUT-countable structures have only countably many automorphisms; for closed automorphism groups this often points toward discreteness. This technique may provide uniform hyperarithmetic bounds on automorphisms or orbits, which could interact with attempts to prove or refute Σ^in_1 definability.

proof_techniques.name: Graph components realizing products of finite cyclic groups
proof_techniques.description: In Theorem 0.3(1), the paper realizes ∏_{p∈S} Z_p as the automorphism group of a computable graph by using one component for each prime p. A p-cycle with identical infinite chains attached to each vertex has rotational automorphism group Z_p, while carefully chosen unequal finite chain lengths make the component rigid. A Π^0_2 approximation controls whether the chains become infinite or stabilize at unequal finite lengths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides a concrete template for computable structures whose automorphism groups are prescribed products or sums of simple finite symmetries. Variants could be used to build examples with many individually controlled automorphisms while retaining computability of the presentation.

proof_techniques.name: Oligomorphic orbit relations from congruence patterns and finite-change approximations
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 0.4 constructs an oligomorphic orbit equivalence relation by defining finite-tuple equivalence via congruence modulo powers of two, with finitely many classes at each arity. A finite-change approximation to a cofinal sequence in ω_1^ck is used to make the full orbit relation non-hyperarithmetic while keeping the acting group closed and Σ^1_1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one investigates highly homogeneous or ω-categorical candidates for the original problem, this construction indicates how orbit relations can be individually finite at each arity yet nonuniformly complex. It also signals a potential obstacle: oligomorphic behavior alone does not guarantee uniform hyperarithmetic control outside the Π^0_1 setting.

other_useful_info: The paper cites the classical result, referenced to Gao [Gao09], that every closed subgroup of S∞ is the automorphism group of some countable structure; Lemma 4.3 gives the effective orbit-relation version of this construction. The paper leaves open broader extension questions, including whether the positive realization theorem for effectively closed discrete groups extends beyond the cases handled, whether Theorem 0.3(2) extends from profinite computable Polish groups to arbitrary closed subgroups of S∞, and whether a Π^0_1 oligomorphic group can witness the kind of non-realizability phenomenon in Theorem 0.2(1). The paper also suggests, but does not develop, extending the notion of effectively closed subgroup from S∞ to more general computable Polish group presentations.
why_relevant: Automorphism groups of countable structures are closed subgroups of the infinite symmetric group, and computable presentations make them effectively closed. Results about computable elements, presentations, and complexity of such subgroups are directly applicable to computably AUT-countable structures.

title: Simple Structures with Complex Symmetry
why_relevant: Introduces and develops automorphism spectra of computable structures. Since the problem is about the computability of all automorphisms in all copies on a cone, its realization and non-realization results for automorphism degrees are directly relevant.

title: Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures
overall_summary: The paper isolates and studies existentially atomic structures: countable structures in which every finite tuple has its automorphism orbit defined by a first-order existential formula. It proves that this model-theoretic condition is equivalent to several descriptive and computability-theoretic simplicity properties, including having an infinitary Π2 Scott sentence, uniform continuous categoricity, support of realized universal types by existential formulas, and a one-quantifier version of primeness. The effective version identifies computable structures with c.e. existential Scott families with uniformly relatively computably categorical structures and uniformly effectively 1-prime structures; with finite parameters, existential atomicity is equivalent to having an infinitary Σ3 Scott sentence and to computable categoricity on a cone. The main proof tools are back-and-forth relations generated by existential orbit formulas, a type-omitting theorem for unsupported universal types in Π2 classes, and a finite-use/permutation argument extracting existential definitions from uniform computable operators. For the original problem, the paper is most useful as a bridge between computability-on-a-cone phenomena and Σ1-in-style existential definability of orbits or graphs, and its Lemma 6.2 gives a particularly relevant template for turning uniform computability of maps into existential definitions by analyzing finite oracle use.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Scott-family observation from Definitions 1.1, 1.5, and 1.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A, let orb_A(a) be the set of tuples b of the same length as a such that some automorphism of A maps a to b. A is existentially atomic, meaning that for every finite tuple a from A there is a first-order existential formula φ_a(x) whose realizations in A are exactly orb_A(a), if and only if A has a Scott family consisting of existential formulas. Here a Scott family is a set S of formulas such that every finite tuple from A satisfies some formula in S, and any two tuples satisfying the same formula in S are automorphic in A. A computable structure is effectively existentially atomic if it has a c.e. Scott family of existential formulas, where c.e. refers to the set of indices for the formulas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If each orbit orb_A(a) is defined by an existential formula φ_a, then the collection of all such formulas is a Scott family: every tuple a satisfies φ_a, and if two tuples satisfy φ_a then both are in the orbit of a, hence are automorphic. Conversely, suppose S is a Scott family of existential formulas. Given a tuple a, choose θ in S with A models θ(a). If b satisfies θ, the Scott-family property gives an automorphism sending a to b. If b is automorphic to a, then b satisfies θ because formulas without parameters are invariant under automorphisms. Thus θ defines exactly orb_A(a). The effective version is just the same equivalence with the additional requirement that the existential Scott family be computably enumerable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure. The following are equivalent. (A1) A is existentially atomic: every finite tuple a has its automorphism orbit orb_A(a) defined in A by a first-order existential formula. (A2) A has an infinitary Π2 Scott sentence, i.e. an L_{ω1,ω} Scott sentence of Π2 complexity whose countable models are exactly the copies of A. (A3) A is uniformly continuously categorical: there is a continuous operator Φ from atomic diagrams of copies B of A to functions ω to ω such that Φ^{D(B)} is an isomorphism from B to A. (A4) Every first-order type realized in A is existentially supported in A: for every realized type p(x), there is an existential formula φ(x), realized in A, such that A models ∀x(φ(x) implies ψ(x)) for every ψ in p. (A5) Every universal type realized in A is existentially supported in A, in the same sense but only for types consisting of universal formulas. (A6) A is 1-prime: for every countable model B of the ∀2-theory of A, there is an embedding f from A into B that preserves universal formulas; the ∀2-theory consists of all sentences of the form ∀y ψ(y), with ψ existential, true in A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implications A1 implies A4 and A4 implies A5 are immediate: if an existential formula defines the automorphism orbit of a tuple a, then every formula true of a is implied by that orbit formula inside A; universal types are special cases of first-order types. The nontrivial implication A5 implies A1 is proved in Section 4 by choosing, for each tuple a, an existential formula φ_a supporting its universal type and then showing that I_A = {(a,b): A models φ_a(b)} has the back-and-forth property; this forces tuples satisfying φ_a to be automorphic to a. The implication A1 implies A2 is Lemma 7.1: use the existential orbit formulas to write a Π2 Scott sentence asserting that the relation I_B = {(a,b): B models φ_a(b)} has the back-and-forth property. The converse A2 implies A1 uses Lemma 5.3: if A were not existentially atomic, then by A1 implies A5 there would be a realized universal type not existentially supported; omitting that type inside the Π2 Scott class would produce a countable model of the Scott sentence not isomorphic to A. The equivalence A1 iff A6 is Lemma 6.1: existential orbit formulas let one build a universal-formula-preserving embedding into any model of the ∀2-theory, while failure of existential atomicity yields, via type omission, a model of the ∀2-theory omitting a universal type realized in A, so no such embedding can exist. The equivalence with A3 is stated as part of the theorem; the paper notes that Melnikov and Montalbán proved the equivalence between A2 and A3 in a more general Polish-group-action setting as an easy consequence of Effros's theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A and B are countable existentially algebraic structures with the same existential theories, then A and B are isomorphic. An element a of a structure A is existentially algebraic if there is an existential formula φ(x) true of a such that the set {b in A: A models φ(b)} is finite; A is existentially algebraic if every element of A is existentially algebraic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Enumerate A as a_0, a_1, ... . For each n choose an existential formula φ_n(x_0,...,x_{n-1}) true of the tuple a_0,...,a_{n-1}, with only finitely many solutions in A, and implying φ_{n-1} on the initial coordinates. Such formulas are obtained by combining finite-solution existential formulas for the individual entries. Define a tree T of finite tuples b from B by putting b of length n into T iff the finite atomic diagram of b in B agrees with that of a_0,...,a_{n-1} in A and B models φ_n(b). The tree is finitely branching because each φ_n has finitely many solutions. It is infinite because, for each n, A satisfies the existential sentence saying that there is a tuple with the relevant atomic diagram and satisfying φ_n; since A and B have the same existential theory, B satisfies the same sentence. By König's lemma T has an infinite path P. The map a_n ↦ P(n) preserves all finite atomic diagrams, so it is an embedding of A into B. To see it is onto, take b in B and an existential formula θ with finitely many solutions in B and true of b. The number of solutions to θ is determined by the existential theory via sentences asserting at least k distinct solutions; hence A and B have the same finite number of θ-solutions. Embeddings preserve existential formulas, so the image already accounts for all θ-solutions in B, and b must be in the image.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every existentially algebraic countable structure is existentially atomic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a finite tuple a in an existentially algebraic structure A. Choose an existential formula φ(x) true of a with the least possible finite number k of solutions in A. The claim is that φ defines the automorphism orbit of a. Suppose b is another solution of φ but b is not automorphic to a. Then the expanded structures (A,a) and (A,b) are both existentially algebraic and not isomorphic, so by Lemma 3.3 they cannot have the same existential theory. Hence some existential formula ψ holds of one of a,b but not the other. If ψ holds of a and not of b, then φ ∧ ψ is an existential formula true of a with fewer than k solutions, contradicting minimality. If ψ holds of b and not of a, let i be the finite number of solutions of φ ∧ ψ. The formula saying φ(y) and there exist i distinct solutions to φ ∧ ψ all different from y is existential, is true of a, and has exactly k − i solutions among the φ-solutions, again contradicting minimality. Therefore every solution of φ is automorphic to a, so φ is an existential orbit definition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Back-and-forth observation after Definition 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be countable structures. A set I ⊆ A^{<ω} × B^{<ω} has the back-and-forth property if for every pair (a,b) in I, the tuples a and b have the same finite atomic diagram, for every c in A there is d in B with (a c, b d) in I, and for every d in B there is c in A with (a c, b d) in I. If I has the back-and-forth property and (a,b) is in I, then there is an isomorphism from A to B mapping a to b. If I is c.e. in the relevant presentations, the isomorphism can be constructed computably.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Starting with the finite partial map sending a to b, alternately satisfy domain and range requirements. At a domain step, for the next element c of A not yet in the domain, use the forth condition to extend the current tuple pair to (a c, b d). At a range step, for the next element d of B not yet in the range, use the back condition to find a matching c. The atomic-diagram condition ensures that every finite partial map constructed is a partial isomorphism. The union of the increasing finite partial isomorphisms is a total bijective isomorphism. If I is c.e., one searches effectively for the required witnesses at each step.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4 proposition: A5 implies A1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If every universal type realized in a countable structure A is existentially supported in A, then A is existentially atomic. More explicitly, if for every finite tuple a there is an existential formula φ_a(x) that is realized in A and implies every universal formula true of a inside A, then the formulas φ_a can be chosen so that each φ_a defines the automorphism orbit of a.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each tuple a choose an existential formula φ_a supporting the universal type of a. First, A models φ_a(a): otherwise ¬φ_a, which is universal because φ_a is existential, would belong to the universal type of a and would be implied by φ_a, contradicting realizability of φ_a. Next prove symmetry: if A models φ_a(b), then A models φ_b(a). If not, ¬φ_b is in the universal type of a, so φ_a implies ¬φ_b; but b satisfies φ_a and, by the previous fact, b also satisfies φ_b. Define I_A = {(a,b): A models φ_a(b)}. If (a,b) is in I_A, then a and b satisfy the same universal formulas, using support and symmetry; hence they have the same atomic diagram. For the forth property, fix c in A. If no d satisfies φ_{a c}(b,d), then A models ¬∃y φ_{a c}(b,y), a universal formula in the universal type of b. Since φ_b supports that type and φ_b(a) holds by symmetry, A would model ¬∃y φ_{a c}(a,y), contradicting the witness c and the fact that φ_{a c}(a,c) holds. The back property follows symmetrically. Therefore I_A has the back-and-forth property, and any b satisfying φ_a is automorphic to a. Thus the φ_a form existential orbit definitions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume, without loss of generality, that the vocabulary is relational. Let K be a Πc_2 class of structures, meaning K is axiomatized by a computable infinitary Π2 sentence, and let K_fin = {D_A(a): A in K and a is a finite tuple from A} be the set of finite atomic diagrams realized in members of K. If K_fin is c.e., then K contains a computable structure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write the Πc_2 axiom for K as a computable conjunction over i of sentences ∀y_i ψ_i(y_i), where each ψ_i is a computable infinitary Σ1 formula. Build a nested sequence of finite partial diagrams σ_s in K_fin; let A_s be the corresponding finite partial structure. At stage s+1, extend A_s to A_{s+1} so that for every i < s and every tuple a from A_s of the right arity, A_{s+1} already contains finite witnesses making ψ_i(a) true. Such an extension can be found by searching the c.e. set K_fin. Existence of the desired extension follows because A_s is in K_fin, so it appears as a finite substructure of some B in K; since B satisfies all ∀y_i ψ_i(y_i), finitely many witnesses in B suffice for the finitely many current requirements, and a finite substructure containing them gives an element of K_fin. The limit atomic diagram D(A) = union_s σ_s is computable because the construction is effective and nested, and A satisfies every Π2 requirement because each tuple and each i is eventually handled.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Πc_2 class of structures, and let S be the existential theory of some structure in K. If S is c.e. in an oracle X, then there is an X-computable presentation of a structure in K whose existential theory is exactly S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Expand the Πc_2 axiomatization of K by a Πc,X_2 condition forcing the existential theory to be S. First include every existential sentence in S as a required sentence. Second, for every finite tuple x, require that its finite atomic diagram be one whose existential realization sentence belongs to S; formally this is expressed using a disjunction over finite atomic diagrams σ such that the existential sentence saying some tuple realizes σ is in S. Call the resulting class K_S. It is nonempty because S came from a member of K, and its finite diagrams are enumeration reducible to S, hence c.e. in X. Applying Lemma 5.1 relative to X yields an X-computable model in K_S, which has precisely the existential theory S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Πin_2 class of structures, and let {p_i(x_i): i in ω} be a sequence of universal types, each not existentially supported in K. Then there is a structure A in K omitting every type p_i. Moreover, if K is Πc_2, K_fin is c.e., and the list of the types p_i is c.e., then A can be chosen computable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Construct a structure by finite approximations as in Lemma 5.1. Even stages meet the Π2 axioms of K by adding finite witnesses for the finitely many currently relevant Σ1 requirements. Odd stages diagonalize against the types. At an odd stage assigned to the pair (i,j), take the j-th tuple a in the current finite approximation A_s and write the rest of A_s as b. Let σ be the finite atomic diagram D_{A_s}(a,b), so a satisfies the existential formula ∃y φ^at_σ(a,y) in the current approximation. Because p_i is not existentially supported in K, some universal formula ψ in p_i is not implied within K by this existential formula. Hence there is a member of K with a tuple realizing ∃y φ^at_σ but failing ψ; by taking a suitable finite substructure and renaming, obtain a finite extension B of A_s in K_fin in which a already has a finite witness to ¬ψ. Set A_{s+1}=B. Since ψ is universal, its failure is existentially witnessed and persists to the limit. In the effective case, one searches through the c.e. list of formulas in p_i and the c.e. set K_fin until such a finite extension is found.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A countable structure A is existentially atomic if and only if it is 1-prime. Here A is 1-prime if for every countable model B of the ∀2-theory of A there is an embedding f:A→B preserving universal formulas, called a 1-embedding.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume A is existentially atomic. Enumerate A as a_1,a_2,..., and let φ_s be an existential formula defining the orbit of the initial tuple a_1,...,a_s. Given a model B of the ∀2-theory of A, define f by stages, maintaining B models φ_s(f(a_1),...,f(a_s)). The extension from s to s+1 is justified because A satisfies the appropriate ∀2 sentence saying that any tuple satisfying φ_s can be extended to one satisfying φ_{s+1}; syntactically, implications from existential antecedents are rewritten by universally quantifying over witnesses to the antecedent, giving a ∀2 sentence. The resulting embedding preserves atomic facts because the orbit formulas imply the relevant finite atomic diagrams. It preserves universal formulas because if a universal formula ψ holds of a_1,...,a_s in A, then A satisfies the ∀2 consequence that φ_s implies ψ, and so B satisfies the same consequence. Conversely, if A is not existentially atomic, then by the previously proved implication from existential atomicity to support of universal types, some universal type realized by a tuple a in A is not existentially supported in A. Let K be the class of countable models of the ∀2-theory of A. Lemma 5.3 gives a model B of K omitting the universal type of a. Any 1-embedding A→B would preserve that universal type and thus would have to send a to a realization of it in B, impossible. Hence A is not 1-prime.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a computable structure A, A is effectively existentially atomic if and only if A is uniformly effectively 1-prime. Uniformly effectively 1-prime means that there is a computable operator Φ such that for every computable model B of the ∀2-theory of A, Φ^{D(B)} is a 1-embedding from A into B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the left-to-right direction, use a c.e. existential Scott family for A to carry out the construction from Lemma 6.1 uniformly in the atomic diagram D(B). At each stage search for an existential orbit formula for the next initial segment and for a tuple in B satisfying it; the ∀2-theory guarantees success. This yields a 1-embedding computable uniformly from D(B). For the right-to-left direction, let Φ witness uniform effective 1-primeness. Apply Φ to D(A), obtaining a 1-embedding of A into itself. For an initial segment a = (a_0,...,a_{m-1}) of the fixed computable enumeration of A, the proof chooses a tuple ã with Φ^{D(A)}(ã) = (0,1,...,m−1). Let s be a finite use of the computation on ã, and let c = (a_m,...,a_{s-1}) so that a c is the first s elements. Define an existential formula φ_a(x) as ∃y φ^at_{D(A) restricted to s}(x,y), where φ^at encodes the finite atomic diagram of the first s elements of A. If b in A satisfies φ_a, take witnesses d such that b d has the same finite atomic diagram as a c. Form a new presentation Ã of A by permuting a c to b d and leaving the rest fixed. Since D(Ã) agrees with D(A) on the first s bits used by the computation, Φ^{D(Ã)}(ã) has the same numerical value as Φ^{D(A)}(ã), but in the new presentation that value denotes b rather than a. Because both Φ^{D(A)} and Φ^{D(Ã)} preserve universal types, a and b have the same universal type. Thus φ_a supports the universal type of a. Uniformly over initial segments, this gives a c.e. family of existential formulas supporting universal types, hence by the Section 4 argument a c.e. existential Scott family.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every existentially atomic countable structure has an infinitary Π2 Scott sentence. Moreover, every effectively existentially atomic computable structure has a computable infinitary Πc_2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let S be an existential Scott family for A, and choose for each finite tuple a an existential formula φ_a defining its orbit. For any structure B, define I_B = {(a,b): b is a tuple from B of the same length as a and B models φ_a(b)}. Since the analogous relation I_A has the back-and-forth property, B is isomorphic to A exactly when I_B has the back-and-forth property. Write a single infinitary sentence asserting this: for every finite tuple a from A and every tuple x in the candidate structure, if φ_a(x) holds, then x has the same finite atomic diagram as a, every element y of the candidate can be matched by some c in A with φ_{a c}(x y), and for every c in A there exists y in the candidate with φ_{a c}(x y). Because the φ_a are existential, this back-and-forth assertion has Π2 infinitary complexity. In the computable effective case, A is computable and the existential Scott family is c.e.; by searching for a formula in the family true of each tuple, one can choose the map a ↦ φ_a computably enough to make the displayed infinitary conjunctions and disjunctions computable, yielding a Πc_2 Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proof of A2 implies A1 in Section 7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a countable structure A has an infinitary Π2 Scott sentence, then A is existentially atomic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let ψ be a Πin_2 Scott sentence for A. Suppose A is not existentially atomic. Since existential atomicity implies existential support of all realized universal types, some universal type p realized in A is not existentially supported. Apply Lemma 5.3 to the Πin_2 class of models of ψ and the single type p. This yields a countable model B of ψ omitting p. But ψ is a Scott sentence, so every countable model of ψ must be isomorphic to A; an isomorphic copy of A realizes exactly the same universal types as A, including p. This contradiction shows that no such unsupported p exists, and hence A is existentially atomic by the Section 4 proposition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A, the following are equivalent: (1) A is existentially atomic over a finite tuple of parameters, meaning that some finite expansion (A,a) by constants for a tuple a is existentially atomic; (2) A has an infinitary Σ3 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If A is existentially atomic over a finite tuple a, then by Lemma 7.1 the expanded structure (A,a) has a Πin_2 Scott sentence θ(c) in the language with constants c for the tuple. Then ∃y θ(y) is a Σin_3 Scott sentence for the reduct A, since it asserts that some tuple names parameters making the expansion a copy of (A,a). Conversely, suppose A has a Σin_3 Scott sentence. Write it as a countable disjunction of sentences ∃y_i ψ_i(y_i), with each ψ_i Πin_2. Since A satisfies one disjunct, that single disjunct is itself a Scott sentence for A. Fix a tuple a in A witnessing A models ψ(a), and add constant symbols c of the same length. The sentence ψ(c) need not be a Scott sentence for the expansion (A,a), because different choices of witnesses may lie in different automorphism orbits. However, every countable model of ψ(c), after forgetting c, is a copy of A, since ∃y ψ(y) is a Scott sentence. Thus, up to isomorphism of expansions by constants, there are only countably many such models, corresponding to countably many tuples in copies of A, and hence only countably many universal types realized among them. Use Lemma 5.3 inside the Π2 class of models of ψ(c) to omit all universal types in this class that are not existentially supported. The resulting model still satisfies ψ(c), so its reduct is isomorphic to A, and all its realized universal types over c are existentially supported. By the Section 4 proposition, this expansion is existentially atomic over c. Transporting the constants through an isomorphism gives that A is existentially atomic over a finite tuple of parameters.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure. The following are equivalent. (B1) A is existentially atomic over a finite set of parameters. (B2) A has an infinitary Σ3 Scott sentence. (B3) A is computably categorical on a cone: there is an oracle C such that, for every oracle X with X ≥_T C, every X-computable copy of A is X-computably isomorphic to A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence B1 iff B2 is Lemma 7.2. The implication B1 implies B2 is obtained by taking a Π2 Scott sentence for the finite-parameter expansion and existentially quantifying over the parameters. The converse uses the Π2 component of a satisfied Σ3 disjunct, expands by constants for a witness tuple, and applies type omission to get an existentially atomic expansion. The equivalence B1 iff B3 is cited as the boldface, or cone-relativized, version of the Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm theorem characterizing relative computable categoricity by effective existential atomicity over finitely many parameters.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure. The following are equivalent. (C1) A is effectively existentially atomic, i.e. has a c.e. Scott family of existential formulas. (C2) A is uniformly relatively computably categorical: there is a computable operator Φ such that for every copy B of A, Φ^{D(B)} is an isomorphism from B to A. (C3) A is uniformly computably categorical in the sense that the same kind of computable operator is required to work for computable copies B of A when given their atomic diagrams as oracles. (C4) A is uniformly effectively 1-prime: there is a computable operator that, for every computable model B of the ∀2-theory of A, outputs a 1-embedding A→B from D(B).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper attributes the equivalence of C1, C2, and C3 to Ventsov. The idea is that a c.e. existential Scott family allows a uniform effective back-and-forth construction of isomorphisms from any copy to A, while a uniform procedure for producing such isomorphisms can be converted back into c.e. existential orbit information by finite-use analysis. The equivalence C1 iff C4 is proved directly in Lemma 6.2: from a c.e. existential Scott family one uniformly builds 1-embeddings into models of the ∀2-theory, and from a uniform 1-prime operator one extracts finite atomic-diagram conditions that existentially support universal types and hence form a c.e. existential Scott family.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Πc_2 class of structures such that no two non-isomorphic structures in K have the same existential theory. Then every structure A in K has enumeration degree given by its existential theory S = ∃-Th(A). Here A has enumeration degree S if every enumeration of S computes a copy of A and every copy of A computes an enumeration of S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Every copy of A can enumerate S by listing the existential sentences true in that copy. Conversely, suppose an oracle X computes an enumeration of S. By Corollary 5.2, X computes a presentation of some B in K whose existential theory is S. By the hypothesis on K, B is isomorphic to A. Therefore exactly the oracles enumerating S compute copies of A, so S is the enumeration degree of A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A and B are existentially atomic countable structures with the same existential theory, then A and B are isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define I to be the set of pairs of finite tuples (a,b), with a from A and b from B of the same length, such that a and b have the same universal type in their respective structures. The empty tuples lie in I because the structures have the same existential theory and hence the same universal theory at the sentence level. If (a,b) is in I, then a and b have the same atomic diagram. For the forth step, take c in A. Let ψ(x,y) be an existential formula defining or supporting the orbit, equivalently the universal type, of the tuple a c in A. Since a and b have the same universal type, B cannot satisfy the universal formula ¬∃y ψ(b,y); otherwise A would satisfy ¬∃y ψ(a,y), contradicting c. Hence choose d in B with B models ψ(b,d). To prove (a c,b d) is in I, let θ(x,y) be any universal formula true of a c in A. In A, the formula ∀y(ψ(a,y) implies θ(a,y)) holds; after rewriting the implication from the existential ψ, this is a universal formula about a, so it also holds of b in B. Since B models ψ(b,d), B models θ(b,d). This gives one inclusion of universal types. For the reverse inclusion, take an existential formula ψ̃ generating the universal type of b d in B. The first inclusion implies that A models ψ̃(a,c), and the same argument with A and B interchanged yields the reverse inclusion. Thus I has the back-and-forth property, so A and B are isomorphic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Πc_2 class all of whose structures are existentially atomic. Then every structure A in K has an enumeration degree, and that enumeration degree is given by the existential theory ∃-Th(A).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 8.2, any two existentially atomic structures with the same existential theory are isomorphic. Therefore, within K, the existential theory determines the isomorphism type. Lemma 8.1 then applies: every copy of A enumerates ∃-Th(A), and any enumeration of ∃-Th(A) computes, via Corollary 5.2, a copy of a structure in K with the same existential theory, hence a copy of A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 8.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a countable structure A has a computable infinitary Σc_3 Scott sentence, then A has an enumeration degree.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write the Σc_3 Scott sentence as a computable disjunction of sentences ∃x_i ψ_i(x_i), each ψ_i Πc_2. Since A satisfies one disjunct, that disjunct is itself a Scott sentence for A. Choose a tuple a in A witnessing ψ_i(a), expand the language by constants c for that tuple, and consider the Πc_2 condition ψ_i(c). The paper claims A has enumeration degree given by the existential type of a, equivalently the existential theory of the expansion (A,a). Every copy of A can enumerate this existential type. Conversely, from an enumeration of it, Corollary 5.2 builds a model of ψ_i(c) with that existential theory. Even if the resulting expansion is not isomorphic to (A,a), its reduct satisfies the Scott sentence ∃x_i ψ_i(x_i), so the reduct is isomorphic to A.

proof_techniques.name: Back-and-forth from existential orbit formulas
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly turns existential orbit definitions into isomorphisms by defining, for a candidate structure B, a relation I_B between tuples of the original structure A and tuples of B: (a,b) belongs to I_B iff B satisfies the existential formula φ_a that defines the orbit of a in A. The Scott sentence construction and several categoricity arguments then reduce to verifying that I_B has the back-and-forth property: matching atomic diagrams and extending on both sides by one element. Because existential formulas are upwardly witnessed by finite pieces of the atomic diagram, the resulting back-and-forth requirements have low infinitary complexity, typically Π2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A graph of an automorphism can often be described by saying that certain parameter-augmented tuples lie in the same orbit. If one can obtain existential orbit definitions over finite parameters a ∪ π(a), this technique is a route to Σ1-in definitions of automorphism graphs; conversely, failure of such orbit definitions is a natural obstruction to definability.

proof_techniques.name: Supporting universal types instead of defining full orbits directly
proof_techniques.description: Rather than constructing orbit formulas outright, Section 4 proves that it is enough to find, for every tuple a, an existential formula φ_a that supports the universal type of a, meaning that φ_a implies every universal formula true of a. The proof derives orbit definitions by showing that the relation A models φ_a(b) is symmetric and has the back-and-forth extension property.
