This converts a type-support condition into existential atomicity without first knowing that the formulas isolate complete first-order types.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a proposed structure satisfying an automorphism-computability hypothesis, it may be easier to show that finite parameter tuples have existentially supported universal types than to define full automorphism orbits. This would still be enough, by the paper's argument, to obtain existential orbit definitions that may feed into Σ1-in definitions of maps.

proof_techniques.name: Type omission for unsupported universal types in Π2 classes
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5.3 gives a constructive type-omitting theorem tailored to universal types and Π2 classes. The construction builds a model by finite approximations, alternating between satisfying the Π2 axioms and diagonalizing against a listed type on a listed tuple. The key step is that if a universal type p is not existentially supported, then any current existential finite-diagram condition can be extended inside the class to force the failure of some universal formula in p; since the failure of a universal formula is existential, it is finitely witnessed and persists to the limit.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful contrapositive tool: if some tuple or parameter configuration lacks existential support, one can often build another copy or expansion omitting its universal type. Such a construction may be used to contradict a cone-categoricity or uniform automorphism-computability assumption.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-use/permutation extraction from uniform computable operators
proof_techniques.description: The right-to-left direction of Lemma 6.2 is one of the paper's most transferable arguments. Given a uniform computable operator producing embeddings, fix a computation on a tuple and record its finite oracle use s. The finite initial atomic diagram seen by the computation becomes an existential formula: another tuple satisfying that finite diagram can be moved into the first s positions by a computable permutation of the presentation, and the computation cannot distinguish the two presentations. Because the operator's outputs preserve universal types, the two tuples must share a universal type; hence the finite diagram formula existentially supports that type.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks about computability of automorphisms relative to presentations on a cone versus Σ1-in definability of automorphisms. This finite-use method is a direct template for converting a uniform procedure that computes maps from diagrams into existential formulas defining or supporting the relevant graph or orbit data, especially after permuting finite tuples in a copy.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-parameter expansion versus Σ3 Scott sentence
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses a clean parameter tradeoff: if an expansion (A,a) by finitely many constants is existentially atomic, then its Π2 Scott sentence becomes a Σ3 Scott sentence for A after existentially quantifying over the constants. Conversely, from a Σ3 Scott sentence, one chooses a satisfied Π2 disjunct with witness tuple, expands by constants for that tuple, and uses type omission to find an expansion in which all realized universal types are existentially supported.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original question quantifies over finite parameter sets and their images under automorphisms. This theorem suggests analyzing finite-parameter expansions of A and asking whether the relevant computability-on-a-cone property implies a Σ3 Scott description or existential atomicity for such expansions.

proof_techniques.name: Existential algebraicity via minimal finite solution formulas
proof_techniques.description: For existentially algebraic structures, Lemma 3.4 defines tuple orbits by choosing an existential formula true of the tuple with the minimum possible finite number of solutions. If another solution is not automorphic, Lemma 3.3 finds an existential formula distinguishing the two expanded structures, and one combines formulas to reduce the number of solutions, contradicting minimality. This is a finite-combinatorial way to get existential orbit definitions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Existentially algebraic structures provide a robust supply of examples where automorphism orbits are existentially definable. They may serve either as positive test cases for proposed implications or as components in constructions where one wants tightly controlled local orbits.

proof_techniques.name: Computable model construction from c.e. finite diagrams
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5.1 constructs a computable model of a Πc_2 class K when the finite diagrams K_fin realized in K are c.e. The construction searches for finite extensions that meet more and more Π2 requirements, relying on the fact that any current finite diagram comes from an actual model of K. Corollary 5.2 refines this by adding Π2 axioms that force a prescribed existential theory S, yielding an X-computable model when S is c.e. in X.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a way to build computable or oracle-computable copies with controlled existential theory. In attempts to separate computability of automorphisms from Σ1-in definability, it may be useful for producing adversarial copies that preserve existential information while changing the behavior of automorphisms or parameter tuples.

proof_techniques.name: Existential theory as an isomorphism invariant for existentially atomic classes
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 8.2 shows that two existentially atomic structures with the same existential theory are isomorphic. The proof again uses a back-and-forth relation, but now tuples are matched by equality of universal types; existential atomicity supplies the formulas needed for the extension steps. Theorem 1.14 packages this into an enumeration-degree result for Πc_2 classes of existentially atomic structures.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate construction is existentially atomic, then its existential theory may determine its entire isomorphism type. This can simplify cone arguments by reducing copies to enumerations of existential facts, but it also indicates that a counterexample to definability may need to exploit automorphism-level complexity not visible at the level of isomorphism type.

other_useful_info: Examples stated in the paper: a linear ordering is existentially atomic iff it is dense or finite; a field is existentially atomic iff it is algebraic over its prime subfield; algebraic fields, connected finite-valence graphs with a named root, and finite-dimensional torsion-free abelian groups with a named basis are existentially algebraic and hence existentially atomic. The introduction notes that every structure becomes existentially atomic after taking enough structural jumps: for Scott rank α, the (<α)-jump obtained by adding relations for computable infinitary Σβ formulas for β < α is existentially atomic relative to a suitable oracle. The paper cites Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm for the characterization of relative computable categoricity by effective existential atomicity over finitely many parameters, Ventsov for uniform computable categoricity, Cholak-McCoy for effective prime uniqueness, and Melnikov-Montalbán/Effros for the Polish-group-action perspective on Π2 Scott sentences and continuous categoricity.
why_relevant: Directly studies structures whose automorphism orbits of tuples are definable by existential formulas, the core model-theoretic notion behind Sigma-in-1 definability. Its equivalences with Pi-2 Scott sentences and effective categoricity are likely central to proving or refuting the proposed separation.

title: A Robuster Scott Rank
overall_summary: The paper introduces the categoricity Scott rank of a countable structure, defined as the least ordinal α such that every automorphism orbit of a finite tuple is parameter-free Σ^in_α-definable. Its main theorem proves a robust equivalence between this orbit-definability condition, existence of a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence, uniform boldface Δ^0_α-categoricity, Borel complexity of the isomorphism class, supported Π^in_α-types, a weak Π^in_{α+1} axiomatization condition, and absence of α-free tuples. The central new infinitary-logic tool is a sharply calibrated type-omitting lemma: a partial Π^in_α-type not supported by a Σ^in_α formula inside A can be omitted in a model of any Π^in_{α+1} sentence true of A. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the α = 1 case, which connects existential infinitary definability of tuple orbits, Π^in_2 Scott sentences, and uniform continuous categoricity, and the parameterized corollary characterizing computable categoricity on a cone by Σ^in_3 Scott sentences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1 and Theorem 1.1 continued
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let α < ω_1 be a countable ordinal. In the infinitary logic L_{ω_1,ω}, write Σ^in_α and Π^in_α for the usual infinitary hierarchy. For a finite tuple a from A, its automorphism orbit is Orb_A(a) = {b : b has the same length as a and some automorphism of A sends a to b}. For tuples u,v of the same length, write u ≤_γ v iff Π^in_γ-tp_A(u) ⊆ Π^in_γ-tp_A(v), equivalently every Π^in_γ formula true of u in A is true of v in A. A Π^in_α-type p(x) realized in A is Σ^in_α-supported within A if there is a Σ^in_α formula θ(x) such that A ⊨ ∃x θ(x) and, for every ψ(x) in p, A ⊨ ∀x(θ(x) → ψ(x)). A tuple a is α-free if for every β < α and every finite tuple d from A there are finite tuples a′,d′ such that a d ≤_β a′ d′ but a is not ≤_α a′. Then the following conditions are equivalent. (U1) Every automorphism orbit of every finite tuple of A is definable without parameters by a Σ^in_α formula. (U2) A has a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence, meaning a Π^in_{α+1} sentence whose only countable models are isomorphic to A. (U3) A is uniformly boldface Δ^0_α-categorical: there is a boldface Δ^0_α operator Θ such that, for every presentation B of A, Θ(B) is an isomorphism from a fixed copy of A to B. The paper recalls that a Δ^0_α operator Θ is one for which {(X,n,i) ∈ 2^ω × ω × 2 : Θ(X)(n)=i} is Δ^0_α, equivalently Θ is obtained continuously from the appropriate α-th jump input, with the finite-ordinal convention stated in the paper. (U4) The set of presentations of A, viewed as a set of reals coding countable structures, is Π^0_{α+1} in the Borel hierarchy. (U5) Every Π^in_α-type realized in A is Σ^in_α-supported within A. (U6) There is a Π^in_{α+1} sentence φ true of A such that every structure B satisfying φ is Σ^in_{α+1}-elementarily equivalent to A, equivalently B ≡_{α+1} A in the α+1 back-and-forth sense. (U7) No tuple in A is α-free.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper proves the infinitary-logic core by the chain (U1) ⇒ (U2) ⇒ (U6) ⇒ (U5) ⇒ (U1), and obtains the other equivalences from known results. For (U1) ⇒ (U2), choose for each finite tuple a of A a Σ^in_α formula φ_a defining its orbit. The standard Scott sentence built from this Scott family is the conjunction, over all finite tuples a, of extension axioms saying that any tuple x satisfying φ_a can be extended exactly as a can: for every b ∈ A there exists y satisfying φ_ab(x,y), and every y is covered by some φ_ab(x,y). Explicitly, the displayed sentence in the paper has conjuncts ∀x(φ_a(x) → ((∧_{b∈A} ∃y φ_ab(x,y)) & (∀y ∨_{b∈A} φ_ab(x,y)))). Counting infinitary quantifier alternations gives a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence. The implication (U2) ⇒ (U6) is immediate because a Scott sentence is stronger than merely forcing α+1-elementary equivalence to A. For (U6) ⇒ (U5), suppose p is a realized Π^in_α-type in A that is not Σ^in_α-supported. Apply Lemma 3.2 to the Π^in_{α+1} sentence φ from (U6) and to p, obtaining a countable B ⊨ φ that omits p. Since B ⊨ φ, (U6) gives B ≡_{α+1} A. Because B is countable, choose a countable subset q ⊆ p still omitted by B, one formula from p failing at each tuple of B. The sentence ∃x ∧_{ψ∈q} ψ(x) is Σ^in_{α+1}; it is true in A because p is realized there and false in B because q is omitted, contradicting B ≡_{α+1} A. For (U5) ⇒ (U1), for every tuple a choose a Σ^in_α support φ_a for Π^in_α-tp_A(a). First φ_a(a) must hold; otherwise ¬φ_a would be in the Π^in_α-type of a and would be implied by φ_a, contradicting realizability of φ_a. Second, if A ⊨ φ_a(b), then A ⊨ φ_b(a): otherwise ¬φ_b belongs to Π^in_α-tp_A(a), so φ_a implies ¬φ_b, contradicting A ⊨ φ_a(b) and A ⊨ φ_b(b). Let P = {(a,b) : A ⊨ φ_a(b)}. The previous symmetry observation and a support argument show P has the back-and-forth property. Namely, if P(a,b) and d ∈ A, suppose no c has P(ac,bd). Then A ⊨ ¬∃y φ_bd(a,y), so the Π^in_α formula ∀y ¬φ_bd(a,y) lies in the Π^in_α-type of a and hence is implied by φ_a. Evaluating at b contradicts φ_bd(b,d). The reverse extension direction follows by symmetry. Thus any b satisfying φ_a is automorphic to a, so φ_a defines the orbit of a. The equivalence (U2) ⇔ (U4) is attributed to Lopez-Escobar's theorem: an isomorphism-invariant class of presentations is Π^0_β Borel iff it is Π^in_β-axiomatizable. The equivalence (U1) ⇔ (U3) follows by combining Ash's characterization of uniformly lightface Δ^0_α-categoricity by Σ^c_α Scott families with relativization to a cone/boldface setting. The equivalence (U1) ⇔ (U7) is obtained from Ash and Knight's α-freeness characterization, using that every structure is α-friendly relative to a sufficiently large oracle and that the parameter-free version has the same proof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2, Type omitting lemma
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a structure, let α be a countable ordinal, and let φ be a Π^in_{α+1} sentence true of A. Let Φ(x) be a partial Π^in_α-type, meaning a set of Π^in_α formulas in the tuple of variables x, and suppose Φ is not Σ^in_α-supported in A: there is no Σ^in_α formula θ(x) such that A ⊨ ∃x θ(x) and A ⊨ ∀x(θ(x) → ψ(x)) for every ψ ∈ Φ. Then there exists a countable structure B such that B ⊨ φ and B omits Φ, i.e. no tuple of B satisfies all formulas in Φ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Put φ into the normal form ∧_j ∀y_j φ_j(y_j), with each φ_j a Σ^in_α formula. Expand the language by countably many new constants C = {c_0,c_1,...}. The proof builds a set S of Σ^in_α sentences in the expanded language satisfying Henkin-style closure and consistency conditions: if a disjunction is in S then one disjunct is in S; if an existential sentence is in S then a constant-witness instance is in S; if a conjunction is in S then all conjuncts are in S; if a universal sentence is in S then all constant instances are in S; for every atomic sentence exactly one of it or its negation is in S; for every j and every tuple c of constants of the right length, φ_j(c) is in S; and for every tuple c of constants of the same length as x, some ψ ∈ Φ has ¬ψ(c) in S. The construction proceeds by stages with finite approximations S_s and assignments v_s sending the constants mentioned in S_s into A so that A satisfies every sentence in S_s under that assignment. The ordinary Henkin requirements for disjunctions, existentials, conjunctions, universals, atomic decisions, and the target conjuncts φ_j are met while extending the assignment in the obvious way, using truth in A to choose witnesses when needed. The key type-omitting step is as follows. Given a tuple c of constants and a current finite S_s, let d be the other constants appearing in S_s and let ρ(c,d) be the finite conjunction of S_s. Then ∃y ρ(x,y) is a Σ^in_α formula realized in A. Since Φ is not Σ^in_α-supported in A, some ψ(x) ∈ Φ is not forced by this formula inside A; hence there are tuples a,b in A with A ⊨ ρ(a,b) ∧ ¬ψ(a). Extend S_s by adding ¬ψ(c), and extend or reset the assignment so that c d maps to a b. This preserves the invariant that the finite set is realized in A. After all requirements are met, define B from the complete consistent atomic part of S in the usual Henkin way, using the constants as domain. A truth induction using the closure properties shows that B satisfies every sentence in S. The requirements forcing all φ_j(c) ensure B ⊨ φ, and the requirements putting some ¬ψ(c) into S for every tuple c ensure that B omits Φ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every countable structure A, the following are equivalent. (1) A is computably categorical on a cone: there exists an oracle Y ∈ 2^ω such that for every X ≥_T Y, A is X-computably categorical, meaning any two X-computable copies of A have an X-computable isomorphism. (2) A has a Σ^in_3 Scott sentence, i.e. a Σ^in_3 L_{ω_1,ω} sentence whose only countable models are isomorphic to A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper presents this as a corollary of Theorem 1.1 and the uniformity facts reviewed in Section 2.2. Ventsov's theorem, in its on-a-cone form, gives that computable categoricity on a cone is equivalent to uniform continuous categoricity after naming finitely many parameters. The α = 1 instance of Theorem 1.1 says that a structure is uniformly continuously categorical exactly when all tuple orbits are Σ^in_1-definable, equivalently when it has a Π^in_2 Scott sentence. Thus, after naming a finite tuple a, computable categoricity on a cone corresponds to the expansion (A,a) having categoricity Scott rank 1, or equivalently a Π^in_2 Scott sentence. The paper notes that A has a Σ^in_3 Scott sentence iff there is a finite tuple a such that (A,a) has a Π^in_2 Scott sentence; existentially quantifying the named parameters gives the forward complexity shift from Π^in_2 to Σ^in_3, and the converse is the corresponding parameter extraction for a countably categorical Σ^in_3 sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let α be a countable ordinal. The following are equivalent. (C1) A is Δ^0_α-categorical on a cone: for all sufficiently high oracles X, any two X-computable copies of A have an isomorphism of complexity Δ^0_α relative to X, in the usual relativized sense. (C2) A has a Σ^in_{α+2} Scott sentence. (C3) There is a finite tuple a from A such that the expanded structure (A,a), obtained by naming the entries of a as constants, has categoricity Scott rank at most α; equivalently, in (A,a), every automorphism orbit of a finite tuple is parameter-free Σ^in_α-definable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is stated as the transfinite generalization of Theorem 2.4. The direction from (C3) to (C2) uses Theorem 1.1: if (A,a) has categoricity Scott rank at most α, then (A,a) has a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence; replacing the constant symbols for a by existentially quantified variables yields a Σ^in_{α+2} Scott sentence for the reduct A. The equivalence between (C1) and (C3) is obtained by the same uniformity principles reviewed in Section 2.2: relative Δ^0_α-categoricity is equivalent, after adding finitely many parameters, to uniform lightface Δ^0_α-categoricity, and on a cone this becomes the boldface/uniform condition that Theorem 1.1 identifies with Σ^in_α orbit-definability. The converse from (C2) to the parameterized rank condition is stated by the paper as part of this expected transfinite correspondence between Σ^in_{α+2} Scott sentences and Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentences after naming finitely many parameters.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.2 uniformity and Scott-family facts used by the paper
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper reviews several known equivalences from computable structure theory. A Scott family for A is a collection of formulas such that every tuple from A satisfies some formula in the family and any two tuples satisfying the same formula are automorphic in A. Goncharov's theorem says that a structure is relatively computably categorical iff it has a computably enumerable Scott family of existential formulas. Ventsov's theorem says that a structure is relatively computably categorical iff it is uniformly computably categorical after adding finitely many parameters; the paper uses the on-a-cone version: A is computably categorical on a cone iff it is uniformly continuously categorical after adding finitely many parameters. For every countable ordinal α, Ash's theorem says that A is relatively Δ^0_α-categorical iff it is uniformly lightface Δ^0_α-categorical after adding parameters iff it has a Σ^c_α Scott family with parameters; moreover, the parameter-free version says that A is uniformly lightface Δ^0_α-categorical iff it has a parameter-free Σ^c_α Scott family. Considering these equivalences on a cone gives the equivalence between Theorem 1.1(U3) and Theorem 1.1(U1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These results are cited rather than reproved. The paper points to Goncharov for relative computable categoricity, Ventsov and Downey-Hirschfeldt-Khoussainov for uniformity after parameters, and Ash/Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman forcing as presented in Ash-Knight for Δ^0_α-categoricity and computably infinitary Scott families. The role of the paper is to combine these known lightface, parameterized results with relativization to sufficiently high oracles. On a cone, computably infinitary Σ^c_α definitions relative to the oracle correspond to boldface infinitary Σ^in_α definitions, yielding the parameter-free uniform boldface equivalence in Theorem 1.1.

proof_techniques.name: Sharp Henkin type omission at a fixed infinitary level
proof_techniques.description: The paper adapts the usual Henkin type-omitting construction to preserve exact infinitary complexity. Given a Π^in_{α+1} sentence true in A and a partial Π^in_α type Φ not supported by any Σ^in_α formula inside A, it builds a countable model of the sentence omitting Φ. The construction keeps finite consistency by assigning currently used constants into A; the non-support hypothesis is used exactly when handling a tuple of constants c, by taking the finite current condition ρ(c,d), observing that ∃y ρ(x,y) is a realized Σ^in_α formula, and choosing ψ ∈ Φ and a realization where ¬ψ holds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For questions about failure of Σ^in_1-definability, the α = 1 instance gives a way to convert non-support of existential definitions into an actual model/copy omitting a relevant Π^in_1 type. This can be useful for proving contrapositive statements: if a cone-computability or categoricity hypothesis prevents such omitting models, then certain existential supports or orbit definitions must exist.

proof_techniques.name: Building a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence from Σ^in_α orbit formulas
proof_techniques.description: If every tuple orbit has a Σ^in_α definition φ_a, the paper uses the standard Scott-family construction: for every tuple a, impose extension axioms saying that any tuple satisfying φ_a has exactly the same one-point extensions, up to the formulas φ_ab, as a has in A. Countable conjunction over tuples and the outer universal/extension form raise the complexity by one level, giving a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the α = 1 case, any attempt to show that naming certain parameters makes all relevant tuple orbits existentially definable can be upgraded to a Π^in_2 Scott sentence for that parameter expansion. This is a bridge from definability assumptions to strong categoricity consequences on a cone.

proof_techniques.name: Recovering orbit definitions from supported Π^in_α-types
proof_techniques.description: The proof of (U5) ⇒ (U1) is a useful back-and-forth argument. For each tuple a, choose a Σ^in_α formula φ_a supporting its full Π^in_α type. The support formulas are shown to be symmetric in the sense that φ_a(b) implies φ_b(a). Then the relation P(a,b) iff A ⊨ φ_a(b) has the back-and-forth property: if P(a,b) and d is an element on one side, failure to find a matching c would make a Π^in_α formula true of a and hence forced by φ_a, contradicting φ_bd(b,d). Countability then yields an automorphism, so φ_a actually defines the orbit of a.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique suggests reducing existential orbit-definability to support of Π^in_1 types in finite-parameter expansions. If one can identify a finite-parameter expansion where a relevant Π^in_1 type is unsupported, the theorem supplies a precise obstruction to Σ^in_1 orbit control.

proof_techniques.name: Relativizing lightface computable structure theory to a cone
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the idea that lightface computable infinitary notions become boldface infinitary notions after allowing a sufficiently high oracle. Known theorems requiring effectiveness hypotheses, such as α-friendliness or computably infinitary Scott families, can be applied relative to a large enough oracle, and then interpreted as on-a-cone or boldface statements. This is how the paper connects Σ^in_α orbit definability with uniform boldface Δ^0_α-categoricity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is explicitly on a cone. This technique indicates that one should look for invariant infinitary definitions or obstructions in the abstract structure, then relativize to a cone rather than trying to manage a particular computable presentation directly.

proof_techniques.name: Finite parameter expansion and existentially quantifying constants
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses finite parameter expansions to relate parameter-free rank conditions to Scott sentences one level higher in the Σ hierarchy. If (A,a) has a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence in the language with constants for a, then the reduct A has a Σ^in_{α+2} Scott sentence by replacing those constants with existentially quantified variables. Conversely, Theorem 2.5 packages the idea that a Σ^in_{α+2} Scott sentence corresponds to a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence after naming a finite tuple.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original question quantifies over finite parameter sets a and also allows parameters π(a). This paper's parameter-expansion viewpoint is directly relevant: one can study the expanded structures with those constants named and apply α = 1 orbit/rank/categoricity criteria there.

proof_techniques.name: α-freeness as a combinatorial substitute for definability
proof_techniques.description: The paper recalls Ash and Knight's notion of α-free tuples using the asymmetric back-and-forth preorders ≤_β. A tuple is α-free if, below level α, it can always be moved to a tuple that looks the same at any lower β-level while not preserving its α-level type. Theorem 1.1 identifies absence of α-free tuples with Σ^in_α-definability of all automorphism orbits.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For α = 1, finding 1-free tuples in finite-parameter expansions would certify failure of existential orbit-definability. Conversely, proving no such tuples exist would force strong Σ^in_1 orbit definitions and therefore strong categoricity consequences.

proof_techniques.name: Borel complexity via Lopez-Escobar
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses Lopez-Escobar's theorem to pass between syntactic Scott-sentence complexity and descriptive-set-theoretic complexity of the isomorphism class. Specifically, for an isomorphism-invariant class of presentations, being Π^0_β in the Borel hierarchy is equivalent to being axiomatizable by a Π^in_β infinitary sentence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the automorphism-computability hypothesis on a cone can be converted into a Borel complexity bound for the class of presentations or for expanded presentations, Lopez-Escobar gives a route back to Π^in_2 or Σ^in_3 Scott-sentence information in the α = 1 case.

other_useful_info: The paper defines the categoricity Scott rank cSR(A) as the least α such that every tuple orbit in A is Σ^in_α-definable without parameters. It also defines the parametrized categoricity Scott rank pSR(A) as the least α such that some finite parameter expansion (A,a) has categoricity Scott rank α; from Theorem 1.1 and Theorem 2.5, the paper notes that for each A, either cSR(A)=pSR(A) or cSR(A)=pSR(A)+1. The introduction observes that since every L_{ω_1,ω}-definable relation is a union of automorphism orbits, condition (U1) is equivalent to saying that every L_{ω_1,ω}-definable relation in A is already Σ^in_α-definable. The discussion of (U6) notes that (U6) implies A is maximal under the α+1 back-and-forth preorder, and in classes with only countably many Π^in_α-types, called Σ^in_α-small classes, this maximality is equivalent to (U6) by a cited lemma of Montalbán. The paper remarks that for natural examples, computable categoricity, relative computable categoricity, and computable categoricity on a cone often coincide because the relevant properties relativize. Useful cited sources include Goncharov [Gon75] for relative computable categoricity and existential Scott families, Ventsov [Ven92] and Downey-Hirschfeldt-Khoussainov [DHK03] for uniformity after parameters, Ash [Ash87] and Ash-Knight [AK00] for Δ^0_α-categoricity and α-freeness, Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman [AKMS89] for forcing, and Lopez-Escobar [LE65] for the Borel/infinitary-axiomatizability theorem. In the printed proof of (U6) ⇒ (U5), there appear to be minor indexing typos: the argument requires a Π^in_{α+1} sentence and a Π^in_α type not Σ^in_α-supported, matching Lemma 3.2 and the statement of (U5).
why_relevant: Contains the key theorem equating Sigma-in-alpha definability of automorphism orbits, Pi-in-(alpha+1) Scott sentences, and uniform boldface Delta-alpha categoricity. The alpha = 1 case is especially relevant for relating existential orbit-definability to computable categoricity on a cone.

title: Punctual Definability on Structures
overall_summary: The paper studies primitive-recursive, or punctual, analogues of relative computability notions for countable structures in finite languages, especially intrinsically punctual functions and punctual categoricity on a cone. Its first main characterization says that a numerical function computable by primitive recursion from every copy of a structure is exactly one that is primitively recursively bounded and uniformly captured, after finitely many parameters, by a punctual family of quantifier-free formulas satisfying simultaneous existential and universal conditions. Its second main characterization says that a structure is punctually categorical on a cone exactly when it is uniformly locally finite, existentially atomic, and highly local; the necessity is proved by constructing bad copies whenever orbit/locality obstructions exist. For the original problem, the most relevant content is methodological: on-cone computability assumptions are converted into finite-parameter existential orbit definitions by failed diagonalization, and failures of finite orbit/locality properties are exploited by building copies that defeat all candidate relative procedures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a punctually branching subset of ω^ω, meaning that P is a compact space of total functions and there is a primitive recursive function b such that every g in P satisfies g(i) ≤ b(i) for all i. For a Turing functional Ψ whose oracle is restricted to range over P, the following are equivalent: (1) there is a primitive recursive time function t such that for every g in P and every input x, Ψ^g(x) halts within at most t(x) computation steps; (2) Ψ can be expressed by a primitive-recursive scheme in which the oracle function g is allowed as an additional basic function. The paper also notes that the lemma relativizes: primitive recursive may be replaced throughout by primitive recursive relative to any fixed total function f.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1) implies (2), replace every unbounded minimization in a Turing computation by bounded minimization using the primitive recursive time bound t(x). For (2) implies (1), argue by induction on the construction of the primitive-recursive scheme using the oracle g. For the basic functions 0, successor, and projections, the time bounds are immediate. For the oracle basic function g itself, the branching bound b gives a primitive-recursive bound on how large an oracle value can be, and the proof takes a monotone primitive-recursive bound such as t(x)=∑_{i≤x} b(i). For composition, if Ψ^g(x)=Φ^g(Θ_1^g(x),...,Θ_m^g(x)) and the subfunctionals have monotone primitive-recursive time bounds t_0,t_1,...,t_m, then a primitive-recursive bound is t(x)=t_0(⟨t_1(x),...,t_m(x)⟩)+∑_i t_i(x), with tuples coded primitively recursively. For primitive recursion, if Ψ^g(x,0)=Θ^g(x) and Ψ^g(x,y+1)=Φ^g(x,y,Ψ^g(x,y)), define the time bound recursively by t(x,0)=t_0(x) and t(x,y+1)=t_1(x,y,t(x,y))+t(x,y), again made monotone if needed. The crucial use of punctually branching is that all possible oracle values that can affect the computation are bounded primitively recursively, so the induction does not require an unbounded compactness search.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countably infinite structure in a finite language. Suppose f:ω→ω has the property that for every copy B of A with domain ω, f ≤PR B, i.e. f is primitive recursive relative to the atomic diagram/function coding of B. Then f is punctually bounded from above: there is a primitive recursive function h such that f(x) ≤ h(x) for every x.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because the language is finite, the number and complexity of terms of any fixed length in the free algebra are bounded effectively. The authors choose a copy B of A on domain ω in which every operation is bounded by a primitive recursive function; equivalently, the possible oracles for computations from such presentations lie in a punctually branching space. Since f ≤PR B, f is computed from B by some primitive-recursive oracle scheme. Applying Lemma 2.2 to this punctually branching oracle space yields a primitive-recursive uniform bound on the time and on the finite oracle values that can occur in the computation of f(x). Combining this with the primitive-recursive branching bound gives a primitive-recursive upper bound h(x) for f(x).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countably infinite structure over a finite language, and let f:ω→ω. The function f is intrinsically punctual in A, meaning f ≤PR B for every copy B of A on domain ω, if and only if both of the following hold. First, f is bounded by a primitive recursive function. Second, there are a finite tuple of parameters ā from A and a punctual family of quantifier-free first-order formulas {Φ_{g(x,y)}}_{x,y∈ω}, where the map (x,y)↦ the Gödel code of Φ_{g(x,y)} is primitive recursive, such that for all x,y∈ω, if z̄ denotes exactly the free variables of Φ_{g(x,y)} other than the parameters ā, then f(x)=y iff (A,ā) satisfies ∃z̄[(∧_{i<j} z_i≠z_j) ∧ Φ_{g(x,y)}(ā,z̄)], and also f(x)=y iff (A,ā) satisfies ∀z̄[(∧_{i<j} z_i≠z_j) → Φ_{g(x,y)}(ā,z̄)]. Thus, for the correct value y=f(x), every tuple of pairwise distinct z̄ satisfies Φ_{g(x,y)}, while for an incorrect y no tuple of pairwise distinct z̄ satisfies it.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the easy direction, assume the primitive-recursive bound h and the formulas exist. Given any copy B of A, fix nonuniformly a tuple b̄ in B corresponding to ā. On input x, compute h(x). For each y≤h(x), compute the quantifier-free formula Φ_{g(x,y)} and choose any tuple z̄ of pairwise distinct elements of B of the required length. Because the existential and universal equivalences force the truth of Φ_{g(x,y)}(b̄,z̄) to be independent of the chosen distinct z̄ and to hold exactly for y=f(x), evaluating this one quantifier-free instance relative to B identifies f(x). This gives a primitive-recursive computation relative to B. For the hard direction, Proposition 2.4 gives the primitive-recursive bound. To obtain the formulas, the proof constructs a sufficiently generic copy B of A inside a punctually branching space P of presentations whose functions are primitively recursively bounded. At a finite stage one has a finite initial segment σ of B and a partial isomorphism ι from σ into A, with range ā. For the e-th primitive-recursive oracle operator P_e, the construction searches for a finite extension τ⊇σ, together with a compatible extension of ι into A, and an input x such that P_e^τ(x) converges to a value different from f(x). If such an extension exists, the construction takes it and thereby diagonalizes against P_e. Since f is intrinsically punctual, in the final B there is a least e with P_e^B=f; at the finite condition σ where the attempt to diagonalize against this e fails, the finite range of ι becomes the parameter tuple ā. For each x,y, Lemma 2.2 is applied inside the punctually branching presentation space to primitively recursively list the finite extensions τ of σ for which P_e^τ(x)=y and to compute a bound N on their lengths. The formula Φ_{g(x,y)} is the disjunction of the finite atomic diagrams of these τ, with elements already in dom(ι) replaced by the corresponding parameters ā and all other elements replaced by variables z̄, together with the distinctness condition on z̄. If f(x)=y, the actual copy B realizes one of these diagrams, giving the existential condition. Conversely, if a compatible finite extension of σ into A of length at most N made P_e output a value different from f(x), it would have supplied the forbidden diagonalization; hence every compatible realization of the relevant finite diagrams in A gives the same output. This yields both the existential and the universal equivalence. Punctuality of the family follows from the primitive-recursive enumeration of the finite computations and finite diagrams.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.6, with Remark 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A is a structure in a finite relational language and f ≤PR B for every copy B of A with domain ω, then f is primitive recursive without any oracle. The proof uses two auxiliary transformations from Remark 3.7: finite parameters can be eliminated by passing to a finite relational expansion with new relations that encode old relations with the parameters inserted, and any finite relational language can be replaced, up to punctual bi-interpretability, by a finite language of totally irreflexive relations all of one common arity K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 2.4, f has a primitive-recursive bound. By Theorem 3.2, f is represented using a finite parameter tuple ā and a punctual family of quantifier-free formulas satisfying the existential/universal equivalences. First eliminate the parameters by adding finitely many relation symbols that encode every way of substituting members of ā into each original relation; formulas with parameters are then rewritten punctually into formulas without parameters in the expanded finite relational language. Next replace each relation by finitely many totally irreflexive relations corresponding to equality patterns among variables, and then pad all relations to a common maximal arity K. On a fixed copy A with domain ω, view the resulting structure as a finite coloring of K-element subsets {x_1<...<x_K}: the color records, for each irreflexive relation P and each permutation π of {1,...,K}, whether P(x_{π(1)},...,x_{π(K)}) holds. Ramsey's theorem gives an infinite monochromatic set X⊆ω. On X, the truth of each relation depends only on the natural order pattern of the variables, so the induced substructure has an automatic, hence primitive-recursive, presentation. In that presentation the quantifier-free formulas from Theorem 3.2 are decidable primitively recursively, and because the existential and universal equivalences force each formula to be uniformly true or uniformly false on distinct tuples, evaluating one tuple for each y≤h(x) computes f(x) primitively recursively.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A countable structure A is punctually categorical on a cone if there exists a total function g such that for every copy B of A with domain ω, there is an isomorphism f:A→B with both f ≤PR g⊕B and f^{-1} ≤PR g⊕B. If the cone oracle g is primitive recursive and hence can be suppressed, this is the paper's relative punctual categoricity notion.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition rather than a theorem. It is important that the cone oracle g is fixed uniformly for the isomorphism type, while the particular primitive-recursive schemes and finitely many numerical constants used to compute an isomorphism may depend on the target copy B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A is punctually categorical on a cone, then there is a finite tuple ū from A such that for every finite tuple x̄ from A, every infinite substructure X⊆A containing ū and x̄ intersects every automorphism orbit over ūx̄. Equivalently, for every y∈A there is some z∈X such that (A,ū,x̄,y) is isomorphic to (A,ū,x̄,z) by an automorphism fixing ūx̄ pointwise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let A be punctually categorical on the cone above a total function f. Suppose the conclusion fails. Then for every current finite tuple ū there is an extension x̄⊇ū, an element y∈A, and an infinite substructure X⊆A containing x̄ but omitting the automorphism orbit of y over x̄. The proof builds a copy B of A such that no pair of primitive-recursive operators (Φ,Ψ), relative to f⊕B, gives an isomorphism A→B together with its inverse B→A. A finite stage consists of a finite partial structure F of the future B and a partial isomorphism from F into A with range ū. To attack a requirement R_{Φ,Ψ}, choose x̄,y,X as above. Form a copy C extending F that is isomorphic to the omitting substructure X, with a tuple x̄^C corresponding to x̄. Because the putative inverse Ψ is total, the values x_i′=Ψ^{f,C}(x_i^C) eventually converge in A. If (A,x̄) is not isomorphic to (A,x̄′), then the inverse has already committed to a wrong type. Otherwise choose y′ such that (A,x̄,y) is isomorphic to (A,x̄′,y′), and let z^C=Φ^{f,C}(y′), which converges because Φ is total. Take a finite part G of C large enough to contain all oracle information used in these computations. Now replace the surrounding infinite copy: extend G to a copy A^C of the full structure A so that (A^C,x̄^C) has the same type as (A,x̄). The computations remain unchanged, but z^C lies in the part originally copied from X, which omitted the orbit of y over x̄; hence (A^C,x̄^C,z^C) is not isomorphic to (A,x̄′,y′). Thus either Ψ maps x̄^C to the wrong orbit, or Φ maps y′ to the wrong orbit, so (Φ^{f,A^C},Ψ^{f,A^C}) cannot be an isomorphism-inverse pair. The construction records a finite extension F′ witnessing this formal disagreement, then also adds one more element whose image is the least element of A not yet in the range of the partial isomorphism, ensuring the union of all finite stages is a full copy of A. Meeting every pair requirement contradicts punctual categoricity on the cone.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a punctual structure in a finite relational language. The following are equivalent: (1) A is relatively punctually categorical; (2) A is punctually categorical on a cone; (3) after naming finitely many constants, A becomes automorphically trivial, i.e. there is a finite set of named elements such that every permutation of the remaining domain fixing those named elements pointwise is an automorphism of the expanded structure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The only implication proved in detail is (2) implies (3), using Proposition 4.3. Let ū be the tuple supplied by Proposition 4.3. If the relational structure were not automorphically trivial after fixing any finite tuple, then ū could be extended to a tuple x̄ such that there exist elements y,z with (A,x̄,y) not automorphic to (A,x̄,z). One may choose y so that infinitely many z fail to be automorphic to y over x̄. Because the language is relational, the substructure generated by x̄ together with all those z is just the corresponding infinite subset, and it omits the automorphism orbit of y over x̄. This contradicts Proposition 4.3. The other directions are immediate from the definitions and the triviality of the automorphism action after finitely many constants: once the finite exceptional set is matched, any bijection of the remaining elements preserves all relations, and such a bijection can be chosen primitively recursively relative to the target presentation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A is punctually categorical on a cone, then for every finite tuple ā from A, the expansion (A,ā) has only finitely many automorphism orbits on single elements. Consequently, by the usual induction on tuple length, A is ℵ_0-categorical.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume A is punctually categorical on the cone above f, but for some fixed tuple ā=(a_0,...,a_p) there are infinitely many automorphism types over ā. The proof builds a copy B of A such that no primitive-recursive operator Φ relative to f⊕B is an isomorphism from B onto A; this suffices because an isomorphism A→B with primitive-recursive inverse would give such a Φ. At stage 0, put a finite partial structure on domain {0,...,p} corresponding to ā. At a later stage one has a finite partial substructure F of B with domain an initial segment {0,...,q}, and a partial isomorphism ι:i↦u_i into A, where ū contains ā. Since there are infinitely many orbits over ā, there are infinitely many orbits over ū. To attack Φ, define a sequence of finite partial extensions F_n of F such that q+1 is included, domains remain initial segments, function applications on old elements are given values in the next stage, each F_n embeds into A extending ι, and the possible images κ(q+1), over all compatible embeddings κ, still range over infinitely many automorphism types over ū. Let n be least such that Φ^{f,F_n}(0),...,Φ^{f,F_n}(q), and Φ^{f,F_n}(q+1) have all converged to values v_0,...,v_q,w in A. Such n exists by totality of the candidate functional on the eventual copy. Choose a compatible embedding κ of F_n into A so that (A,u_0,...,u_q,κ(q+1)) is not isomorphic to (A,v_0,...,v_q,w); this is possible because κ(q+1) had infinitely many possible types while the target tuple has one fixed type. Continue with F_n and this embedding. In the final union B, the stage attacking Φ permanently ensures that the tuple (0,...,q,q+1) in B is not sent by Φ to a tuple of the same type in A, so Φ is not an isomorphism. Meeting all Φ contradicts punctual categoricity on the cone.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A is punctually categorical on a cone, then A is uniformly locally finite: there is a function h such that for every finite F⊆A, the substructure generated by F has size at most h(|F|).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Theorem 4.4, A is ℵ_0-categorical. In an ℵ_0-categorical structure, algebraic closure is uniformly locally finite: for each n there is a finite bound on the size of acl(F) for |F|=n, because there are only finitely many orbits over n-tuples. Every element of the generated substructure ⟨F⟩ is definable over F, hence algebraic over F. Therefore ⟨F⟩⊆acl(F), and the uniform algebraic-closure bound gives the required h.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.7 and Definition 4.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A is punctually categorical on a cone, then A is highly local. Concretely, there is a finite tuple ū in A such that for every finite tuple x̄ in A there is an integer m_{x̄} with the following property: for every tuple z̄=z_1,...,z_{m_{x̄}} of pairwise distinct elements outside the generated substructure ⟨ū,x̄⟩, the generated substructure ⟨ū,x̄,z̄⟩ intersects every automorphism orbit over ūx̄. Definition 4.8 names this property high locality.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let ū be the tuple from Proposition 4.3. Suppose the displayed high-locality conclusion fails. Then for this ū there is some tuple x̄ such that, for every m, one can find pairwise distinct z_1,...,z_m outside ⟨ū,x̄⟩ with ⟨ū,x̄,z_1,...,z_m⟩ omitting an automorphism orbit over ūx̄. By Theorem 4.4 there are only finitely many automorphism orbits over ūx̄, so one fixed orbit can be assumed omitted for infinitely many m. The finite generated substructures obtained in this way form an infinite finitely branching tree under embeddability, using the finiteness of possible finite types over ūx̄. König's lemma yields an infinite substructure X containing ū and x̄ that omits that fixed orbit. This contradicts Proposition 4.3, which says every such infinite substructure must meet every orbit over ūx̄.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.9 / Theorem 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A in a finite language, A is punctually categorical on a cone if and only if all three of the following hold. (1) A is uniformly locally finite: there is a function h such that |⟨F⟩|≤h(|F|) for all finite F⊆A. (2) A is existentially atomic: there is a finite tuple ū such that for every finite tuple x̄ from A, the automorphism orbit of x̄ over ū is defined in (A,ū) by an existential first-order formula Φ_{x̄}; that is, (A,ū) satisfies Φ_{x̄}(x̄′) exactly when (A,ū,x̄′) is automorphic to (A,ū,x̄). (3) A is highly local in the sense of Definition 4.8: for the same finite tuple ū, for every x̄ there is m_{x̄} such that every m_{x̄}-tuple of distinct elements outside ⟨ū,x̄⟩ generates, together with ūx̄, a finite substructure meeting all automorphism orbits over ūx̄.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For necessity, uniform local finiteness is Corollary 4.6 and high locality is Corollary 4.7. Existential atomicity follows because punctual categoricity on a cone implies ordinary computable categoricity on a cone, and the paper invokes Montalbán's result from [Mon17] that computable categoricity on a cone yields existential atomicity. For sufficiency, fix a finite tuple ū witnessing existential atomicity and high locality. Choose a total cone oracle f coding four pieces of noneffective information for a fixed copy A on domain ω: the uniform local-finiteness bound h; the atomic diagram of A; the assignment x̄↦Φ_{x̄} of existential orbit formulas; and the assignment x̄↦m_{x̄} of high-locality numbers. Given any copy B of A, build an isomorphism g:A→B by a back-and-forth construction, primitive recursive in f⊕B, after nonuniformly choosing a tuple ū′ in B corresponding to ū. Suppose a finite partial isomorphism maps x̄ to x̄′. To extend forward to a new y∈A, one needs a y′∈B satisfying the existential orbit formula Φ_{x̄y}(x̄′,y′). High locality supplies finitely many candidate orbit representatives: take m_{x̄} pairwise distinct elements z̄′ outside ⟨ū′,x̄′⟩; then ⟨ū′,x̄′,z̄′⟩ meets every orbit over ū′x̄′. Uniform local finiteness, via h, lets one generate this finite substructure primitively recursively relative to f⊕B. The remaining issue is to decide which candidate satisfies an existential formula. If Φ has form ∃t̄ Ψ(t̄,x̄,y), with Ψ quantifier-free, the proof bounds the search for witnesses t̄ recursively using high locality again: for a candidate y′, add enough fresh elements outside ⟨ū′,x̄′,y′⟩ to form a finite substructure D_0 meeting all possible orbits for t_0 over the current parameters; for each possible t_0, repeat to form D_1 meeting all orbits for t_1, and so on. The maximum of the finitely many relevant m-values is computable from the cone oracle f, and h bounds the finite generated closures. Thus a finite, f⊕B-computable set D_k contains representatives sufficient to decide the existential formula by a bounded search through quantifier-free instances. Once a suitable y′ is found, set g(y)=y′. The backward step is analogous: for a new y′∈B, take in A a finite high-locality tuple z̄ outside ⟨ū,x̄⟩, consider the finite family of formulas Φ_{x̄y} for y in ⟨ū,x̄,z̄⟩, and use the same bounded witness-search procedure in B to find which formula y′ satisfies; define g^{-1}(y′)=y. Alternating these steps gives an onto isomorphism with both g and g^{-1} primitive recursive in f⊕B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countably infinite algebraic structure in a finite language consisting only of unary function symbols and predicates. The following are equivalent: (1) A is relatively punctually categorical; (2) A is punctually categorical on a cone; (3) there are finite substructures E⊆D of A and finite substructures F_i for i∈ω such that A=D∪⋃_{i∈ω} F_i, for i≠j one has F_i∩F_j=E and F_i∩D=E, every element x∈F_i\D generates F_i, and for every i≠j, F_i is automorphic to F_j over D, meaning by an automorphism fixing D pointwise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implications needing proof are (2) implies (3) and (3) implies (1). For (2) implies (3), use the preceding machinery to know that one-generated substructures are finite and uniformly bounded. Choose an isomorphism type F of one-generated finite substructure of minimal cardinality among those occurring infinitely often. Let E={y∈F: |⟨y⟩|<|F|}. By minimality, almost all copies of F of this type intersect exactly in E. Let B be the union of these common-type one-generated substructures. If A\B were infinite, then some exceptional one-generated type would occur infinitely often outside B. The proof would then build a copy C and diagonalize against all candidate isomorphism-inverse pairs by copying only B while waiting for a candidate to map too many exceptional substructures into C, then adding one more exceptional substructure; this contradicts punctual categoricity on a cone. Hence A is the union of a finite exceptional part D and infinitely many isomorphic finite one-generated pieces F_i meeting in E. Enlarging D absorbs finitely many bad intersections, giving conditions (i) to (iii). For condition (iv), Theorem 4.4 gives only finitely many automorphism orbits over D. If two different infinite classes of F_i occurred over E, split them into two infinite subsequences. Build a copy C, wait for a candidate isomorphism to map E to its natural image, then copy only one subsequence; eventually the candidate must send pieces from different E-orbits into the same available orbit, contradicting preservation of orbit type. Enlarging D absorbs finite exceptions, so all remaining F_i are automorphic over D. For (3) implies (1), hard-code the finite exceptional substructure D, then run a direct back-and-forth matching the infinitely many finite components F_i in one copy with corresponding components in another. All searches are bounded by the common finite size of the F_i, so the isomorphism and inverse are primitive recursive relative to the target presentation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 5.1 and Theorem 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A structure A is bottom punctually categorical on a cone if there is a copy C≅A and a total function f such that for every copy B≅A, some isomorphism g:C→B satisfies g≤PR f⊕B. It is uniformly bottom punctually categorical on a cone if the same primitive-recursive operator, with oracle f⊕B, computes such a g for all B. Theorem 5.2 states that if A is a relational structure, then A is uniformly bottom punctually categorical on a cone if and only if A is automorphically trivial.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The interesting direction assumes A is relational and not automorphically trivial, and diagonalizes against a proposed uniform operator Φ on a cone above f. Choose n minimal such that for some x_1,...,x_n,y,z, the tuples (x_1,...,x_n,y) and (x_1,...,x_n,z) are not in the same automorphism orbit. Minimality implies all n-tuples are automorphic. There are two cases. In Case 1, over x̄=(x_1,...,x_n) there are two infinite automorphism-invariant sets Y_{x̄} and Z_{x̄} made of distinct orbits. Enlarge the cone oracle so that for any n-tuple x̄′ it can list and decide the corresponding sets Y_{x̄′},Z_{x̄′}. Build a copy B by copying A until Φ^B maps x̄ to some x̄′; then copy only elements from Z_{x̄′}. If Φ is to be an isomorphism, eventually some y∈Y_{x̄} must be mapped into Z_{x̄′}. Then resume copying all of A. The committed image violates orbit preservation. In Case 2, over x̄ there is one cofinite orbit Y_{x̄}, and the union Z_{x̄} of all other orbits is finite. For the proposed punctual functional, use the primitive-recursive bounded-use property, available because the language is relational and finite, to find a finite use bound N. A combinatorial claim constructs a finite H⊆A of size N such that no (n+1)-tuple from H has the form x̄′z′ with z′∈Z_{x̄′}. If m=|Z_{x̄}|, choose large disjoint sets K_1,...,K_{N-1} and recursively select elements a_i avoiding the finitely many elements exceptional over n-tuples from all potential H; the sizes are chosen so the finite exclusions cannot exhaust the next K_i. Copy H into B through the computation use. Then Φ cannot correctly map a tuple x̄z with z∈Z_{x̄}, since its image would have to be a forbidden exceptional tuple inside H. Thus no nontrivial relational structure is uniformly bottom punctually categorical on a cone. The converse is immediate because if A is automorphically trivial, after fixing the finite exceptional set any bijection of the rest is an automorphism and can be chosen uniformly.

proof_techniques.name: Failed diagonalization yields finite-parameter definitions
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.2 constructs a generic copy B while trying to diagonalize against each primitive-recursive oracle operator. When an operator cannot be diagonalized against because it really computes the intrinsically punctual function, the finite condition σ at which the strategy gets stuck becomes a tuple of parameters ā in A. The finite computations compatible with σ are then converted into quantifier-free formulas: disjunctions of finite atomic diagrams of computation-supporting extensions. The absence of a possible diagonalizing extension gives not only an existential definition of the correct value, but also a universal one, because every compatible finite realization must force the same output.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the paper's closest template for turning an on-cone computability hypothesis into finite-parameter Σ^in_1 information. In the original setting, one can try to replace primitive-recursive operators by Turing functionals relative to B⊕C and use the first finite condition at which an automorphism-computing strategy cannot be defeated to define the graph or orbit behavior of that automorphism over parameters such as ā∪π(ā).

proof_techniques.name: Omitted-orbit copy-switching diagonalization
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 4.3 shows that if an infinite substructure X containing current parameters omits an automorphism orbit, then one can defeat a candidate isomorphism-inverse pair. The construction temporarily builds the target copy inside X, waits until the candidate functionals commit to images of the relevant tuple and element, then switches back to a full copy of A extending the finite computation use. Since the committed image lies in the part copied from X, it remains outside the required orbit after the switch, giving a permanent finite isomorphism disagreement.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For automorphism-countability questions, the analogous obstruction would be an infinite computable-copy construction that lets a candidate relative procedure commit inside a substructure omitting the orbit needed for an automorphism. If such an omitted-orbit configuration exists, the same switch-back idea may build a copy with an automorphism not computable from the prescribed oracle, forcing structural orbit-intersection properties.

proof_techniques.name: Infinite-orbit diagonalization by a fresh element of many possible types
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 4.4 defeats a candidate isomorphism by adding a new domain element q+1 whose finite partial diagram remains compatible with infinitely many automorphism types over the current tuple. Once the candidate functional converges on the old tuple and on q+1, the construction chooses an embedding of q+1 into A whose type differs from the type of the candidate image. This finite type mismatch is then built permanently into the final copy.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful whenever an on-cone computability property should imply finitely many orbits over finite parameters. In the original problem, if infinitely many parameter-orbits allow choosing a fresh element after a procedure has converged, this strategy suggests how to contradict uniform computability of all automorphisms or of inverse maps.

proof_techniques.name: High locality from finite-orbit compactness and König's lemma
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 4.7 converts the global orbit-intersection property of Proposition 4.3 into a uniform finite bound m_{x̄}. If no such finite bound existed, one could find arbitrarily large finite generated substructures omitting a fixed orbit over the parameters. Since there are only finitely many orbits over those parameters, a fixed omitted orbit can be chosen, and the finite generated substructures form an infinite finitely branching tree. König's lemma then gives an infinite substructure omitting that orbit, contradicting Proposition 4.3.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides a route from qualitative no-bad-copy diagonalization conclusions to finite bounds usable in definitions. If an AUT-countability-on-a-cone hypothesis yields an analogue of Proposition 4.3, König's lemma may upgrade it to finite-parameter bounded locality needed for existential definitions.

proof_techniques.name: Bounded witness search using high locality and uniform local finiteness
proof_techniques.description: In the sufficiency proof of Theorem 4.9, existential formulas defining orbits are evaluated without unbounded search by repeatedly applying high locality. For each existential witness variable, the construction adjoins enough fresh elements so that the generated finite substructure meets every relevant orbit over the current parameters. Uniform local finiteness bounds the size of each generated substructure, and the cone oracle supplies the high-locality numbers and the formulas. Thus an apparently unbounded existential search is replaced by a bounded finite search through orbit representatives.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original problem requires showing that a computable relative automorphism is Σ^in_1-definable from finite parameters, this technique indicates what additional structural property can make existential definitions effective or canonical: finite generated closures plus enough local orbit representatives.

proof_techniques.name: Relational Ramsey homogenization
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 3.6 uses finite relationality to pass to an infinite homogeneous substructure. After eliminating parameters and converting all relations to irreflexive relations of a common arity K, the structure becomes a finite coloring of K-subsets of ω. Ramsey's theorem gives an infinite monochromatic set X, on which all relation truth depends only on the order pattern of variables. This yields an automatic or primitive-recursive induced presentation, collapsing intrinsically punctual functions to primitive-recursive functions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For relational candidate examples in the original problem, this suggests that finite relational languages may be too homogeneous under on-cone assumptions: one can often pass to a very simple subcopy and eliminate hidden computational content. It may help rule out relational counterexamples or isolate why functions are needed.

proof_techniques.name: Use of an arbitrary cone oracle to code non-effective structural data
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 4.9's sufficiency proof does not require the local-finiteness bound, orbit-defining formulas, or high-locality bounds to be computable from the structure alone. Instead, all these choices are coded into a single total function f defining the cone. Once f is available, the construction of isomorphisms is primitive recursive relative to f⊕B.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This clarifies what an on-cone result is allowed to use: finite parameters and non-effective catalogs of formulas or bounds may be absorbed into the cone oracle. In the original problem, a proof that on-cone AUT-countability implies definability may similarly choose a cone oracle coding a fixed copy, parameter catalogs, or orbit representatives.

proof_techniques.name: Uniform-use bounded computation on compact presentation spaces
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.2 and Proposition 2.4 repeatedly restrict possible presentations to a compact, primitively bounded space, so every computation from an oracle has a uniformly bounded finite use. This lets the authors turn computations into finite partial diagrams and lets diagonalization act after only finite commitments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The exact primitive-recursive bounded-use conclusion does not transfer verbatim to ordinary Turing computability, but the conceptual finite-use principle does: every convergent Turing computation uses only a finite part of the presentation. Copy constructions for the original problem can exploit this by waiting for convergence and then preserving that finite use while changing the rest of the copy.

proof_techniques.name: Orbit-splitting diagonalization for relational bottom categoricity
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.2 handles a non-automorphically-trivial relational structure by choosing a least tuple length n at which an orbit split appears. If there are two infinite invariant sides of the split, the construction copies only one side after the candidate maps the base tuple, forcing an element from the other side to be sent to the wrong side. If instead one orbit is cofinite and the exceptional part is finite, a combinatorial finite set H is chosen with no forbidden exceptional (n+1)-tuple, and the candidate's bounded use is trapped inside H.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a more specialized diagonalization against uniform one-way isomorphisms, but the split into infinite-orbit and cofinite-orbit cases may be useful when analyzing automorphism groups over finite parameters in relational structures.

other_useful_info: The paper works throughout with finite languages, and predicates are treated as {0,1}-valued operations when coding structures as total functions. It explicitly relies on Montalbán [Mon17] for the computable-structure-theoretic fact that computable categoricity on a cone gives existential atomicity. The authors note that for relative punctual categoricity without a cone, the conditions of Theorem 4.9 are necessary but they do not know whether they are sufficient. The relational consequences are strongly trivializing: punctual categoricity on a cone for punctual relational structures is equivalent to automorphic triviality after finitely many constants, and uniform bottom punctual categoricity on a cone for relational structures is equivalent to automorphic triviality. Corollary 3.8, derived from Corollary 3.6, states that finite relational structures as a class are not punctually universal, because non-primitive-recursive information can be punctually coded into simple functional structures but not intrinsically recovered from relational ones.
why_relevant: Characterizes punctual categoricity on a cone using existential atomicity, local finiteness, and finite automorphic triviality. Although the notion is different, its methods are closely related to proving that on-cone computability assumptions force finite-parameter existential definability.

title: Computability theoretic classifications for classes of structures
overall_summary: This survey organizes computability-theoretic behavior of classes of countable structures around two poles: tame classes, especially Σ-small and effectively Σ-small classes, and complete classes under several reducibilities. The core tools are existential/back-and-forth type counting, relativization to cones, relatively intrinsically computably enumerable relations and structural jumps, listability/copyability games, and effective bi-interpretability. The paper gives several cone dichotomies: non-Σ-small classes can code arbitrary reals into structures on a cone, while low-property hypotheses for Π^in_2 classes force Σ-smallness. For the original problem, the most relevant extractable tools are the Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman/Chisholm characterization of r.i.c.e. relations by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 definitions with parameters, the construction of uniform structural jumps in effectively Σ-small classes, and the effective-bi-interpretability lemma showing how automorphisms and definability-relevant invariants transfer between structures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1, Lopez-Escobar theorem and lightface version
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a countable computable relational language, and identify a presentation A of an L-structure with its atomic diagram D(A) ∈ 2^ω. Let K ⊆ 2^ω be a class of presentations closed under isomorphism. For each countable ordinal α, K is a Σ^0_α set in the Borel hierarchy if and only if K is axiomatizable by an infinitary L_{ω1,ω} sentence of syntactic class Σ^in_α. Moreover, in the effective lightface version, K is lightface Σ^0_α if and only if K is axiomatizable by a computably infinitary Σ^c_α sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey states this as a standard theorem and does not give the proof. Its role in the paper is to identify isomorphism-invariant descriptive set-theoretic complexity of classes of presentations with infinitary syntactic complexity of axiomatizations. The lightface clause is cited from Vanden Boom and is used as the effective counterpart: computably Borel definitions correspond to computably infinitary formulas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2, Becker-Kechris bounded Scott rank criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an L_{ω1,ω}-axiomatizable class of countable structures. The following are equivalent: (1) the isomorphism problem for K, namely the set of pairs ⟨D(A), D(B)⟩ such that A, B ∈ K and A ≅ B, is Borel; (2) K has bounded Scott rank, meaning that sup{SR(A) : A ∈ K} is some countable ordinal β < ω1. In this paper SR(A) is defined as the least ordinal α such that every automorphism orbit in A is Σ^in_α-definable without parameters.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey cites this result and does not prove it. It is used to connect the descriptive complexity of the isomorphism relation on a class with uniform bounds on infinitary orbit definitions inside the individual structures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.3 and Corollary 3.4, Richter extendibility criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a countable structure A, say that A has the c.e. extendibility condition if every existential type realized in A is a computably enumerable set of existential formulas. Then the following are equivalent: (1) A has the c.e. extendibility condition; (2) every set X ⊆ ω that is computably enumerable relative to every presentation of A is already computably enumerable without oracle. Consequently, if A has the c.e. extendibility condition and A has a Turing degree x in the sense that x computes a copy of A and every copy of A computes x, then x = 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1) implies (2), the paper says the argument is essentially Richter's proof: because all realized existential types are c.e., finite partial information about a copy that is needed by an oracle enumeration can be effectively recognized and extended, so any enumeration that succeeds relative to every presentation can be simulated effectively. For (2) implies (1), fix an existential type p realized by a tuple in A. In any presentation of A, choose a tuple realizing p and hardwire its finitely many natural-number entries; then the existential formulas true of that tuple are c.e. relative to the atomic diagram of the presentation. Thus p is c.e. in every presentation, so by (2) p is c.e. without oracle. For the corollary, if X is of the Turing degree of A, then X and its complement are c.e. relative to every copy of A, hence both are c.e. by the theorem, so X is computable and the degree is 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.5, non-Σ-small classes code arbitrary reals on a cone
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an L_{ω1,ω}-axiomatizable class of countable structures. The following are equivalent: (1) K is not Σ-small, meaning that the set of existential types ∃-tp^A(a) realized by finite tuples a in structures A ∈ K is uncountable; (2) relative to every oracle on a cone, for every real Y ∈ 2^ω there is a structure A ∈ K such that Y is left-c.e. in every copy of A. Here Y is left-c.e. in a presentation A if {σ ∈ 2^{<ω} : σ ≤_lex Y} is c.e. in D(A), equivalently if Y has a D(A)-computable approximation from the left.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey cites this theorem from Montalbán 2010 and does not give a full proof. The construction behind the forward direction is described elsewhere in the paper in the same style: non-Σ-smallness supplies a perfect tree of existential types; after relativizing to an oracle computing this tree, one builds, for any desired real Y, a structure whose realized existential-type branch codes Y. Every copy reveals enough positive existential information to enumerate increasingly accurate left approximations to that branch, so Y is left-c.e. in every copy. The reverse direction is the tame-side obstruction: if only countably many existential types are available, arbitrary reals cannot be uniformly forced to be left-c.e. in every copy in this way.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.10, Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm characterization of r.i.c.e. relations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let R ⊆ A^{<ω} be a relation on finite tuples from A. R is relatively intrinsically computably enumerable, abbreviated r.i.c.e., if for every copy (B, R^B) of the pair (A, R), the relation R^B, viewed as a subset of ω^{<ω}, is c.e. in the atomic diagram D(B) of the underlying copy B. Then R is r.i.c.e. if and only if R is uniformly definable by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas with parameters from A: there are a finite tuple a ∈ A^{<ω} and a computable sequence of Σ^c_1 formulas φ_i(x_1, ..., x_|a|, y_1, ..., y_i), one for each arity i ∈ ω, such that for every finite tuple b ∈ A^{<ω}, b ∈ R if and only if A ⊨ φ_|b|(a, b).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites the theorem and does not prove it in detail. The easy direction is that a computable infinitary Σ^c_1 definition gives a c.e. procedure in each copy: in a copy, hardwire the image of the finite parameter tuple a, enumerate the computable disjunction of existential formulas, and search the atomic diagram for witnesses. The converse is the AKMS/Chisholm intrinsic definability theorem: from the assumption that every copy admits an enumeration of the transported relation using only the atomic diagram, one analyzes finite forcing conditions for generic copies and collects the finite existential conditions that force membership. These forcing conditions form a computable infinitary Σ^c_1 definition; the finitely many parameters account for the non-uniformity allowed in the definition of r.i.c.e.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 3.11-3.13 and surrounding fact, complete r.i.c.e. relations and structural jumps
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a structure A, a relation R ⊆ A^{<ω} is r.i.c.e. complete in A if R is r.i.c.e. and every r.i.c.e. relation Q ⊆ A^{<ω} is relatively intrinsically computable from R. Such complete relations always exist: if φ_{i,j}(y_1, ..., y_j) is an effective listing of the Σ^c_1 formulas of arity j, define K^A ⊆ A^{<ω} × ω by (b, i) ∈ K^A if and only if A ⊨ φ_{i,|b|}(b); then K^A is r.i.c.e. complete. The jump of A is A′ = (A, K^A). If X ⊆ ω, write vec X ⊆ A^{<ω} for the tuple-length coding b ∈ vec X iff |b| ∈ X; if X is c.e., then vec X is r.i.c.e. A relation R is structurally r.i.c.e. complete if R ⊕ vec 0′ is r.i.c.e. complete, and then (A, R) is called a structural jump of A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The completeness of K^A is immediate from the syntactic characterization of r.i.c.e. relations: any r.i.c.e. Q is defined by some Σ^c_1 formula from the effective list, so membership of b in Q is exactly membership of the coded pair (b, i) in K^A for the corresponding index i, up to the standard coding of arities. The tuple-length coding vec X supplies external c.e. information uniformly inside A^{<ω}. The structural version separates purely structural r.i.c.e. content R from the unavoidable computability-theoretic oracle vec 0′.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.14, structural complete relations in effectively Σ-small classes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an effectively Σ-small class of structures. Thus there is a computable list {p_i : i ∈ ω} of computable existential types that lists, without repetition, all existential types realized in structures in K, with computable operations for erasing and permuting variables and computable decidability of inclusion between existential types. For each i ∈ ω, let φ_i be the computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formula φ_i := big disjunction of all existential formulas ψ such that ψ ∉ p_i, with the appropriate tuple arity. Then these formulas φ_i define, uniformly on every structure in K, a structurally r.i.c.e. complete relation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The formula φ_i is Σ^c_1 because p_i is computable, so its complement among existential formulas can be effectively enumerated. On a tuple b whose existential type is q, the truth of φ_i(b) records that q contains some existential formula not in p_i, equivalently that q is not included in p_i. Since the effective Σ-small presentation of K gives a computable list of all possible q and a decision procedure for type-inclusion, the collection of these non-inclusion facts, together with the standard vec 0′ component allowed in structural completeness, lets one decide enough about the realized existential type of b to compute any r.i.c.e. relation on tuples. A r.i.c.e. relation is given by a Σ^c_1 disjunction of existential formulas, and its truth on b depends only on whether the realized existential type of b intersects the corresponding c.e. set of existential formulas. Thus the relation defined by the family φ_i, plus vec 0′, is r.i.c.e. complete uniformly throughout K.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.15, no uniform structural complete relation in non-Σ-small classes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If K is not Σ-small, then there is no computable sequence of Σ^c_1 formulas that defines a structurally r.i.c.e. complete relation simultaneously on all structures in K. Simultaneously means that the same computable sequence of formulas must work, in every A ∈ K, as a structural r.i.c.e. complete relation for A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey states this as a result from Montalbán 2010 and does not give the proof.
