The stated contrast with Theorem 3.14 is the key point: in the Σ-small case, a computable list of all existential types can be used to build a uniform structural jump; in the non-Σ-small case, the perfect-set coding phenomenon for existential types prevents any fixed computable family of Σ^c_1 formulas from capturing all r.i.c.e. information uniformly across the class.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.17, low property on a cone implies Σ-smallness for Π^in_2 classes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Π^in_2 class of structures, i.e. a class axiomatizable by an infinitary Π^in_2 sentence. If K has the low property on a cone, meaning that relative to all sufficiently high Turing oracles every low presentation A ∈ K has a computable copy, then K is Σ-small.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume K is not Σ-small. The paper invokes Montalbán's construction to obtain, relative to an oracle on a cone, the following: for every X ∈ 2^ω and every Y c.e. in X, there is a structure A ∈ K computable from X such that Y is left-c.e. in every copy of A. Choose a set Y that is c.e. over a low set X but is not left-c.e. The paper gives the example using Chaitin's Ω relativized to a low 1-random real R: the relevant coded set is c.e. in R but not left-c.e., since otherwise it would have c.e. degree, contradicting the 2-randomness behavior. Since A is computable from the low oracle X, A is low. By the low property on the cone, A has a computable copy. But Y is left-c.e. in every copy of A, hence left-c.e. in a computable copy, contradicting the choice of Y. Therefore K must be Σ-small.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.19, low property, listability of the jump, and copyability
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Π^in_2 class of structures. The following are equivalent: (1) K has the low property on a cone; (2) K is Σ-small and K′ is listable on a cone; (3) K is Σ-small and K′ is ∞-copyable. Here K′ = {A′ : A ∈ K} is the class of jumps of structures in K. A class S is listable if there is a Turing functional which, for every oracle X, outputs an X-computable sequence of structures listing all X-computable structures in S, allowing repetitions. The ∞-copyable condition is defined by a game G_∞(S): player C builds an infinite list of structures in S by finite approximations, while player D builds one structure and may wait; C wins if one of C's structures is isomorphic to D's structure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey states the theorem and describes the game but does not give the full proof. The idea indicated is that listability is usually proved by a priority construction monitoring all computable functions and listing those that code structures in the class. The game G_∞(K) abstracts the combinatorics of such priority constructions: C's strategy is exactly a uniform way to copy any structure D builds into one entry of an infinite list. The theorem identifies, for Π^in_2 classes, the low property on a cone with the conjunction of Σ-smallness and such a uniform listing/copying ability for the structural jumps.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Reported characterizations in Subsection 3.6, relative and cone computable categoricity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper recalls Goncharov's characterization: relatively computably categorical structures are exactly the atomic models, over a finite set of parameters, in which all relevant types are generated by existential formulas and there is a c.e. listing of those formulas. The paper also reports Montalbán's characterization: the structures that are computably categorical on a cone are exactly those having a Σ^in_3 Scott sentence. A class K has the categoricity property if every computably categorical structure in K is relatively computably categorical; the paper lists Q-vector spaces, algebraically closed fields, Boolean algebras, linear orderings, equivalence structures, trees as posets, ordered abelian groups, and p-groups as classes having this property.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These results are cited as background, not proved in the survey. The common mechanism is the conversion between computable isomorphism construction and definability of orbit/type information: relative computable categoricity is controlled by existential generators for types over finitely many parameters, while cone computable categoricity is captured by the existence of a low-complexity Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.25, relationship between bf-ordinal and Turing ordinal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a Π^in_2 class of structures with bf-ordinal τ < ω1, where the bf-ordinal is the least α such that K is not Σ^in_α-small, with value ∞ if no such α exists. Suppose K has a Turing ordinal on a cone. Then, on a cone, the Turing ordinal of K is either τ or τ + 1. The Turing ordinal notion used here says: for all β below the ordinal, if a structure in K has βth-jump Turing degree, that degree must be 0^(β), while at the ordinal all degrees above 0^(ordinal) occur as corresponding jump degrees of structures in K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper gives a sketch. For every γ < τ, K is Σ^in_γ-small, so on a cone the greatest lower bound of each relevant degree spectrum at level γ is 0^(γ). Since K is not Σ^in_τ-small, there is a perfect tree T of existential types over the τ-jump language L_τ. Relativize to an oracle computing T. For every X ≥_T 0′, choose a 1-generic G with G ⊕ 0′ ≡_T G′ ≡_T X. Using the construction from Montalbán 2010, build A_τ ∈ K computable in G and realizing the existential type T[G]. Then X computes the jump of some copy of A_τ. Conversely, G is left-c.e. in every copy of A_τ, so the jump of every copy computes both G and 0′, hence computes X. Thus structures realizing arbitrary X occur at the τ+1 level, while the smallness below τ prevents earlier nontrivial degrees; the only possible Turing ordinal on a cone is τ or τ+1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.5, Pull Back theorem for Turing-computable embeddings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Φ be a Turing-computable embedding from a class K of structures to a class S: for every presentation A ∈ K, Φ^{D(A)} is the atomic diagram D(B) of a structure B ∈ S, and isomorphism is preserved and reflected. Then for every computably infinitary Π^c_α formula φ in the language of S, there is a computably infinitary Π^c_α formula φ* in the language of K such that for all A ∈ K, A ⊨ φ* if and only if Φ(A) ⊨ φ. Consequently, if A ≤_α Ã in the α-back-and-forth relation, then Φ(A) ≤_α Φ(Ã).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey states this theorem and does not prove it. The operative idea is an induction on the computably infinitary formula complexity. Atomic facts about the output structure Φ(A) are determined by finite computations of the Turing operator from finite pieces of D(A), so their preimages are expressed by computably infinitary formulas of the corresponding base complexity. Countable conjunctions, disjunctions, and quantifiers are pulled back recursively, preserving the Π^c_α level. Since α-back-and-forth relations are characterized by preservation of Π^c_α formulas, the formula pullback implies preservation of ≤_α.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3, invariants preserved by effective bi-interpretability
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be effectively bi-interpretable structures. This means that A is effectively interpretable in B and B is effectively interpretable in A using uniformly r.i.c.e. domains inside finite tuples, uniformly r.i. computable interpreted equality and relations, and interpretation isomorphisms f^B_A and f^A_B, with the two induced composition maps uniformly r.i. computable in the respective structures. Then: (1) A and B have the same degree spectrum. If A and B are computable, then also: (2) A is computably categorical iff B is; (3) A and B have the same computable dimension; (4) A is rigid iff B is; (5) A and B have the same Scott rank; (6) for every finite tuple a from A there is a finite tuple b from B such that the expansions (A, a) and (B, b) have the same computable dimension, and conversely; (7) for every relation R ⊆ A^{<ω} there is a relation Q ⊆ B^{<ω} with the same degree spectrum, and conversely; (8) A has the c.e. extendibility condition iff B does; (9) if A and B are infinite, their index sets are Turing equivalent; (10) their jumps A′ and B′ are effectively bi-interpretable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper sketches the proof in detail. Fix the copy of A coded inside B^{<ω}; then inside that coded A there is a coded copy B~ of B, and the bi-interpretation gives a uniformly r.i. computable isomorphism f : B~ → B. For (1), from any copy of B the effective interpretation enumerates a copy of A, so any degree computing a copy of B computes a copy of A; symmetry gives equality of degree spectra. For (2), if B_1 and B_2 are computable copies of B and A_1, A_2 are the copies of A interpreted in them, a computable isomorphism A_1 → A_2 induces a computable isomorphism between the corresponding coded copies B~_1 and B~_2; composing with the computable interpretation isomorphisms yields a computable isomorphism B_1 → B_2. Symmetry gives the converse. For (3), the same argument shows that non-computably-isomorphic copies of B induce non-computably-isomorphic interpreted copies of A, and conversely, so computable dimensions agree. For (4), a nontrivial automorphism h of B acts on B^{<ω}, induces an automorphism of the interpreted A, then an automorphism h_1 of B~; because the interpretation isomorphism f is definable/invariant, h ∘ f = f ∘ h_1, so h nontrivial forces a nontrivial induced automorphism on A. Symmetry proves equivalence of rigidity. For (5), if SR(A)=α, take a tuple b from B and a tuple c in the coded B~ with f(c)=b. The orbit of c is Σ^in_α-definable inside the interpreted A; since A is Σ^c_1-definable in B and f is Σ^c_1-definable in B, the orbit of b is Σ^in_α-definable in B. Hence SR(B)≤α, and symmetry gives equality. For (6), view a tuple a from A as a tuple b in the interpreted copy inside B; then the expanded structures are effectively bi-interpretable and part (3) applies. For (7), view R as the corresponding subset Q of tuples in the interpreted copy inside B; in each copy the two relations have the same degree, and the double interpretation gives the converse. For (8), each existential type in A is one-one reducible to a Σ^c_1-type in B and conversely, preserving the c.e. extendibility condition. For (9), an index for a candidate copy of B can be transformed into an index for the interpreted candidate copy of A; if the latter is not isomorphic to A, the former is not isomorphic to B, while if it is, 0′′ can check that the bi-interpretation produces the required isomorphism, and infinite-structure index sets compute 0′′. Symmetry gives Turing equivalence. For (10), complete r.i.c.e. relations transfer because Σ^c_1 formulas in one structure are interpreted as Σ^c_1 formulas in the other.

proof_techniques.name: Relativization to a cone
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly treats a property P by considering its relativizations P^X and then asking whether P holds for all X above some base oracle Z under Turing reducibility. This is presented as a way to ignore unnatural counterexamples: proofs of natural computability-theoretic facts usually relativize, so the robust statement is often the cone version. Many theorems in the survey, including non-Σ-small coding and the low-property implications, are explicitly formulated relative to every oracle on a cone.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypothesis is already a cone hypothesis. This paper suggests allowing the base oracle to encode auxiliary structural information, such as a type list or a perfect tree of types, and then proving a relativized structural conclusion above that base.

proof_techniques.name: Σ-smallness via existential-type bookkeeping
proof_techniques.description: A class K is Σ-small if only countably many existential types are realized by finite tuples in structures in K. In the effective version, there is a computable list of all realized existential types, without repetition, together with computable operations such as erasing variables, permuting variables, and deciding inclusion between types. This finite/recursive bookkeeping is what permits the construction of uniform structural jumps and prevents arbitrary real coding by existential information.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A possible strategy is to show that computable AUT-countability on a cone imposes a Σ-small-like condition on an associated class or expansion. If so, the effective type bookkeeping may convert relative computability of automorphisms into low-complexity infinitary definitions.

proof_techniques.name: Richter extendibility as no-hidden-information principle
proof_techniques.description: Richter's c.e. extendibility condition says that all existential types realized in a structure are c.e. Theorem 3.3 converts this syntactic condition into a representation-invariance principle: any set c.e. relative to every presentation of the structure is already c.e. This is a powerful method for eliminating apparent oracle dependence when the only information used uniformly across all copies is existential finite-extension information.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an automorphism graph or a family of possible images can be shown to be c.e. in every relevant presentation of an expanded structure, c.e. extendibility could force a parameterized Σ^c_1 definition via Theorem 3.10.

proof_techniques.name: Perfect-tree coding from non-Σ-smallness
proof_techniques.description: When a class is not Σ-small, the paper uses the existence of a perfect tree of existential types to code arbitrary reals into structures. A branch through the tree is realized as the existential behavior of a constructed structure; every copy can enumerate enough positive information to recover the branch as a left-c.e. real. This technique underlies Theorem 3.5 and the proof of Theorem 3.17 by contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A contrapositive approach to the original problem could try to show that failure of finite-parameter Σ^in_1 definability for automorphisms yields a perfect coding of many automorphisms or reals, contradicting computable AUT-countability on a cone.

proof_techniques.name: R.i.c.e. equals computably infinitary Σ^c_1 definability with parameters
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.10 is the paper's main bridge between computability over all copies and infinitary syntax. A relation on tuples is r.i.c.e. exactly when it is definable by a computable family of Σ^c_1 formulas with finitely many parameters. The direction from syntax to computability is by enumerating existential witnesses in each copy; the converse is an intrinsic definability theorem using finite forcing conditions for copies.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An automorphism is a function, hence its graph is a binary relation on the domain. If the graph of an automorphism can be shown r.i.c.e. in the structure expanded by the parameters a and π(a), then Theorem 3.10 would give a stronger computably infinitary Σ^c_1 definition than the Σ^in_1 definition appearing in the question.

proof_techniques.name: Structural jump from omitted existential types
proof_techniques.description: For an effectively Σ-small class with type list {p_i}, Theorem 3.14 constructs formulas φ_i by disjoining all existential formulas omitted by p_i. On a tuple, these formulas record non-inclusion relationships between the tuple's existential type and the listed types. With vec 0′ added, this relation is complete for all r.i.c.e. relations on every structure in the class.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If automorphism computation can be reduced to a r.i.c.e. relation or to the structural jump of a suitable expansion, the omitted-type formulas may supply uniform Σ^c_1 definitions of the data needed to recover the automorphism.

proof_techniques.name: Coding tuples, products, functions, and external sets inside A^{<ω}
proof_techniques.description: Remark 3.9 records several standard codings used throughout the paper. A subset of (A^{<ω})^2 can be coded by a subset of A^{<ω} by concatenating tuples and coding lengths; direct sums R ⊕ Q of relations are coded by parity of tuple length; an external set X ⊆ ω is coded as vec X ⊆ A^{<ω} by tuple length; and with more work, subsets of the hereditarily finite extension HF(A) can be coded by subsets of A^{<ω}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graphs of automorphisms and families of finite partial automorphisms can be treated uniformly as relations on A^{<ω}. A cone oracle C can similarly be represented as vec C when comparing the original problem with structural-jump or r.i.c.e. frameworks.

proof_techniques.name: Low-property contradiction using a low oracle with non-left-c.e. information
proof_techniques.description: The proof sketch of Theorem 3.17 uses a characteristic contradiction pattern. If non-Σ-smallness lets one build, from every low oracle X and every Y c.e. in X, a low presentation whose every copy makes Y left-c.e., then the low property would give a computable copy and hence make Y left-c.e. outright. Choosing Y c.e. in a low oracle but not left-c.e., such as information derived from relativized Chaitin Ω over a low 1-random, yields the contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This pattern may be adaptable if computable AUT-countability on a cone can be used to turn a low presentation into a computable or definable automorphism enumeration, while non-definability would code a non-left-c.e. set.

proof_techniques.name: Listability and copyability games
proof_techniques.description: The paper abstracts priority constructions for listing all computable members of a class into the game G_∞(K). Player C builds infinitely many approximating structures, trying to ensure one copies the single structure built by player D. Theorem 3.19 identifies, for Π^in_2 classes, low property on a cone with Σ-smallness plus listability/copyability of the jump class.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Computable AUT-countability on a cone resembles a uniform enumerability/countability property for automorphisms. A solver could try to formulate an analogous listing or copying game for automorphism graphs or for expansions by an automorphism.

proof_techniques.name: Back-and-forth pullback through computable embeddings
proof_techniques.description: The Pull Back theorem says that Turing-computable embeddings preserve computably infinitary Π^c_α formulas by translating each output formula to an input formula of the same complexity. Consequently they preserve α-back-and-forth relations. This lets one transfer back-and-forth complexity and definability obstructions through computable reductions between classes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate example or obstruction is easier to build in one class and then encode into another, this theorem indicates how to control the relevant infinitary definability levels during the encoding.

proof_techniques.name: Effective bi-interpretability as invariant-transfer mechanism
proof_techniques.description: Effective bi-interpretability interprets each structure inside finite tuples of the other using r.i.c.e. domains and r.i. computable relations, with r.i. computable composition maps. Lemma 5.3 shows that this strong structural equivalence preserves degree spectra, computable categoricity, computable dimension, rigidity, Scott rank, c.e. extendibility, jumps, relation spectra, and index sets. Its proof also shows explicitly how automorphisms of one structure induce automorphisms of the other through the interpretations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: One may build or analyze an example in a convenient effectively bi-interpretable presentation and transfer many computability and definability invariants back. The paper only states rigidity as an automorphism-group consequence, so preservation of the exact AUT-countability property would need to be checked, but the proof provides the relevant mechanism.

other_useful_info: The paper works with countable relational languages and says this loses no generality for its purposes; functions and constants may be relationalized when applying the general machinery. A presentation A is coded by its atomic diagram D(A), and phrases such as A computes X mean D(A) computes X. The paper's Scott rank convention is especially relevant: SR(A) is the least α such that every automorphism orbit in A is Σ^in_α-definable without parameters. Examples listed as Σ-small, often effectively Σ-small, include vector spaces over a fixed computable field, algebraically closed fields, differentially closed fields of characteristic 0, abelian p-groups, equivalence structures, trees as partial orders, finite-height trees as graphs, linear orderings, linear orderings with adjacency, Boolean algebras, Boolean algebras expanded by any Σ^in_<ω relations such as atom/not-atom style predicates, and generalized Boolean algebras. The paper observes that all known examples of classes with the low property are Σ-small and conjectures that every Σ-small Π^in_2 class satisfies the categoricity property on a cone. It also notes that classes on top for effective bi-interpretability include undirected graphs, partial orderings, and lattices; with finitely many constants added, integral domains, commutative semigroups, and 2-step nilpotent groups become on top; and cited recent work shows that fields are on top for effective bi-interpretability. Theorem 3.26, under ZFC plus ∀X(X# exists), states that for an L_{ω1,ω}-axiomatizable class with uncountably many models, being a counterexample to Vaught's conjecture is equivalent to satisfying hyperarithmetic-is-recursive relative to all oracles on a cone.
why_relevant: Surveys the standard toolbox for properties on a cone, Sigma-small classes, back-and-forth types, and effective bi-interpretability. It provides context and techniques for turning a cone hypothesis into structural definability information.

title: Computable Polish Group Actions
overall_summary: The paper develops a computable-analysis framework for effective actions of computable Polish groups on computable Polish spaces, with the standard action of S∞ on the space of countable structures as a central motivating example. It proves group-action analogues of several computable-structure-theoretic results: a cone version of uniform computable categoricity characterized by G_delta orbits via Effros's theorem, a theorem ruling out nontrivial countable unions of enumeration cones as degree spectra, and a Goncharov-style infinite computable dimension theorem under effective openness and a non-high c.e. approximation hypothesis. The main techniques are effective names for points and open sets, effectively open maps and inverse selection, Baire category arguments over the acting group, and a finite-injury construction using true stages for non-high c.e. oracles. For the original problem, the most relevant contribution is the descriptive/topological translation of computability questions about copies and isomorphisms into properties of orbit maps and group actions; the paper also supplies reusable machinery for extracting computable group elements from open orbit maps and for diagonalizing against computable group elements in an effective action.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Formal inclusion calculus, Definition 6 and properties F1-F4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a computable Polish metric space with basic open balls. For basic balls B_r(u) and B_q(v), define formal inclusion B_r(u) ≪ B_q(v) to mean r + d(u,v) < q and r < q/2. Writing A ≫ B for B ≪ A, the paper records the following properties for basic open balls A, B, B_i, C: (F1) if A ≪ B then A ⊆ B; (F2) for every point x in M and every basic open C with x ∈ C, there is a basic open B with x ∈ B and B ≪ C; (F3) if B_0 ≫ B_1 ≫ B_2 ≫ ... is an infinite chain, then ⋂_i B_i is a singleton {x}, denoted B_0 ≫ B_1 ≫ ... → x; (F4) if B_0 ≫ B_1 ≫ ... and C contains ⋂_i B_i, then C ≫ B_i for some i, i.e. B_i ≪ C.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (F1), if z ∈ B_r(u) and r + d(u,v) < q, then d(z,v) ≤ d(z,u) + d(u,v) < r + d(u,v) < q, so z ∈ B_q(v). For (F2), if x ∈ B_q(v), choose a dense-set center u sufficiently close to x and choose rational r small enough that x ∈ B_r(u), r + d(u,v) < q, and r < q/2. For (F3), formal inclusion gives ordinary nesting and also forces radii to shrink at least geometrically, since each next radius is less than half the previous target radius. The centers are therefore Cauchy; completeness gives a limit x, nesting puts x in every ball, and radii tending to 0 make the intersection a singleton. For (F4), let x be the singleton intersection. Since x ∈ C, use (F2) to find D with x ∈ D ≪ C. Fast convergence of the chain to x implies that, for all sufficiently large i, B_i is small and centered close enough to x to be formally included in C; hence C ≫ B_i for some i. The paper emphasizes that formal inclusion, unlike ordinary inclusion, is c.e. in a computable metric space, and this is why it is used throughout the effective constructions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Fact 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a computable Polish space with a fixed computable basis {B_i}. For a point x ∈ X, let N_x = {i : x ∈ B_i} be its topological name. Then x is a computable point, equivalently x has a computable fast Cauchy name, if and only if N_x is c.e. Moreover, the conversion is uniform, and N_x has the least enumeration degree among enumeration degrees of fast Cauchy names of x.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If N_x is c.e., enumerate basic neighborhoods of x and recursively choose a chain B_0 ≫ B_1 ≫ ... with x ∈ B_i for every i. This is possible by property (F2) of formal inclusion and because formal inclusion is c.e. The centers of the B_i form a computable fast Cauchy name for x. Conversely, if a c.e. fast Cauchy name is given, equivalently a c.e. chain B_0 ≫ B_1 ≫ ... → x, then a basic open B contains x exactly when B_i ≪ B for some i, by the fast-convergence property (F4). Since formal inclusion is c.e., this enumerates N_x. Uniformity of both transformations gives the least-enumeration-degree conclusion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f : X → Y be a function between computable Polish spaces. The following are equivalent. (1) f is effectively continuous: there is a c.e. family F of pairs (U,V) of basic open sets, U in X and V in Y, such that f(U) ⊆ V for every (U,V) ∈ F, and for every x ∈ X and every basic open E containing f(x), there is a basic open D containing x with (D,E) ∈ F. (2) There is an enumeration operator which, given a name of an open set W ⊆ Y, enumerates a name of f^{-1}(W) ⊆ X. (3) There is an enumeration operator which, given the point name N_x of x ∈ X, enumerates the point name N_{f(x)} of f(x) ∈ Y. (4) There is a uniformly effective procedure which, given any fast Cauchy name of x ∈ X, outputs a fast Cauchy name of f(x) ∈ Y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as a well-known lemma. The standard reconstruction is as follows. From an effective-continuity witness F, enumerate f^{-1}(W) by listing all U such that (U,V) ∈ F for some basic V listed in the name of W; the local coverage condition ensures that all points of f^{-1}(W) are covered. This gives (1) ⇒ (2). Applying the preimage operator to basic neighborhoods of f(x) gives N_{f(x)} from N_x, yielding (2) ⇒ (3). Given N_{f(x)}, Fact 9 uniformly builds a fast Cauchy name for f(x), so (3) implies (4) after converting the input fast Cauchy name of x to N_x. Conversely, a uniform fast-Cauchy-name transformer gives the usual effective local modulus: search for enough finite input information forcing the output fast name into a target basic open E, producing a basic neighborhood D of the input point with f(D) ⊆ E. This recovers an effective-continuity witness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 12 and Lemma 13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A function f : X → Y between computable Polish spaces is effectively open if there is a c.e. family F of pairs (U,V) of basic open sets, U in X and V in Y, such that (O1) f(U) ⊇ V for every (U,V) ∈ F, and (O2) for every x ∈ X and every basic open E containing x, there is a basic open D containing f(x) with (E,D) ∈ F. Lemma 13 says that f is effectively open if and only if there is an enumeration operator which, given a name of an open set A ⊆ X, enumerates a name of the open set f(A) ⊆ Y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume f is effectively open with witness F. If A = ⋃_{i∈W} B_i is an open subset of X, then f(A) = ⋃_{i∈W} f(B_i), and by (O1)-(O2) each f(B_i) is named by all basic D with (B_i,D) ∈ F. Thus, from an enumeration of W, enumerate all such D; this is a name of f(A). Conversely, suppose an enumeration operator sends names of open sets A to names of f(A). Apply it uniformly to each basic open B_i of X, obtaining a uniformly c.e. name V_i for f(B_i). Let F consist of all pairs (B_i,D) with D appearing in V_i. Then f(B_i) contains every such D, giving (O1), and because V_i names all of f(B_i), every f(x) with x ∈ B_i is contained in some such D, giving (O2).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f : X → Y be a continuous effectively open map between computable Polish spaces. Then there is an enumeration operator which, given the point name N_y of any y ∈ f(X), enumerates the point name N_x of some x ∈ f^{-1}(y).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: From N_y, use Fact 9 to obtain a fast chain U_0 ≫ U_1 ≫ ... → y in Y. Recursively construct a fast chain B_0 ≫ B_1 ≫ ... in X such that for each i there is some j with f(B_i) ⊇ U_j. Given B_{i-1}, the existence of a suitable B_i follows from effective openness and the fact that y is in the image: choose a preimage point x' of y inside B_{i-1} if needed conceptually, and use (O2) to find a neighborhood of f(x')=y contained in the image of a smaller basic neighborhood inside B_{i-1}; then shrink formally. Because effective openness and formal inclusion are c.e., one can search until such a B_i and U_j are found. The chain B_0 ≫ B_1 ≫ ... converges to some x. Continuity implies that ⋂_i f(B_i) contains only f(x), while the construction ensures arbitrarily small U_j around y are contained in f(B_i); hence f(x)=y. Finally, Fact 9 converts the fast chain for x into N_x.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a computable Polish group, and let A ⊆ ω. Let N_h denote the topological name of h ∈ G, and let A ≤_e N_h mean enumeration reducibility. If for nonmeager many h ∈ G one has A ≤_e N_h, then A is c.e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since there are only countably many enumeration operators and the relevant sets of h are Borel, there is an enumeration operator Ψ and a nonempty basic open set U such that, for comeager many h ∈ U, A = Ψ(N_h). The paper then shows that A is exactly the following c.e. set: n is listed if there are finitely many basic open balls B_{k_1},...,B_{k_m} with nonempty intersection B_{k_1} ∩ ... ∩ B_{k_m} ∩ U and n ∈ Ψ({B_{k_1},...,B_{k_m}}). If n ∈ A, choose a comeager-good h ∈ U with A=Ψ(N_h); the computation enumerating n uses finitely many basic neighborhoods of h, giving the finite witness. Conversely, if such a finite witness exists, the nonempty open set B_{k_1} ∩ ... ∩ B_{k_m} ∩ U meets the comeager set of h satisfying A=Ψ(N_h); extending the finite set of basic balls to N_h for such an h gives n ∈ A. The right-hand side is c.e. because finite nonempty intersections of basic open balls are semidecidable in the computable Polish presentation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 18, Effros
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let a Polish group G act continuously on a Polish space X, and fix x ∈ X. Let f_x : G → Orb_G(x) be the orbit map f_x(g)=g·x, with the orbit carrying the subspace topology. Effros's theorem, as quoted in the paper, says that f_x is an open map if and only if Orb_G(x) is a G_delta subset of X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove Effros's theorem; it invokes it as a classical black box. Its role in the paper is precisely to convert the descriptive-set-theoretic hypothesis that an orbit is G_delta into the topological fact that the orbit map is open, which can then be relativized to effective openness and combined with Lemma 14 to compute a group element sending x to a given orbit point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 17
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,G,a) be a computable transformation group, meaning G is a computable Polish group, X is a computable Polish space, and the action a(g,x)=g·x is computable. For x ∈ X, the following are equivalent in the paper's cone sense: (1) x is uniformly computably categorical on a cone, i.e. after relativizing to every oracle in some upper Turing cone, there is a uniform enumeration operator which, given a name N_y of any y ∈ Orb_G(x), outputs a name of some g ∈ G with g·x=y; (2) Orb_G(x) is Π^0_2, equivalently G_delta, in X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1) ⇒ (2), let Φ be the relativized uniform operator on a cone. For a point y, membership y ∈ Orb_G(x) is expressed by the assertion that Φ(N_y) is the name of an element g ∈ G and that g·x=y. The first assertion is Π^0_2: the enumerated basic opens must form a filter and must contain arbitrarily small basic balls. For the second assertion, using the effective presentation of the action, g·x=y is written as: for every basic open Y containing y, there exist a basic group neighborhood B listed by Φ(N_y) and a basic open B' containing x such that B·B' ⊆ Y. This is again Π^0_2 in the relevant names. Thus the orbit has a Π^0_2 definition relative to the cone base, hence is topologically G_delta. For (2) ⇒ (1), use Effros's theorem: since the orbit is G_delta, the orbit map f_x:g↦g·x is open onto its image. Relative to an oracle coding the needed open-map information, f_x is effectively open; it is continuous because the action is computable. Lemma 14 then gives an enumeration operator which, from N_y for y in the range, enumerates a name of some preimage g, i.e. some g with g·x=y. This relativized operator works uniformly on a cone.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a computable Polish group acting effectively on a computable Polish space X. For x ∈ X, define the G-enumeration-degree spectrum DgSpe_G(x) to be the set of all subsets Z ⊆ ω such that every enumeration of Z computes a presentation, equivalently a name, of some point y ∈ X with y in the G-orbit of x. Then DgSpe_G(x) cannot be a nontrivial union of at most countably many, with at least two, pairwise incomparable enumeration cones. In particular, there are no pairwise enumeration-incomparable sets A_i such that DgSpe_G(x)=⋃_i {Z⊆ω : A_i ≤_e Z} unless this union collapses to a single cone.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper proves the two-cone case and notes that the same proof works for countably many cones. Suppose, toward contradiction, that DgSpe_G(x) = {Z : A ≤_e Z} ∪ {Z : B ≤_e Z}, with A and B enumeration-incomparable. Define C_A = {g ∈ G : A ≤_e N_{g·x}} and C_B analogously. Each N_{g·x} itself presents a point in the orbit, so by the assumed spectrum equality, C_A ∪ C_B = G. These sets are Borel, so by Baire category one of them is nonmeager; assume C_A is nonmeager. If Z is any oracle relative to which N_x is c.e., then, uniformly in g, the effective action makes N_{g·x} enumerable from N_g ⊕ Z. Hence for nonmeager many g, A is enumeration-reducible to N_g relative to Z. Applying Lemma 16 relativized to Z gives that A is Z-c.e.; in particular A ≤_e N_x. The same reasoning applies with any orbit point y in place of x, since translating the acting group carries the nonmeager set C_A for x to the corresponding nonmeager set for y. Therefore any oracle or set that enumerates a presentation of any orbit point computes A. Thus every member of the spectrum lies above A, so DgSpe_G(x) ⊆ {Z : A ≤_e Z}. This makes the alleged union equal to the single cone above A, contradicting nontriviality and incomparability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ⊆ ω be c.e. with a fixed computable enumeration, and let X_s be the usual finite approximation at stage s. Say that a stage t is true if X_t ⊆ X, and that s looks true at t, for s<t, if X_s ⊆ X_t. Let G be a computable Polish group, and fix a computable fast Cauchy name E_0 ≫ E_1 ≫ E_2 ≫ ... → e_G for the identity. For g ∈ G, g is computable from X if and only if there are computable sequences of basic open balls {F_s} and {tilde F_s} in G and an infinite sequence of stages t_0<t_1<t_2<... such that: (1) F_{t_0} ≫ F_{t_1} ≫ F_{t_2} ≫ ... → g and tilde F_{t_0} ≫ tilde F_{t_1} ≫ tilde F_{t_2} ≫ ... → g^{-1}; (2) for every n, tilde F_{t_n}·F_{t_n} ⊆ E_n and F_{t_n}·tilde F_{t_n} ⊆ E_n; (3) for all s<t, if s looks true at t, then F_t ⊆ F_s and tilde F_t ⊆ tilde F_s; (4) every t_n is a true stage.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the right-to-left direction, the sequence of true stages is computable from X. Using X, choose a subsequence of the true t_n along which the formal inclusions give a fast Cauchy name; this computes g. For the left-to-right direction, take X-computable fast Cauchy names H_0 ≫ H_1 ≫ ... → g and tilde H_0 ≫ tilde H_1 ≫ ... → g^{-1}. Thin them if necessary so that tilde H_n·H_n ⊆ E_n and H_n·tilde H_n ⊆ E_n. Let Φ^X and Ψ^X compute indices for H_n and tilde H_n. At finite stage s, using the finite approximation X_s as oracle, let i_s≤s be greatest such that for all i≤i_s, the computations Φ^{X_s}(i), Ψ^{X_s}(i) converge to basic balls J_i, tilde J_i, the product inclusions with E_i have been witnessed, and J_0≫...≫J_{i_s} and tilde J_0≫...≫tilde J_{i_s}. Set F_s=J_{i_s} and tilde F_s=tilde J_{i_s}. If X_s⊆X_t, then computations available at s remain available at t, so F_t⊆F_s and tilde F_t⊆tilde F_s. Along true stages, choose an increasing subsequence t_n for which i_{t_n} strictly increases; then the corresponding F_{t_n} and tilde F_{t_n} are subsequences of the original X-computable fast names and satisfy all requirements.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 20
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a computable Polish group, let E_0≫E_1≫...→e_G be a fixed computable fast Cauchy name of the identity, and let g ∈ G be computable from a non-high c.e. set X. Then there are computable sequences of basic open balls {G_s} and {tilde G_s} and an infinite sequence t_0<t_1<t_2<... such that: (1) G_{t_0}≫G_{t_1}≫G_{t_2}≫...→g and tilde G_{t_0}≫tilde G_{t_1}≫tilde G_{t_2}≫...→g^{-1}; (2) for every n, not merely for n in the selected subsequence, tilde G_n·G_n ⊆ E_n and G_n·tilde G_n ⊆ E_n; (3) for all s<t, if s looks true at t for the enumeration of X, then G_t⊆G_s and tilde G_t⊆tilde G_s; (4) every t_n is a true stage. The explicit improvement over Lemma 19 is the all-n product control in clause (2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start with sequences F_s and tilde F_s from Lemma 19. The true-stage subsequence t_0<t_1<... from Lemma 19 is computable in X. Let g_X(n) be an X-computable function which is at least t_n and also bounds the finite witnesses to the inclusions tilde F_{t_n}·F_{t_n}⊆E_n and F_{t_n}·tilde F_{t_n}⊆E_n. Since X is non-high, Martin's theorem gives a computable function f not dominated by g_X. For each n, let s_n be the first stage s which looks true at f(n) and for which the inclusions tilde F_s·F_s⊆E_n and F_s·tilde F_s⊆E_n have been witnessed. Such an s_n exists. If g_X(n)≤f(n), then s_n is actually a true stage; this happens for infinitely many n because f is not dominated by g_X. Define G_n=F_{s_n} and tilde G_n=tilde F_{s_n}. The product inclusions in clause (2) hold for every n by the definition of s_n. For infinitely many n the selected s_n are true, and on those n the corresponding balls form decreasing subsequences converging to g and g^{-1}, giving clause (1). The looks-true monotonicity is inherited from the monotonicity clause of Lemma 19.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a computable Polish group acting effectively on a computable Polish space X, and assume the action is also effectively open in the sense needed to enumerate images of open sets effectively. Let x,y ∈ X be computable points which are not computably G-equivalent, meaning there is no computable h ∈ G with h·x=y. Suppose nevertheless that for some g ∈ G computable from a c.e. non-high set, y=g·x. Then x has infinite computable G-dimension: its orbit contains infinitely many computable points z_i such that no two distinct z_i,z_j are related by a computable group element.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First apply Lemma 20 to g and g^{-1}, obtaining computable approximating balls G_s around g and tilde G_s around g^{-1}, with strong product control G_s·tilde G_s⊆E_s and tilde G_s·G_s⊆E_s. The construction builds computable orbit points z_i and, for each i, group elements a_i,b_i with z_i=a_i·x=b_i·y. It diagonalizes against every potential computable group element h_e, where e is treated as an index for a fast Cauchy name H_{e,0}≫H_{e,1}≫... of an element h_e when such a name is total. For each i and each active priority level k at stage s, the construction maintains a tuple q_k^i[s] of one of two forms: (Z_k^i,A_k^i,-,t_k^i), called copying x, or (Z_k^i,-,B_k^i,t_k^i), called copying y. Here Z_k^i is a basic open neighborhood of the intended z_i; A_k^i is an open set of possible witnesses a with a·x near z_i; B_k^i is an open set of possible witnesses b with b·y near z_i; and t_k^i is a stage currently believed to be true in the approximation to g. The missing side is defined from the present side using the appropriate approximate multiplier G_t or tilde G_t according to the invariant z=a·x=b·y. The construction preserves nesting Z_0^i≫Z_1^i≫..., A_0^i⊇A_1^i⊇..., B_0^i⊇B_1^i⊇..., and t_0^i≤t_1^i≤..., and it always keeps the c.e.-checkable conditions (Ca) A·x∩Z≠∅ and (Cb) B·y∩Z≠∅, witnessed by basic subneighborhoods. The current approximation Z_i[s] to z_i is the least active Z_k^i, and the construction ensures Z_i[0]⊇Z_i[1]⊇... with fast formal shrinking, so z_i=⋂_s Z_i[s] is computable. The main diagonalization requirement CD_{i,j,e} consists of convergence subrequirements for i and j together with D_{i,j,e}, which aims to force h_e·z_i≠z_j. It is arranged so that, when CD_{i,j,e} acts, z_i is copying x and z_j is copying y. Its length of agreement at stage s is the largest n such that Φ_e has so far produced a plausible basic ball H_{e,n} and the construction has witnessed H_{e,n}·Z_i[s]∩Z_j[s]≠∅. Expansionary stages are those at which this length reaches a new maximum. When CD_{i,j,e} acts, it reinitializes lower-priority strategies and shrinks the active x-side approximation for z_i and y-side approximation for z_j while preserving (Ca) and (Cb); the missing sides are recomputed using the current approximations to g or g^{-1}. If CD_{i,j,e} acted infinitely often after its last initialization, then the convergence parts would make A_i converge to a computable a_i and B_j converge to a computable b_j, while the unbounded length of agreement would imply h_e·z_i=z_j. Then y=(b_j)^{-1}·h_e·a_i·x, giving a computable element sending x to y, contradicting the hypothesis. Thus each such requirement eventually stops acting and achieves the desired diagonalization. Switch requirements S_i^x and S_i^y are inserted before diagonalization requirements to ensure the required copying mode. For example, a switch from copying y to copying x starts with data (Z,-,B,t). It chooses a basic point or small neighborhood b' in B with b'·y∈Z and chooses t'≥t which currently looks true and is large enough that b'·E_{t'}⊆B. Using the convention relating A and B through the approximations G_{t'} and tilde G_{t'}, it defines the new side by multiplying b' with the appropriate approximating neighborhood; the displayed calculation in the paper uses the key product inclusion tilde G_{t'}·G_{t'}⊆E_{t'}, and the symmetric switch uses the other inclusion. This keeps the newly defined B-side inside the old B while preserving both (Ca) and (Cb), unless t' was not actually true. If later t' is discovered false, the switch acts again. A crucial bookkeeping device prevents it from skipping the next true stage: whenever a lower-priority strategy wants to shrink Z, the switch silently finds a candidate new b and candidate new t for the smaller Z and checks that its current t' still looks true up to that candidate t before allowing the shrink. The paper proves that after the switch's last initialization, if it chooses a false t', then when the falsity is detected the next choice is at most the next genuine true stage above t'; hence after finitely many such corrections it chooses a true t' and never requires attention again. With the switch requirements finite-injury and the CD requirements finite-injury by the contradiction argument above, every priority requirement stabilizes. The stabilized nested Z_i chains define computable points z_i in the orbit of x, and the stabilized A_i and B_i chains witness z_i=a_i·x=b_i·y. Finally, for every i≠j and every computable h_e, the requirement CD_{i,j,e} ensures h_e·z_i≠z_j. Therefore the z_i represent infinitely many computable G-equivalence classes inside Orb_G(x).

proof_techniques.name: Names and fast formal-inclusion chains
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly replaces metric convergence by nested chains of basic open balls B_0≫B_1≫... using formal inclusion. This has two advantages: the chain automatically converges to a unique point in a complete space, and the relation ≪ is c.e., so constructions can effectively search for refinements and verify containment-like requirements. Fact 9 then allows free conversion between c.e. topological names N_x and computable fast Cauchy names.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the S∞ action on presentations of a countable structure, this suggests organizing constructions of copies, isomorphisms, or automorphisms through effective basic neighborhoods rather than element-by-element definitions. It may help formalize cone arguments about when a group element or automorphism is computable from a copy.

proof_techniques.name: Effective openness gives computable preimage selection
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 14 is a central computable-analysis tool: if f:X→Y is continuous and effectively open, then from a name of a point y in the range one can enumerate a name of some preimage x with f(x)=y. The proof builds a fast Cauchy chain in the domain whose images cover smaller and smaller neighborhoods of y, using the c.e. data witnessing effective openness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When the map in question is an orbit map g↦g·A, this produces a computable isomorphism from a computable copy once openness is known. For automorphism questions, analogous preimage-selection over stabilizers or fibers could be a way to obtain computable group elements uniformly from presentations.

proof_techniques.name: Effros theorem as a bridge from orbit complexity to computability on a cone
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 17 combines Effros's theorem with effective openness. A G_delta orbit makes the orbit map open; after coding the relevant open-map information into an oracle, Lemma 14 turns openness into a uniform procedure that computes a group element carrying x to any named orbit point. Conversely, a uniform procedure for such group elements gives a Π^0_2 definition of the orbit by saying that the computed name is a group element and acts correctly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is about a cone property for automorphisms of copies. Although Theorem 17 addresses existence of isomorphisms rather than computability of all automorphisms, it supplies a model for converting topological/descriptive orbit information into cone-uniform computability statements for the S∞ action on structures.

proof_techniques.name: Baire category extraction of c.e. information from many group elements
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 16 shows that if a fixed set A is enumeration-reducible to the names of nonmeager many elements of a computable Polish group, then A is actually c.e. The argument chooses one enumeration operator on a comeager set inside a basic open set and reconstructs A from finite pieces of names whose basic-open intersection is nonempty.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful whenever one wants to show that a noncomputable object cannot be uniformly hidden in many automorphisms or isomorphisms. In a stabilizer/automorphism group, a nonmeager family of automorphisms computing the same information would force that information to be computably enumerable.

proof_techniques.name: Cone-collapse argument for enumeration degree spectra
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 3 uses Baire category on the acting group to show that if an orbit spectrum were a countable union of incomparable cones, one cone must occur on a nonmeager set of translates. Lemma 16 then forces that cone base to be computable from every presentation in the orbit, collapsing the whole spectrum to a single cone.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proposed example for the original question involves calibrating the degree spectrum of copies or automorphisms by a union of cones, this theorem gives a structural obstruction. It also suggests testing candidate constructions by looking for nonmeager subsets of S∞ or of automorphism groups that impose the same enumeration-degree information.

proof_techniques.name: True-stage approximation for non-high c.e. oracles
proof_techniques.description: Lemmas 19 and 20 convert a group element computable from a c.e. oracle into computable sequences of basic neighborhoods which behave monotonically along true stages. The non-highness assumption, via Martin's theorem, gives a computable function not dominated by an X-computable bound, allowing the construction to arrange product inclusions G_n·tilde G_n⊆E_n and tilde G_n·G_n⊆E_n for every n, not just along a sparse true subsequence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a way to use a Δ^0_2 or c.e.-relative automorphism/isomorphism approximation while controlling the damage caused by false stages. It may be useful in constructing or analyzing structures where automorphisms are computable on a cone but one needs to diagonalize against definability or against uniformly computable candidates.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-injury diagonalization inside a Polish group action
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 4 generalizes a computable-structure finite-injury construction to arbitrary effective Polish actions. It builds computable orbit points z_i while satisfying requirements CD_{i,j,e} that diagonalize against the e-th computable group element mapping z_i to z_j. If a diagonalization requirement acts infinitely often, its convergence components produce computable witnesses that would contradict the assumed non-computable equivalence of x and y.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides a template for building copies or automorphisms with prescribed failures against countably many computable or definable candidates. The strategy is particularly relevant if the original problem is attacked by constructing a structure whose automorphism group has controlled computability but resists all finite-parameter infinitary definitions.

proof_techniques.name: Switching between two witnesses while preserving orbit membership
proof_techniques.description: In Theorem 4, each constructed point z_i is simultaneously represented as a_i·x and b_i·y. Requirements sometimes need to refine the x-side witness and sometimes the y-side witness, so switch strategies S_i^x and S_i^y change which side is actively copied. The switch uses approximations to g and g^{-1}, together with small identity neighborhoods E_t, to make the newly defined witness set stay inside the old one while preserving both intersection conditions A·x∩Z≠∅ and B·y∩Z≠∅. Silent bookkeeping ensures that false true-stage guesses are corrected without skipping the next true stage.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A similar two-witness method could help when an automorphism or isomorphism must be preserved while different requirements demand incompatible local actions. It offers a way to switch between equivalent descriptions of the same copy or orbit point without losing computability of the final object.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly notes that for a countable language L, the space X_L of L-structures with domain ω is Polish and the usual S∞ action by permuting the domain is effective in the sense needed; presentations of structures, degrees of presentations, isomorphisms, and degrees of isomorphisms fit into the computable Polish action framework. The introduction explains that Montalbán's earlier theorem for structures says, in this language, that uniform computable categoricity on a cone is equivalent to the orbit being Π^0_2; the paper derives this from Effros's theorem rather than from Goncharov's characterization, Ventsov's analysis, Lopez-Escobar's theorem, and type-omitting arguments. The cited Lopez-Escobar connection between Borel complexity and L_{ω1,ω} formulas may be relevant to questions involving infinitary definability. For Theorem 4, the authors stress that group actions are always open topologically as maps G×X→X, but their proof assumes effective openness; they leave open whether the extra effectiveness and non-high c.e. hypotheses can be removed or improved. The paper also remarks that the standard S∞ action on structures has special behavior for Δ^0_2 permutations, whereas the non-high true-stage method gives a different reason for the finite-injury construction to work in general Polish actions.
why_relevant: Generalizes Montalban-style cone/categoricity theorems to computable Polish transformation groups. Since copies of a countable structure and their automorphisms are naturally handled via Polish group actions, this gives a descriptive framework for the problem.

title: Scott Complexity of Countable Structures
why_relevant: Classifies possible Scott complexities and constructs examples with delicate behavior after naming finitely many constants. These constructions and invariants are useful for building structures where finite parameters do not lower automorphism-orbit complexity to Sigma-in-1.
